[an owl puppet with a string which raises arms and legs of owl when you pull it] When using a dictionary to find out how to say something in another language one has to be careful to choose the right word. A word like "right" has two basic meanings in English: 1) "correct", and 2) the opposite of "left". Other languages have different words for these different meanings, but they have homonyms of their own. A word like "put" has many meanings. A good dictionary will have a large list of these meanings to help people find the word they want. In many languages, for example, the word "put" will be different according to whether something is being put onto something (e.g. a table) or into something (e.g. a cupboard). You need to bring enough food and drinks for everyone, so you decide to make a list of things to bring. This list will help you predict how many items you need to pack for the picnic. Now, suppose you have eight different types of food and drink options - sandwiches, chips, juice boxes, fruit salad, cookies, water bottles, soda cans, and candy bars. That makes a total of eight items to choose from. To keep track of everything, you create two lists: List A and List B. List A uses something called "BIC," which stands for "Best-Subset Regression based on Bayesian Information Criterion." With this method, you find the combination of items that gives you the most accurate prediction while keeping the number of items on the list as small as possible. After testing different combinations, you discover that bringing both sandwiches and juice boxes will give you the best prediction for how much food and drink you need. So, those go on List A. Meanwhile, your friend creates their own list using "Forward Selection based on AIC," or "Stepwise Selection based on Akaike Information Criterion." This method adds items to the list one by one, always choosing the item that improves the prediction the most. Your friend finds that starting with just water bottles helps them make a pretty good prediction. Then, when adding more items, hours\_of\_sleep\_before\_exam becomes the second choice because it further enhances the prediction accuracy. So, those end up on List B. So, even though you started with the same set of choices, you and your friend ended up with slightly different lists because of the differences between BIC and AIC methods. Both methods aim to provide the best predictions, but BIC tends to prefer fewer items on its list, while AIC may add extra items if they improve the overall prediction accuracy. Get started with maths: age 3-5 At this age you just want to make sure that your child gets off to the best possible start. These top tips outline some practical information and ideas for you. It's all about exploring number together through enjoyable activities! Tip 1: Listen to and sing songs and rhymes Sing songs that have numbers in them, such as '10 Green Bottles' and '1, 2, Buckle My Shoe.' Singing songs is a good way for children become comfortable with numbers. Don't worry if they choose the same songs again and again! Want a number rhyme to try? Tip 2: Play games Play games that involve number, such as bingo, dice and card games. Board games such as 'Snakes and Ladders' can help with counting forwards and backwards. Dominoes are another favourite. Want to play some games? Tip 3: Talk about the day Maths is all around you! You can spot things at home and out and about to talk about. - At home Ask children how many items in the living room are square or triangular. Ask: 'How many sides are there? How many sides are the same length? Ask young children to help you sort the cutlery or the laundry. Count whenever you can - remember practise makes perfect! Count how many stairs there are, or how many pairs of shoes you have. Don't worry if children remember the answer - they can count to check! Try following a recipe together: 'We need 2 scoops of flour. We need 1 cherry for each cake.' Want a recipe to try? Talk about the numbers you see when you're out and about. Look at house numbers and numbers in signs. Look for shapes in the world around you. Make a leaf collection and sort them by shape, size or colour. Count how many there are in each group. Tip 4: Read together Cuddle up and read books together. Take time to talk about what children can see on each page. Count objects and compare the amount of objects from one page to another. Look at the page numbers and say them together. Any book can be used to help children with numbers and counting! Find the maths on every page with these e-books! Below are just a few tips and ideas on how to help your child develop their maths skills further. Have fun practising together by writing numbers in sand with a stick, on the pavement with chalk or on sheets of paper with finger paints. Write numbers for your child to copy. Hold your hand over theirs as they write the number so they can feel how to write it. Try holding their finger and forming the number in the air. Begin to encourage your child to write numbers on their own. Want a fun activity to practise writing numbers? Talk about the numbers you see around you. Use magnetic numbers on the fridge or playing cards. Tap your finger a certain amount of times and ask your child tell you the number or point to the number on the fridge or find the card. Practise chanting numbers. Encourage your child to join in with you. As their confidence grows, start from different numbers: 5, 6, 7 , etc. When you go out, ask questions such as 'Can you count how many _____there are? (e.g. windows in your house, red cars on the street, cups on the table, green traffic lights on your trip to the shops). Looking for patterns Look for repeating patterns on curtains, wallpaper, or clothing. Ask children: 'Can you see a pattern? Tell me about it. What will come next?' Start patterns with blocks, beads, playing cards or toys and get children to build on the pattern to make it longer. Look for patterns in time together (e.g. seasons, months or daily routines) and talk about what you notice: 'We always go the supermarket on a Monday. We go to swimming on a Tuesday.' Listen for patterns in songs and clap or dance the rhythm. Want more fun ideas to try? Play 'Shape Tickle'. Draw shapes on your child's back and ask they can guess what shape it is by feel. Ask: 'How many sides has it got? How many sides do you think are the same length?' Cut out a picture from a magazine and cut it into pieces to make jigsaws. Use building blocks or construction kits to make shapes. Want to read an e-book about shapes? Practising position words Practise position words by having a treasure hunt! Follow clues like over the bench, under the tree, next to the bush. Draw a map to show the route you took. Want to read an e-book about position words? Drawing and measuring Help children to practise using a ruler for drawing straight lines and measuring. Make a picture using straight lines. Help your child to hold the ruler carefully as they draw. Play 'How Long?' or 'How wide?'. Work together to measure objects in the house. Point out the starting and finishing number on the ruler and read the measurement together. Help children line the object up with the 0 on the ruler when they measure. An electrochemical cell which causes external electric current flow can be created using any two different metals since metals differ in their tendency to lose electrons. Zinc more readily loses electrons than copper, so placing zinc and copper metal in solutions of their salts can cause electrons to flow through an external wire which leads from the zinc to the copper. As a zinc atom provides the electrons, it becomes a positive ion and goes into aqueous solution, decreasing the mass of the zinc electrode. On the copper side, the two electrons received allow it to convert a copper ion from solution into an uncharged copper atom which deposits on the copper electrode, increasing its mass. The two reactions are typically written Who do you want to give cookies to? That was in the refrigerator and I just took it out. And I have to take a shower yet. The most common ones are antidepressants (medicines to treat depression) and ADHD medication (medication to treat ADHD, a condition affecting the brain, including memory and focusing on surroundings.) If a person has taken anti-vomiting medication (medication to stop them being sick) for some time and suddenly stops, he or she may get schizophrenia symptoms. Hundreds of medications have schizophrenia symptoms as rare side effects (unintended effects of taking them). Title: How to Dress for the Theatre: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Attending a theatre performance is an exciting experience that allows you to immerse yourself in captivating stories, engaging performances, and stunning visuals. While enjoying the show itself should be your primary focus, dressing appropriately can significantly enhance your overall theatre-going experience by making you feel more comfortable and confident. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and advice on what to wear when attending a theatre event. Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With the Venue's Dress Code Before deciding on an outfit, research the specific dress code requirements or recommendations for the theatre venue you will be visiting. Some venues may have strict policies regarding attire, while others might encourage a more casual approach. When in doubt, opting for smart casual clothing ensures you are adequately dressed without overdressing. Step 2: Consider the Type of Performance The nature of the production you plan to attend plays a significant role in determining your wardrobe choices. For example, if you are going to see a classic Shakespearean play, formal business attire could be appropriate; however, for contemporary musicals or comedies, semi-formal or even smart casual outfits would suffice. Research the particular show and its context to help inform your decision. Step 3: Choose Appropriate Fabrics and Colors Opt for fabrics that offer both comfort and elegance. Natural materials such as wool, silk, linen, and cotton tend to breathe better than synthetic alternatives, ensuring you remain cool during warmer months and cozy in colder weather. Additionally, darker colors like black, navy blue, and deep greens generally convey formality and sophistication. However, don't shy away from bolder hues if they align with the atmosphere of the performance or suit your personal style. Step 4: Select Flattering yet Comfortable Footwear While heels and dress shoes often complement formal outfits, ensure any footwear you choose does not compromise your ability to enjoy the entirety of the performance. Opt for well-fitted, broken-in shoes to prevent discomfort throughout the evening. If high heels aren't feasible, consider stylish flats or loafers instead. Step 5: Accessorize Thoughtfully Accessories serve two purposes - enhancing your appearance and functionality. Jewelry, scarves, belts, and bags should coordinate with the rest of your ensemble but also serve their intended purpose (e.g., keeping track of tickets, phones, etc.). Be mindful of accessories that may make noise and potentially distract fellow audience members during quiet moments of the performance. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Avoid excessive fragrances, which can disturb other patrons. Instead, rely on subtle colognes or perfumes. * Layer your clothes to accommodate potential temperature fluctuations within the theatre. * Refrain from wearing large hats or headpieces that obstruct views for those seated behind you. * Keep bulky items at home or in the car since oversized bags and backpacks can take up valuable space and create unnecessary distractions. * Always check the weather forecast before leaving home to prepare accordingly. Conclusion: By following this comprehensive guide, you will be able to select an appropriate outfit for your next theatre visit, ensuring maximum enjoyment and appreciation of the artistic spectacle unfolding before you. Remember, proper etiquette extends beyond just behavior during the performance; being respectfully dressed contributes to creating a positive environment where everyone can fully appreciate the magic of live theatre. Examples of ISO numbers are ISO 50, ISO 100, IS0 200, ISO 400, ISO 800, and ISO 1600. The ISO number is sometimes called the <nowiki>"ASA number" or the "film speed"</nowiki>. When the ISO number is low, for example ISO 50, the film takes a long time to record the picture. This is called a slow film. This means the shutter has to stay open for a long time. When the ISO number is high, for example ISO 800, the picture is made in a very short time. This is a fast film. This means the shutter has to open and close quickly. [Home Visit 3 takes place at the kitchen table] Title: How to Control Weeds with a Hoe Introduction: Weeding is an essential task in maintaining a healthy garden. One effective way to control weeds is by using a hoe. This tool can help you remove young weeds before they establish deep roots or set seeds, making your job easier. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do it. Step 1: Choose the Right Type of Hoe There are various types of hoes available, such as oscillating hoes, stirrup hoes, and Warren hoes. For general weed control, a standard Dutch hoe works well. It has a flat blade perpendicular to the handle, which allows efficient slicing action just below the soil surface. Step 2: Select the Appropriate Time The best time to hoel weeds is when the soil is dry. Dry soil causes the cut weed parts to shrivel up and die quickly, preventing re-growth. Also, working in damp conditions might create clumps that could potentially harbor more weeds. Step 3: Prepare Your Body Using a hoe requires physical effort, so prepare yourself adequately. Stretch your muscles, wear protective clothing like gloves, and ensure proper footwear. Using a kneeling pad may also make the process more comfortable if you plan to work close to the ground. Step 4: Start Hoeting Hold the hoe handle firmly near its end, keeping your arms straight. Use a pushing-and-pulling motion rather than lifting the hoe out of the ground after every stroke; this saves energy and reduces back strain. Always aim to slice off the weed at its base. Key Tip: Try to disturb only the top inch or two of soil during this process. Going deeper will bring up new dormant seeds, increasing future weed problems. Step 5: Dispose Of The Weeds Properly Don't leave chopped-up weeds on the ground where they can regrow or compost naturally. Collect them and dispose of them away from your garden area. Step 6: Frequency Matters Regular hoeing (every 7-10 days) keeps weeds under control. If you wait too long between sessions, the weeds will have grown large enough to require hand pulling or chemical treatment. Step 7: Maintain Your Tool Clean your hoe after each use to prevent rust build-up. Sharpen the blade regularly for clean cuts. Store it in a cool, dry place to prolong its lifespan. Guidelines: 1. Avoid hoeting when the weather is windy to prevent accidentally chopping desirable plants. 2. Be cautious around plant roots - repeated shallow cultivation encourages root growth, but going too deep can damage them. 3. Hoeing is most effective against annual weeds. Perennial weeds with extensive root systems might need additional measures like digging them out completely. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be able to maintain a beautiful, weed-free garden using nothing more than a humble hoe! See what clothes are in there to hang now. Title: How to Change the Name on a Third Generation iPod Shuffle The third generation iPod Shuffle is a compact digital audio player developed by Apple Inc. If you own one of these devices and want to change its name, this tutorial will guide you through the process. By following these steps, you can customize your device's name according to your preference. This personalization enhances user experience and makes identification easier when connecting multiple iOS devices to a computer or other compatible accessories. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A computer with internet access (Windows or Mac) 2. The latest version of iTunes installed 3. Your third generation iPod Shuffle connected to your computer via USB cable **Step 1: Download and Install iTunes** If you haven't already, download and install the latest version of iTunes from the official Apple website onto your computer. Having an updated version ensures compatibility with your iPod Shuffle and allows smooth functioning during the renaming process. **Step 2: Connect Your Device** Connect your third generation iPod Shuffle to your computer using the provided USB cable. Wait for iTunes to recognize the connection; usually indicated by a pop-up notification in the upper right corner of the screen. **Key Tip**: Make sure that your device is turned on before plugging it into your computer. Also, ensure that no other applications are utilizing the device while performing this operation. **Step 3: Open iTunes Library** Launch iTunes if it doesn't open automatically after recognizing the connected device. Locate your music library within iTunes where all your media files reside. **Step 4: Select Your iPod Shuffle** In the top left corner of iTunes window, click on the small icon resembling a musical note. From the dropdown menu, select your iPod Shuffle under 'Devices'. Clicking on it opens up another window displaying information about your device including settings, storage capacity, serial number etc. **Step 5: Navigate To Summary Tab** Once your iPod Shuffle details appear, navigate to the 'Summary' tab located at the top of the new window. Here you can see general info about your device such as software version, playlist sync options, and more importantly, the current name of your device displayed under 'Name'. **Step 6: Edit Device Name** To modify the existing name, simply place your cursor inside the text box next to 'Name', delete the old name, then type in your preferred new name. Remember to keep it short yet descriptive for easy recognition. **Guideline**: Avoid special characters or symbols which may not be supported by some systems causing difficulty during file transfers or synchronizations. **Step 7: Apply Changes & Sync** After entering the new name, click on the 'Apply' button at the bottom right of the iTunes window. Confirmation message should pop up saying "iPod Shuffle successfully renamed". Now hit the 'Sync' button nearby to save changes and transfer them to your device. **Key Tip**: Always eject your iPod safely once sync completes. On Windows, you can do this via Safely Remove Hardware option near system tray. For Mac users, just right-click on the device in Finder sidebar and choose Eject. Congratulations! You have now successfully changed the name on your third generation iPod Shuffle. Enjoy hassle-free connectivity and quicker identification whenever you attach it to different interfaces. Disable Message Previews in iOS: A Comprehensive Tutorial ===================================================== Are you tired of message previews popping up on your iPhone's lock screen, revealing sensitive information to anyone who happens to glance at your phone? Luckily, Apple has provided a way to disable these previews, giving you more privacy and control over your device. This comprehensive tutorial will guide you through the process of disabling message previews in iOS, with clear instructions, helpful tips, and important guidelines. Why Disable Message Previews? ------------------------------ Disabling message previews can help protect your privacy by preventing others from seeing the content of your messages without unlocking your device. It also reduces visual clutter on your lock screen, making it easier to focus on notifications that require immediate attention. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Make sure you have the latest version of iOS installed before proceeding. You can check for updates by going to Settings > General > Software Update. * The following steps are applicable for devices running iOS 14 or later. If you have an older version of iOS, some steps may differ slightly. * Be aware that after disabling message previews, you will need to unlock your device to view new message contents. Step 1: Access Notification Settings ----------------------------------- 1. Open the **Settings** app on your iPhone's home screen. The gear icon makes it easy to identify. 2. Scroll down and tap on **Notifications**. This section contains all settings related to your device's notification behavior. Step 2: Configure Lock Screen Settings ------------------------------------- 1. Locate the **Show Previews** option near the top of the page. Tap on this toggle switch to turn off message previews on the lock screen. When disabled, the switch will appear gray instead of green. * _Key Tip:_ Leave Show Previews enabled if you want to continue receiving preview snippets when your device is unlocked. ![Show Previews Option](#) Step 3: Customize Message App Settings (Optional) ----------------------------------------------- If you wish to further customize how message notifications behave, complete the following optional steps. These adjustments affect how messages are displayed when your device is unlocked. 1. Go back to the main **Settings** menu and select **Messages**. 2. Tap on **Notification Style**. Here, you can choose between three options: **None**, **Banners**, or **Alerts**. Select **None** to completely hide message previews while your device is unlocked. * _Key Tip:_ Consider using Banners as they briefly display the message but automatically disappear, reducing distractions compared to Alerts which remain visible until dismissed manually. ![Message App Settings](#) Congratulations! By completing these steps, you have successfully disabled message previews on both your locked and unlocked iPhone, enhancing your privacy and minimizing visual clutter. Remember, should you ever change your mind, simply revisit these settings and enable Show Previews to restore previous functionality. See if we can see how many... Imagine you have a big box full of toys, and each toy has a "funness" score represented by the number $q$. This score can be any non-negative number, so some toys might not be very fun ($q=0$), while others could be super duper fun ($q$ is really large). Now, let's think about two things: 1. **Total Funness:** This is just the sum of all individual toy funness scores, or in math language, $\int_\Omega q\, d\mu$. We denote it by $B$. 2. **Overall Happiness:** This represents the total happiness you get when playing with all the toys together. To calculate this, you need to consider both the funness and the number of toys. It turns out that the overall happiness never exceeds $(1+\text{total funness})$. In mathematical notation, $(\int_\Omega \sqrt{1 + q^2}\, d\mu)^2 \leq (\int_\Omega (1+q)\, d\mu)^2 = (1+B)^2$. Why does this make sense? Well, imagine you had one incredibly fun toy with $q=100$. Playing with it alone makes you happy, but having another equally fun toy doesn't double your happiness-you still feel like there's only one amazingly fun thing around! That's why our overall happiness grows slower than the total funness. For the lower bound part, remember that every toy should provide _some_ amount of happiness. So, the least possible overall happiness must come from toys being barely fun at all. Thus, $\sqrt{1+(\int_\Omega q\, d\mu)^2}=\sqrt{1+B^2}$ serves as the minimum overall happiness. It is a vestigial reflex, a remnant of the past when pre-human babies clung to their mothers' hair as the mothers swung through the trees. This is borne out by the babies' feet, which curl up when it is sitting down (primate babies grip with the feet as well). All primates except modern man have thick body hair to which an infant can cling, unlike modern humans. The grasp reflex allows the mother to escape danger by climbing a tree using both hands and feet. Now what are you going to go after? Umnini, a chief who lived near the head of Natal Bay, had the chief command of the whole army, whilst Inyati had command of the men who came from the Umzimvubu district. There had been several consultations between Umnini, Inyati, and other chiefs, as to the best method of defending the country, and a plan was arranged which was well thought out and carefully planned. The course which it was thought the Zulus would follow, was on the open country about five miles from the coast; they must, however, pass through a portion of the bush near the Umbilo river at the head of Natal Bay, and it was here that our attack was to be made. Our plan was, to allow a certain portion of the army to pass through the bush; then to dash in, and separate this portion from the main body; keep the main body at bay whilst our men attacked those who had passed into the open beyond the bush. Creating Grungy Brushes using Inkscape: A Comprehensive Tutorial ============================================================= Inkscape is a powerful vector graphics editor that can be used to create various types of designs, including grungy brushes. This comprehensive tutorial will guide you through the process of creating your own custom grungy brushes using Inkscape. By following these steps, you'll learn how to manipulate shapes, utilize patterns, and adjust settings to produce an authentic grunge effect. I. By: Christen Walton Rationale: Children need to be able to understand that when our mouth moves and makes a sound, that is a phoneme. Along with this, they need to be able to understand the relationship between letters and phonemes, and this lesson will help students do this. Short vowels are some of the first correspondences that students need to learn and so today we are going to learn the correspondence a = /a/. We will do this by learning a meaningful representation of the /a/ sound, by recognizing it in spoken words, by spelling words with /a/ in them in a letterbox lesson, and by recognizing /a/ words in the text The Cat Nap. Materials: letterboxes for each child, letter tiles for each child (they will need the letters c, a, t, b, l, p, s, g, r, h), chalk, chalkboard, class copies of the book The Cat Nap (Educational Insights), two sheets of primary paper for each child, picture page for the assessment 1. Introduce the lesson by explaining why it is important to learn that letters represent the sounds. "Today we are going to learn about the letter a and the sound it makes. We want to learn this sound so that when we hear it we will be able to write is as well." 2. Ask the students to take out primary writing paper. Review how to make the letter a and have the students practice on their own paper after they have listened to me explain and model how to make it. 3. "Have you ever heard a baby cry? Well the /a/ sound is very similar to that sound. I want to hear everyone make a sound like a baby does." (Have all the children make this sound) "I am going to say some words and if you hear the /a/ sound then I want you to say, "aaaahh." Cat, dog, bed, jack, glass, yes, and can." 4. Pass out letterboxes and letter tiles to each child. "Now we are going to work on spelling words that have the /a/ sound in them. Each of you have his or her own letterboxes and these letterboxes will help use know how many sounds are in each word. First we are going to use three boxes. I will show you first with the word cat. I hear the /k/ sound so I am going to put a c in the first box. I hear the /a/ sound that we were working on so I am going to but an a in the second box. I hear a /t/ sound and I am going to put a t in the last box. Now I want each of you to try." I will give them the words bat, lap, pal, last, grab, trash. After each word I will call on one child to write on the board what he or she put in each letterbox. Then I will write the words on the board one and at a time and the class will say each word together. 5. Pass out a copy of The Cat Nap to each student and have them each read the book silently. "Today we are going to read a book about a cat named Tab who likes to nap all the time. I want you to read the book silently and then when you are finished, I want you to be thinking of words you found in the book that have the /a/ sound." Then I will call on students to share words they found and write them on the board. 5. For the assessment I will give each child a page that has many pictures on it. Across the top of a page, there will be a list of the words: mat, cab, tag, and sack. The children will match the words to the pictures and write them below the picture. Reference: Murray, Bruce A., and Theresa Lesniak. (1999) "The Letterbox lesson: A hands-on approach for teaching decoding." The Reading Teacher, March 1999. pp 644-650. Marti MacKenzie: We have four classes of 4th grade, and actually this is somewhat of a typical class, I think, for Winchester city-a mix of abilities, but no one at the really high end and no one at the really low end, so it's a nice, average class. These are very inquisitive children. Marti MacKenzie: Imagine you've been exploring in America. Imagine you were Christopher Columbus, and you went back to Europe. Jocelyn: You could bring a garden; um, they could have some gourds. Marti MacKenzie: Okay, guys, this is what happened. The people that came to America wanted the Europeans to know what the Indians were like, so they wrote a lot of stories; but they also sent this wonderful guy called John White, and guess what he was. He was an artist. He was the Mr. Edwards of these explorers. And he decided to do some drawings. So what we're going to do today is we're going to look at actual drawings that John White did, but there's a problem. Marti MacKenzie: Now we've just moved into the first Virginians, the first Americans, so we're really just starting the history content. Student: [indistinct comment] Marti MacKenzie: Well, this guy named De Bry, said "I'm going to take that drawing, and I am going to make an engraving." Marti MacKenzie: The idea was just to understand what a primary source is, to have a chance to interact. This is the first opportunity they've had to really interact with a primary source. Marti MacKenzie: So, look at your drawing. Just study it. Look at detail. Then we're going to look at the watercolor first. Deduce and compare. How are they alike, but how are they different? Student 1: Um. It's that, um, picture-that picture that he made, their heads are more rounded. And this one more like, um. Marti MacKenzie: Yeah, they really are. Well, as good historians, we would go and probably look at both. We would look at the watercolor, and we would look at the engraving; but today, we're going to do some history. We're going to do some history by looking at the one in front of you-at the engraving. Marti MacKenzie: I just wanted to introduce something I thought they would have some fun with, that would be easy on their level, and give me some scaffolding that I can start building on; and then we can get a little more complex in using primary sources in the future. Once upon a time, there was a young girl named Emmy. Emmy was gifted and curious. She often wondered what it would be like to be something else in the world. One day, Emmy saw a beetle. She got closer to take a better look. "Hello there," said Emmy. "I want to be like you, little beetle. Would you show me how it feels to be like you?" The beetle smiled, and nodded its head. Then, it flew onto the tip of Emmy's finger. Emmy smiled, and the beetle flew away. Emmy suddenly felt different. She felt like she could fly, and she wanted to see what it was like. Taking a leap of faith, Emmy jumped off the ground and flew around. Emmy was amazed. Now she knew what it was like to be like the beetle. Most medium- and large-sized banks maintain reserve accounts at one of the 12 regional Federal Reserve Banks, and they pay for the cash they get from the Fed by having those accounts debited. Some smaller banks maintain their required reserves at larger, "correspondent," banks. The smaller banks get cash through the correspondent banks, which charge a fee for the service. "as the only means, after my loss of reputation, of obtaining respect, or even the toleration of humanity, I had not the least scruple to secrete a part of the sums intrusted to me, and to screen myself from detection by a system of falshood. But, acquiring new principles, I began to have the ambition of returning to the respectable part of society, and was weak enough to suppose it possible. The attention of my unassuming instructor, who, without being ignorant of his own powers, possessed great simplicity of manners, strengthened the illusion. Having sometimes caught up hints for thought, from my untutored remarks, he often led me to discuss the subjects he was treating, and would read to me his productions, previous to their publication, wishing to profit by the criticism of unsophisticated feeling. The aim of his writings was to touch the simple springs of the heart; for he despised the would-be oracles, the self-elected philosophers, who fright away fancy, while sifting each grain of thought to prove that slowness of comprehension is wisdom. [begins to draw eyes on the pumpkin] [mother points to the ears on the hat.] [sits on floor and puts toe protector on her heel] TenMarks teaches you how to apply the order of operations to evaluate expressions. Read the full transcript Learn about Order of Operations in Expressions Order of operations Before we begin and move on to our problem, let's talk about order of operations. We use the rules for the order of operations. They are a set of rules. Now the rules for the order of operations for solving a problem have more than one operation. So let's talk about this for a second. The order of operation goes as such. First, you do the operations in parenthesis first. So you do what's in parenthesis first, then you calculate all expressions with exponents. After that, you then do any multiply and divide. Anything that you need to multiply and divide, so you do that but remember you go from left to right. You always move from left to right and then the last stop in the order of operations is you add and subtract. But remember again, you always go from left to right. The order of operations can be easily remembered by memorizing the following sentence. Please excuse my dear Aunt Sally or use the PEMDAS. Please excuse me, so please begins with the P that tells you to do parenthesis. Excuse means you do your exponents. My means you do your multiplication. My dear, so D means your division. Aunt Sally, so A for aunt means your addition and then S for Sally is your subtraction. These are your order of operations. Now let's move on and start our problem here. So here we need to write an expression for the cost of 32 jars of lady bugs and 25 crates of bees. Then we need to evaluate. So here, the price of 1 jar of lady bugs is $10.50. We're looking for the number of jars of lady bugs that were bought were 32. And the price of 1 crate of bees is $12.75 and we know that the number of crates of these bought were 25 crates, so we know they bought 25 crates. That means that the cost of 32 jars of lady bugs is $10.50 32, so we're taking the cost for 1 jar of lady bugs and we're multiplying it by the number of jars of lady bugs bought. And now we know that the cost of 25 crates of bees is $12.75 25 because we're taking the cost of 1 crate which is $12.75 and we're multiplying it with the number of crates of bees. We can write the expression for the total cost. So our total cost will be $10.50 32+$12.75 25, our total cost will be the total of the jars of lady bugs plus the total of the crates of bees. Now let's evaluate our expression. We're going to use our order of operations here. Our first step is parenthesis, well in this expression we do not have any parenthesis so we're going to move on. Our next step is exponents, we don't have any exponents. So our next step is multiplication. We're going to multiply first and when you multiply from left to right, so we're going to multiply starting with our left to our right. We multiply $10.50 32 and we get $336.00 and then we do our other multiplications. So I'm not adding anything yet, I'm multiplying. I went from my left now I go to my right and I multiply $12.75 by 25 and I get $318.75. Now, our next step after we multiply is divide but we don't have any division so now we go to A, Aunt Sally. So aunt is addition, we do have addition. I'm going to add my two numbers and I get a total of $654.75. The total cost of the 32 jars of lady bugs and the 25 crates of bees is $654.75. Let's move on to our next problem. So here we need to evaluate 125-(8+9)+112 13. Here we need to follow the order of operations to evaluate the expression. The first thing to do remember is we need to do what's in parenthesis. We're going to solve our parenthesis first. I'm going to take 125-(8+9) which is 17 and then I'm going to write the rest of my equation. So here I did what's in parenthesis first. The next step is use exponents and I do have my exponents, 112. So 125 remain the same, 17+, well 112 would be 121 13. Now in the order of operations, we multiply and divide next. So here I have multiplication. Everything else I'm keeping the same and then I multiply. I'm multiplying 121 by 13, 121 13 is 1,573. And remember when you multiply you're going l And her poor eye doesn't work right. [tries to look inside the teepee in the busy book] Title: How to Get Music from YouTube Using MediaConvert.com Introduction: MediaConvert.com is an online tool that allows you to convert YouTube videos into various formats, including MP3 audio files. This tutorial will guide you through the process of extracting music from YouTube using this website while providing helpful tips and guidelines along the way. Step 1: Find Your Video on YouTube Start by searching for the video containing the song you want to download on YouTube's search bar. Once you find your desired video, copy its URL from the address bar at the top of your web browser. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Make sure the video contains only the audio content you wish to obtain. Videos with copyrighted material may be taken down or have their audio disabled due to legal reasons. - Verify if there are any restrictions regarding usage or distribution before proceeding further. Step 2: Go to MediaConvert.com Open a new tab in your browser and navigate to <https://mediaconvert.com/>. Upon arrival, read through their terms of service and ensure they align with what you intend to do (download and convert YouTube videos). If everything checks out, continue to the next step. Step 3: Paste the Copied URL Locate the input box labeled 'Paste YouTube Link Here'. Right-click inside the box and select 'Paste', or press Ctrl+V (Cmd+V on Mac) to paste the copied URL directly. Click the 'Download Now!' button right beside the input field. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Double-check the URL to avoid errors during conversion. - Be patient as the server processes your request; it might take several seconds depending on traffic volume. Step 4: Choose Output Format After clicking 'Download Now!', you'll be redirected to another page displaying multiple output format options. For converting YouTube videos to audio files, choose the '.mp3' option under Audio Formats section. Then click 'Start Conversion'. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Choosing .mp3 ensures maximum compatibility across different devices and platforms. - Other audio formats like AAC, FLAC, M4A etc., can also be chosen based on specific requirements. Step 5: Wait for Conversion Completion The conversion process usually takes longer than loading the initial page. During this time, refrain from closing the active window or navigating away until completion notification appears. Step 6: Download Converted File Upon successful conversion, you'll see a green 'Download' button near the completed progress bar. Click it to save the converted file onto your device storage. Depending on your browser settings, you might need to confirm the location where the file should be saved. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Remember the directory where you stored the file for easy access later. - Some browsers block automatic downloads for security purposes; allow them manually if prompted. Conclusion: By following these six steps meticulously, you now know how to get music from YouTube using MediaConvert.com. Always respect copyright laws and fair use policies when obtaining media contents online. Enjoy listening to your favorite tunes offline without compromising sound quality! " Skeleton you have done your wicked deeds until you have tried my patience. " A day at the beach is a wonderful way to spend time with your family and friends. You can swim, play games and build sand castles. But have you ever wondered how the beach you are standing on came to be? How, for example, did all of that sand get there? Beaches are formed and continually changed by the ocean's waves moving rock particles onshore, offshore and along the shore. In this activity, you can investigate how beach formations are made by some parts of a beach that can resist erosion from the waves more than other parts. A beach is a geologic formation made up of loose rock particles such as sand, gravel and shell fragments deposited along the shoreline of a body of water. A beach has a few key features. The berm is the part that is mostly above water; this is the active shoreline. The top of the berm is known as the crest, and the part that slopes toward the water is called the face. At the bottom of the face there may be a trough and, further seaward, there may be sandbars parallel to the beach. The erosion of rock formations in the water, coral reefs and headlands create rock particles that the waves move onshore, offshore and along the shore, creating the beach. Continual erosion of the shoreline by waves also changes the beach over time. One change that erosion can cause is the appearance of a headland. This is land that juts out from the coastline and into the water and affects how the surrounding shoreline is eroded. Paint-roller pan Measuring cup Digital camera Plastic 500-milliliter water bottle (empty) Adult volunteer to help take pictures Small gravel, such as aquarium gravel Cover the bottom of the paint-roller pan with five cups of sand. Build up a beach with most, but not all, of the sand at the shallow end of the pan. Slowly pour six cups of water into the deep end of the pan. Let the water and sand settle for five minutes. How has the beach changed during this time? Take a picture of your beach so that you have a record of how it looked in its original state. Where is the shoreline (the area where beach and water meet)?* Lay a plastic bottle horizontally so it is floating in the water in the deep end of the pan. For two minutes bob the water bottle up and down with your fingertips to create waves. If the waves get so big that water splashes out of the pan, make them smaller. How does the water swirl? How does the shoreline change after one minute? What about after two minutes? After two minutes of bobbing the bottle, take a picture of the beach. How does it look compared with the first picture? Empty, clean and dry the paint-roller pan. Prepare a "beach" again, as you did for the preparation. When the beach is complete, make a "headland" by creating a mound out of two cups of small gravel in the middle of the shoreline. The headland should be partly in the water and partly on the beach. Take a picture of the beach with the headland. Again, lay the plastic bottle horizontally so it is floating in the water. For two minutes, bob the water bottle up and down with your fingertips. Again, if the waves are so big that water splashes out, make them smaller. How does the water swirl? How does the shoreline change after one minute? What about after two minutes? After two minutes, take a picture of the beach. How does it look compared with the previous picture? How does the headland affect where the water goes? How does it affect how much the shoreline erodes? Extra: Repeat this activity at least two more times with a ruler taped to the side of the pan. Exactly how much shoreline erosion occurs with and without a headland? Extra: Try increasing or decreasing the speed of bobbing the bottle. Does this affect how the beach changes over time? Extra: Pour a large volume of water all at once into the deep end of the pan to simulate a storm surge or a tsunami. [begins to show Cha how the beads pop together] Inside a conductor, electrons bounce around, but they do not keep going in one direction for long. If an electric field is set up inside the conductor, the electrons will all start to move in the direction opposite to the direction the field is pointing (because electrons are negatively charged). A battery can make an electric field inside a conductor. If both ends of a piece of wire are connected to the two ends of a battery (called the "electrodes"), the loop that was made is called an electrical circuit. Electrons will flow around and around the circuit as long as the battery is making an electric field inside the wire. This flow of electrons around the circuit is called electric current. (En espa ol: Bronquios) Say: brong-kee-ul toobz When a person breathes, air comes in through the nose or mouth and then goes into the trachea (windpipe). From there, it passes through the bronchial tubes, which are in the lungs. These tubes, or airways, let air in and out of your lungs, so you can breathe. You can call them by their nickname: bronchi (say: brong-kye). The bronchial tubes branch into smaller tubes called bronchioles (say: brong-kee-ohlz). Sexual identity and sexual behavior are connected to sexual orientation but are different. Sexual identity is a concept of self. Sexual behavior is sex with other people. Sexual orientation is connections to other people. An individual may or may not show sexual orientation from behavior. A person who is not heterosexual by sexual orientation and has a heterosexual sexual identity is 'closeted'. This term may show a stage of change in any society that, little by little, attempts to support the sexual minority. In any sexual orientation study, scientists use the terms "concordance" (sexual attraction, sexual behavior, and sexual identity match) or "discordance" (sexual attraction, sexual behavior, and sexual identity do not match)"." Here is an example of discordance. A woman with attraction to women says she is heterosexual and only has sex with men. Sexual orientation (homosexual), sexual identity (heterosexual), and sexual behavior (heterosexual) of the woman do not match. oh, poor doll don't throw her around. One of his young students was a boy whose name was Gian Giacomo Caprotti da Oreno. He was a handsome boy with beautiful long golden curls. He looked perfect as an artist's model for an angel. But he was such a difficult and dishonest boy that Leonardo called him "Salai" or "Salaino" which means "the little devil". Salai stayed in Leonardo's household for thirty years as a pupil and a servant. Blood carries various substances that must be brought to one part of the body or another. Red blood cells are an important element of blood. Their job is to transport oxygen to the body's tissues in exchange for carbon dioxide, which is carried to and eliminated by the lungs. Red blood cells are formed in the red bone marrow of bones. Stem cells in the red bone marrow called hemocytoblasts give rise to all of the formed elements in blood. If a hemocytoblast commits to becoming a cell called a proerythroblast, it will develop into a new red blood cell. The formation of a red blood cell from hemocytoblast takes about 2 days. The body makes about two million red blood cells every second. Blood is made up of both cellular and liquid components. If a sample of blood is spun in a centrifuge, the formed elements and fluid matrix of blood can be separated from each other. Blood consists of 45% red blood cells, less than 1% white blood cells and platelets, and 55% plasma. An' then we'll put a little toothpaste on it? Baby Jenny was feeling quite fierce. She was finally relaxed. [Nina wants her mother to build again] Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's learn about factoring - a fun and useful tool to solve certain types of math problems. Factoring is like breaking down a number into its building blocks, also known as "factors." In algebra, factoring helps us simplify expressions and solve equations more easily. Let me show you how it works using our favorite animal friends! Suppose we have three happy animals: two dogs and one bird. They all want to cross a river together, but unfortunately, there's only room on the raft for two animals at a time. How can they get across? Well, first, they need to pair up differently so that their combined weight allows them to travel safely on the raft. Our possible pairs would be Dog-Dog or Bird-Dog since both combinations will fit nicely onto the raft. Now, imagine this situation represented with an equation instead: D + B = R, where D represents the dogs, B represents the bird, and R stands for the total capacity needed for the raft. We don't know the exact number yet, but we can still work with this information! To find out the value of D and B, we must remember that safe options include having just dogs on the raft or combining a dog and the bird. That means our equation should break down into these separate possibilities: D * 1 = D (only dogs) B * 1 = B (bird and dogs) This step of breaking things apart based on some conditions is similar to factoring polynomials - those complicated expressions involving variables and exponents. When faced with a polynomial expression, we aim to break it into simpler pieces called "factors." These factors often represent familiar concepts, making solutions easier. Let's take a look at an example: Original Quadratic Equation: 2 + x = -3x Standard Form: x + 3x + 2 = 0 Factored Form: (x + 1)(x + 2) = 0 To reach the factored form, notice that the last term (-2) comes from multiplying -1 and -2, while the middle term (3x) results from adding (-1) times x and (-2) times x. This gives us the following steps: Step 1: Identify the pattern in coefficients, i.e., -1, 3, 2 Step 2: Break the middle coefficient into parts that add up to zero, i.e., -1 + 2 = 1 and 1*-1 = -1; 2*-1 = -2 Step 3: Add these breakdowns next to x in the original expression giving us (x + 1)(x + 2). In conclusion, factoring is like finding creative ways for animals to ride the raft or breaking down bigger numbers into smaller ones. By applying this skill to polynomial expressions, we open doors to new techniques for solving complex equations! Keep practicing, and soon enough, factoring will become second nature! Happy learning! THE FOX AND THE BRAMBLE. A FOX, hard pressed by the hounds, was getting over a hedge, but tore his foot upon a Bramble, which grew just in the midst of it, upon which he reproached the Bramble for his inhospitable cruelty in using a stranger, which had fled to him for protection, after such a barbarous manner. "Yes," says the Bramble, "you intended to have made me serve your turn, I know; but take this piece of advice with you for the future: Never lay hold of a Bramble again, as you value your sweet person; for laying hold is a privilege that belongs to us Brambles, and we do not care to let it go out of the family." He chugged past some toy soldiers and a drum past a doll house and an airplane. Pucker up! Have you ever heard of the "kissing disease"? If you said that it's mono, you're absolutely correct. But you don't get mono only from kissing. Infectious , called mono for short, is caused by the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), which is a type of herpes virus. Other viruses in the family cause cold sores and illnesses like chickenpox. How Do Kids Get Mono? Most people who get mono are between the ages of 15 and 25, but younger kids can get it, too. The mono virus affects the , throat, salivary glands, liver, spleen, and blood, and it can make a person feel tired and achy all over. It can also make you lose your appetite. You probably know what your lymph nodes are, and you probably guessed that your salivary glands are inside of your mouth. But what about your spleen? It's located on the left side of your abdomen, just under the ribcage, and it helps cleanse your blood of bacteria and viruses. Mono is contagious, which means you can spread the virus to other people who haven't had mono before. Even though you can get mono from kissing someone infected with EBV, there are other ways you can get it, but they all involve contact with saliva. Sharing straws, toothbrushes, or food from the same plate can also spread mono. At first, people usually don't feel sick after getting infected with the EBV virus. So someone could be infected - and be spreading mono - and not even know it. That's why it's important not to share things like forks, straws, water bottles, or lip gloss at school. Sometimes, it may seem like you have the flu or maybe strep throat because the symptoms are so much alike. The only way to tell for sure if you have mono is to go to a doctor, who will examine you and draw blood for tests (one test is called the Monospot) to see if you have mono. What If I Have Mono? Usually with mono you will need plenty of rest. This might mean no school for a while, no sports, and no running outside playing with friends or even wrestling with your little brother. While you're resting, it's a good idea to drink plenty of water and other fluids. You can ask your mom or dad to give you a pain reliever if you have a fever or if your muscles are sore. Don't take any aspirin, though, because that can put you at risk for a condition called , which can be dangerous. Other kids might not feel very sick at all, so a lot of bed rest isn't necessarily for everyone. But it's very important to listen to your body. Someone who has mono should tell a parent if he or she starts feeling worse. And if the person feels tired and run down, it's the body's way of saying more rest is needed. If you play contact sports, such as football and basketball, you will probably need to avoid them while you're sick and for about a month after the illness - especially if your spleen is enlarged. Your doctor will let you know when it's safe for you to get back in the game. You'll probably be happy to hear that mono usually goes away after a few weeks, even though you'll have to take it easy for awhile. Make sure you wash your hands after you cough or sneeze. Keep your straws, forks, and toothbrushes to yourself, and . . . no kissing for a few months! And these people live here these bunnies. Creating Custom Keyboard Shortcuts on iPhone Follow these steps to create custom keyboard shortcuts on your iPhone: 1. Open the Settings app on your iPhone's home screen. This will open the settings menu where you can adjust various options for your device. 2. Tap on General, which is located towards the top of the list. This will take you to the general settings menu, where you can change various aspects of your phone's functionality. 3. Scroll down and tap on Keyboard. This will bring up the keyboard settings menu, where you can adjust options related to the virtual keyboard that appears when you type on your iPhone. 4. Tap on Text Replacement. This option allows you to set up text shortcuts, which are abbreviated phrases that automatically expand into longer phrases or sentences when typed. 5. Tap the plus sign (+) at the top right corner of the screen. This will allow you to add a new text replacement shortcut. 6. In the Phrase field, enter the full phrase or sentence that you want the shortcut to expand into. For example, if you frequently type out your email address, you could make a shortcut that expands "em" into "[your-email@example.com](mailto:your-email@example.com)." 7. In the Shortcut field, enter the abbreviation or shorthand version of the phrase that you want to trigger the expansion. Using the previous example, you might choose "em" as the shortcut. 8. (Optional) If you would like this shortcut to be available only in specific apps, toggle off the "Prediction" switch next to "All Languages." Then select the applications from the list below where you want this shortcut to work. 9. Once you have entered both the phrase and its corresponding shortcut, tap Save in the upper right corner to save the new shortcut. It should now appear in your list of text replacements. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Keep your shortcuts simple and easy to remember. The more complex they are, the harder they will be to recall and use consistently. Ideally, aim for 2-4 characters per shortcut. * Consider using common acronyms, abbreviations, or initialisms as part of your shortcut. These are often easier to remember than arbitrary combinations of letters. * Avoid creating overlapping shortcuts. Having multiple shortcuts with similar triggers may lead to unpredictable behavior when typing quickly. * Test your shortcuts after adding them to ensure they function correctly across different apps. Not all third-party apps support custom keyboard shortcuts natively, so test thoroughly before relying on them in daily usage. * Regularly review and update your list of shortcuts to keep it current and relevant. Remove any unused ones to avoid confusion and maintain efficiency. By following these steps and keeping the key tips in mind, you can effectively create custom keyboard shortcuts on your iPhone that streamline your typing experience and help you communicate more efficiently. They are also used to teach about Islam. Religious festivals and gatherings are held in mosques. Weddings are one example. Mosques have rules to control what people do inside. One of these is that it is disrespectful to disturb another person who is worshipping. Help for Hands By Barbara Greenspan, OT, Pediatric Occupational Therapist When will I know if my child is a left-handed or right-handed? You may see signs of "hand preference" as early as 12 months. Hand dominance is usually established by the age of 5 years old. A great way to "test" this is to constantly place objects directly in front of your child at their "midline" (the center of their body). Watch to see which hand they use to pick up the object. Repeat this five to ten times for a few days in a row for more valid results. How can I help my child learn to use scissors? Teach your child that the small hole is big enough for only one finger (the thumb) and the larger hole is for 2 fingers. The correct way to hold the scissors is with the middle finger and the ring finger inside the larger hole and the index finger sits outside the large hole to help direct the scissors around curves. The best way to start learning scissor skills is by making short, small snips. Cutting straight lines is next. Cutting around curves is the most difficult. Three-year old children can do some very simple cutting and by age 5 a child should be proficient with scissor skills. By then they are using one hand (the dominant hand) to open and close the scissors and the other hand has the job of holding and turning the paper. |What are some indoor activities I can do with my child?||Barbara Greenspan, OT| |Help for Hands||Barbara Greenspan, OT| Self-defence means fighting off something or another person to protect yourself and maybe others. Ways of self-defence include martial arts or using a weapon. Sometimes, self-defense can cause serious harm to the other person. In most nations, you cannot be prosecuted for this harm. In the United States, an act of self-defence can only count as self-defence if the victim is in a situation that can cause them serious injury or death. In international law all persons have the right to self-defence. The thing inside the tire is a compass. In the 1960s, other people followed, including Puerto Ricans. Introduction to the United States Map ||The students will identify a map of the United States. ||United States Map, country, state ||United States map, globe, map marker |Ask the students the name of our country. Write the name United States on the board. Write the word country on the board. A country is an area of land where people live under one government. |Ask a student to locate the United States on the globe. Outline the shape of the United States on the globe. Locate and circle the Great Lakes and circle Florida. Locate and mark the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans on the globe. |Ask the students if the United States is large enough to be seen well on the globe. Tell the students we want to see a large picture of the United States, therefore we use a map that only shows the United States. This is called a United States Map. |Pull down the map of the United States. Have the globe positioned so that the United States is facing the students. On the map of the United States, locate the same features you located on the globe; the Great Lakes, Florida, and the two oceans. |Tell the students that we will use the United States Map to study about our country. Our country has fifty states. A state is an area of land that is part of our country. Ask the name of the state. Write the name of your state on the board. When we study the states, cities, lakes, and rivers in the United States, we use the United States map because it shows a bigger picture of the United States and is easier to |Locate and list the state(s) that touches your state. Use spatial terms. Ask the students to find the state(s) to the right of your state. Which state is to the left? If she is wearing spike heels, standing on her heels, her total surface area conveying her weight to the floor would be the sum of the area of the heels. Let's say her spike heels each have a surface area of 0,16 cm2 This would be possible is say they were 0,4 cm x 0,4 cm. That would mean she has a total pressure of 70 kg on 0,16 cm2. This is the same as 437,5 kg pr. cm2. Now for the elephant! A male African elephant can weigh as much as 4.500 - 6.000 kg. For the sake of this calculation lets say the elephant weighs 5.000 kg. An elephants foot can be as large as 40-50 cm in length and width. The circumference can be as large as 1,3 m. From math we know that A = x r2 We know , but we need to know the radius, r. This we could take from the width / length measurements, but we want to find it for a circle that has a circumference of 1.3 m. Since O = x d => O / = d => d = 41,4 cm. This equals a radius of 20,7. No we can calculate the surface area of 1 elephant foot to be x 20,72 = 1345, 45 cm2. Since an elephant has 4 feet, the total surface area will be 5381,8 cm2 This gives the elephant a weight to surface-area of 5000 kg on 5381,8 square centimeters of surface, which equals 0,92 kg /cm2. Compare this to the womans which was 437,5 kg / cm2. (Though you should keep in mind, that the womans total surface area was only 0.16 cm2). The answer is: The woman has by far the most pressure on the ground compared to an elephant. Actually she has 470 times as much pressure. This is why you want women to take of their high heels at your party, if you have wooden floors. We are also Using Kolbe's second grade lesson plan for spelling. Each week she gets 12 new words to review and study from Monday to Thursday. A final written test is given on Fridays. It was a bit too challenging for her; the first two weeks she did poorly on the final exam, spelling only one or two words out of 12 correctly. So now we study each list for two weeks. I give her six words each week. If a word is easy and she masters it quickly, I replace it with another word in Kolbe's list for that week. At the end of two weeks we take a final written exam with all 12 words. This has worked much better and she doesn't become overwhelmed by six new words like she did when she saw 12 new words. The first thing you need to do is open the Tinder app on your mobile phone or tablet. This will take you to the main screen where you can see potential matches. 2. Access your profile settings. To access your profile settings, tap on the grayed out profile picture located at the top left corner of the main screen. This will bring up a menu with several options; select "Edit Info" to proceed. 3. Edit your birthdate. Once you are in your profile settings, scroll down until you find the section labeled "Birthday." Here, you should see two fields: one for your month of birth, and another for your day of birth. Tap on either field to edit your birthdate. After making changes, make sure to save them by tapping the checkmark icon at the top right corner of the screen. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Make sure to enter your correct birthdate as per your government issued ID proofs. Entering false information violates Tinder's Terms of Use and may result in account suspension or termination. - If you accidentally entered the wrong birthdate, don't create a new account just yet. Instead, try contacting Tinder support to request assistance in updating your birthdate. 4. Update your photos and bio (optional). While this doesn't directly affect your age displayed on Tinder, updating your photos and bio regularly can help keep your profile fresh and engaging. Additionally, having high-quality photos and a well-written bio can increase your chances of matching with other users. 5. Check if the change was successful. Go back to the main screen and refresh your feed by swiping down. Then, view your profile again by tapping on the grayed out profile picture at the top left corner of the screen. Your updated birthdate should now be visible under the "Birthday" section. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep in mind that changing your birthdate might impact your visibility on Tinder, especially if you were previously listed as being outside of the preferred age range for most users. However, this shouldn't discourage you from updating accurate information. 6. Notify any existing matches about the change (if necessary). If you have already matched with someone before changing your birthdate, consider sending them a message explaining the situation. Honesty goes a long way in building trust and maintaining healthy relationships. 7. Monitor your account closely after making the change. Be aware that some users might react negatively to the sudden change in your age. It's essential to remain respectful and understanding while addressing any concerns raised by your matches. 8. Remember to abide by Tinder's Community Guidelines and Terms of Service when using the platform. Always treat others with kindness and respect, avoid sharing explicit content, and never engage in harassment or bullying behavior. By following these steps, you can ensure that your age is accurately represented on Tinder and maintain a positive user experience for both yourself and your potential matches. Testicular cancer is cancer in one or both testicles, a part of the male reproductive system. In the United States, about 8,000 to 9,000 people are diagnosed with the cancer every year. Every male has a 1 in 250 (four tenths of one percent, or 0.4%) chance of being diagnosed with it. It is most common among males aged 15-40 years. Testicular cancer has one of the highest cure rates of all cancers; more than 90%. This is most often achieved by removing one or both testicles. Even in the small number of cases where the cancer has spread widely, chemotherapy offers a cure rate of at least 50%. Pull that string out and see what's in there. Title: Create a Chinese Fan Hairstyle Introduction: The Chinese fan hairstyle is an elegant and intricate updo that has been worn by women in China for centuries. This hairstyle involves creating a series of folds at the back of the head, reminiscent of a folded Chinese fan. It's perfect for special occasions such as weddings, parties or cultural events. Here is a step-by-step guide to help you recreate this beautiful hairstyle. Materials Needed: 1. Comb 2. Hairpins 3. Elastic bands 4. U-shaped hairpins (optional) 5. Hairspray (optional) Step 1: Brush Your Hair Before starting any hairstyle, begin by brushing your hair thoroughly from roots to ends. This will remove any tangles or knots, making the rest of the styling process easier. Step 2: Section Off Top Portion of Hair Take about one third of your hair from the top of your head and secure it with an elastic band. This section should start just above your ears and go all the way back to the crown of your head. Step 3: Divide the Top Section into Three Parts Divide the secured portion of hair into three equal sections. These divisions don't have to be precise; they're simply there to make the next steps more manageable. Step 4: Start Creating the First Fold Take the rightmost strand and fold it towards the middle, then take the leftmost strand and do the same over the first fold. You should now have two folds side by side. Secure these folds with a few hairpins. Step 5: Continue Making Additional Folds Repeat Step 4, taking care to tuck each new fold under the previous ones. Each time you add a new fold, tighten the preceding folds slightly so that they maintain their shape. Keep adding folds until all the hair in this section is incorporated. Step 6: Repeat Steps 2-5 With Remaining Hair Release the remaining lower two thirds of your hair. Again, divide this section into three parts and repeat steps 4 and 5. Be sure to integrate the upper fan neatly into the lower one. Step 7: Finalize the Look Once all your hair is incorporated into the 'fan', check for stray hairs and tuck them in place using additional hairpins if necessary. If desired, apply hairspray to hold everything in place. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Patience and precision are key when executing this hairstyle. Don't rush - take your time to ensure each fold is properly placed and secured. - Use enough hairpins to keep each layer stable but avoid overloading with pins which can cause discomfort. - For longer hair, consider using U-shaped pins for added stability and security. - Practice makes perfect. The first few attempts might not turn out perfectly, but with practice, you'll get better. Conclusion: Creating a Chinese fan hairstyle may seem intimidating initially, but with patience and careful attention to detail, anyone can master this stunning look. Whether you're preparing for a traditional celebration or just want to try something different, give this historic style a chance. Christina Ricci (born February 12, 1980) is an American actress. In January 1994, Jerry Yang and David Filo were Electrical Engineering graduate students at Stanford University. In April 1994, "Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web" was renamed "Yahoo!", for which the official is "Yet Another Hierarchical Officious Oracle". Filo and Yang said they selected the name because they liked the word's general meaning, which comes from Gulliver's Travels by Jonathan Swift: "rude, and ". Its URL was akebono.stanford.edu/yahoo. Yang describes their selection of the name as "We thought it fit well with what we were doing. It was irreverent. It was reflective of the Wild West nature of the Internet. A lot of people found it easy to remember, and besides, it's exactly what me and Jerry are. A couple of yahoos." Title: How to Bowl a Cricket Ball Step 1: Understand the Basics Before you start bowling, it is essential to understand the basics of cricket and the rules regarding bowling. A cricket match involves two teams, each taking turns to bat and bowl. The team that scores the most runs wins. Bowlers deliver the ball towards the wicket defended by the batsman from the opposite team. Their aim is to get the batsman out (a situation known as 'wicket') through various methods such as bowled, caught, leg before wicket (LBW), stumped or run out. Step 2: Grip the Ball Correctly Hold the cricket ball with your index finger and middle finger placed on top of the seam, slightly apart. Your ring finger should be tucked under the ball while your little finger provides balance underneath. Your thumb rests lightly on the other side of the seam. This grip allows control over swing and spin. Key Tip: Avoid holding the ball too tightly; maintain a firm but relaxed hold. Step 3: Adopt the Right Stance Stand at the center of the crease facing the batsman. Position your body square to the target, i.e., the wickets. Both feet must be behind the popping crease. Bend your knees slightly, keep your back straight, relax your shoulders, and tilt your head so you can see the batsman clearly. Guideline: Make sure your non-bowling arm points towards the batsman for better alignment and balance. Step 4: Run-Up The run-up is a crucial part of the approach to delivery stride. It varies among bowlers depending upon their pace and comfort level. Start with slow jogging steps, gradually increasing speed until just before delivering the ball. Maintain rhythm and momentum throughout the process. Key Tip: Practice different lengths of run-ups to find what works best for you without compromising accuracy and speed. Step 5: Action Towards Delivery Stride As you complete your run-up, shift weight onto your leading foot (the one closer to the batsman). Bring your bowling arm upwards, keeping it close to your ear, elbow high, and wrist cocked back. Rotate your shoulder and hip simultaneously during this motion. Step 6: Delivering the Ball At the point of release, extend your bowling arm fully, snapping your wrist downward to impart force on the ball. Release the ball when it's directly above the target. Follow through after releasing the ball by continuing your action naturally, letting your momentum carry you past the crease. Key Tips: * Keep your eye on the target till the last moment. * Use your fingers to generate spin or movement rather than throwing the ball. Step 7: Assess Performance and Improve Analyze every delivery critically - where did the ball pitch? Did it move away or come into the batsman? Was there enough pace? Based on these observations, make necessary adjustments to improve future performances. Remember, becoming an effective bowler requires consistent practice. Mastering line, length, pace, and variation will only come with time. So keep practicing, stay patient, and enjoy the game! Do you want to take the buttons off and put them on? There's a puzzle in here I think. Title: How to Care for Snakes Introduction: Snakes are fascinating creatures that can make unique pets. However, they require specific care to ensure their health and well-being. This guide will provide you with comprehensive steps on how to properly care for snakes. Step 1: Choosing Your Snake Before buying a snake, research different species to understand their individual needs regarding habitat, diet, handling, and lifespan. Some suitable beginner snakes include corn snakes, ball pythons, and milk snakes due to their manageable size, easy feeding habits, and docile nature. Key Tip: Never buy a wild-caught snake as they often carry diseases and have poor survival rates in captivity. Always choose a reputable breeder or pet store. Step 2: Setting Up the Habitat Aquariums or specialized reptile enclosures work best for most snakes. The size should be at least twice the length of your snake and one-third its width. Provide hiding places using commercial reptile hides or homemade ones from cardboard boxes or plastic containers. Use substrates like cypress mulch, aspen shavings, or coconut fiber which retain moisture but allow burrowing. Why It Matters: Adequate space and hiding spots reduce stress and mimic natural environments, promoting overall health. Step 3: Regulating Temperature & Humidity Most snakes need a temperature gradient within their enclosure - cooler on one end (70-80 F) and warmer on the other (85-90 F). You can create this by placing a heating pad under half of the tank or using a heat lamp above. Monitor humidity levels; many snakes thrive around 40-60%, although some tropical species may need higher humidity. Importance: Proper temperatures and humidity are essential for digestion, shedding, and immune system function. Step 4: Feeding Time Feed based on your snake's age, size, and species. Young snakes usually eat once a week while adults might only need food every two weeks. Offer pre-killed mice or rats depending on the size of your snake. Avoid overfeeding; obese snakes can develop serious health issues. Relevance: Regular, appropriate meals meet nutritional requirements and support growth and development. Step 5: Handling Guidelines Wait until your new snake is comfortable in its environment before beginning regular handling sessions. Start with short periods (10 minutes), gradually increasing time as your snake becomes more accustomed to being handled. Be gentle and move slowly to avoid frightening your snake. Rationale: Gradual habituation reduces stress and helps build trust between owner and snake, making handling safer and more enjoyable for both parties. Step 6: Cleaning and Maintenance Spot clean daily to remove feces and uneaten food. Weekly cleaning involves removing all contents, disinfecting surfaces, and replacing bedding. Check regularly for signs of parasites such as mites or ticks. Consult a vet if any concerns arise. Purpose: Routine cleaning prevents bacterial growth, ensuring hygienic conditions and reducing risk of disease. Conclusion: Taking proper care of a snake requires knowledge, patience, and commitment. By following these steps, you can establish a rewarding relationship with your scaled companion while providing them with the necessary environment and lifestyle they need to live happily and healthily. In decoherence, an interaction with the field takes the observer into only one determinant constellation of the quantum field, and so all observations align with that new, combined quantum state. Everett's thesis has inspired Many worlds interpretation, whereby within our universe are predicted to be virtually or potentially infinite parallel worlds that are real, yet each a minuscule distance from the other worlds. As each world's waveform is universal-not collapsing-and its mathematical relations are invariant, parallel worlds simply fill the gaps and do not touch. [Nina is still crying. She thought it must be more than anything else she had ever carried. So she got some scales and carefully placed the moon on top.
