[puts a second silver man next to the first on the wand] A WebQuest for 7th Grade Social Studies by Jennifer Bourque and Lisa Cerulli Introduction | Task | Process | Evaluation | Conclusion | Credits | Teacher Page Slavery was one of the major causes of the Civil War. Some slaves were able to escape their bondage through the underground railroad, which was a network of people that worked together to help slaves escape to freedom. However, there were other issues that divided North and South in the 1800s. These issues were cultural, economic, and political in nature. The election of 1860 proved to be the breaking point, and what followed was the "War between the States," or Civil War, which lasted from 1861-1865. Your task is to create a quilt about the events leading up to the Civil War. Read the directions below to create your quilt. Your quilt will be constructed of Nine, 8 x 8 paper squares. Each square will have its own set of directions for completion. When all squares are completed, attach them by using glue, tape, or staples. You may also attach them to a poster board. Other than the center square, the quilt can be arranged in any pattern. Center Square (square 1) - CAUSES OF THE CIVIL WAR In this square you will explain one of the major causes of the Civil War. Use the links to help you explain why this issue was a cause of the Civil War. Choose from the following: Compromise of 1850 Dred Scott v. Sandford Uncle Tom's Cabin John Brown's Raid on Harper's Ferry Square 2 - ELECTION OF 1860 Explain the important issues at stake in this critical election. Use the sites to answer these guiding questions. Write your answer in paragraphs. You may include a picture of the candidates. 1. Who were the candidates, and what were their platforms? 2. Why was the outcome of this election so important? 3. What does "secession" mean? 4. What was the outcome of the election? Square 3 - CHOOSING SIDES After the Election of 1860, South Carolina seceded from the Union. Over the next few months, 10 more states followed. Which states sided with the Union? With the Confederacy? Which slave states remained in the Union? Your teacher will provide you with a U.S. map. Use the links, and textbook page 485, to help you label and color the map to show: 1. Union States and Confederate States 2. Slave States that remained in the Union 3. A timeframe for secession (when did states secede?) Square 4 - UNDERGROUND RAILROAD OVERVIEW Using the sites provided below, you are to write a paragraph summarizing what you have learned about the underground railroad. Be sure to include information about how it got its name, when it was in operation, who was involved, and how the railroad worked. (Remember that the Underground Railroad was a network created to help slaves escape to the North. Secret escape routes was passed on through secret codes used by the "conductors" and by slaves themselves. These codes could be found in slave songs as well as hand sewn quilts. Include some of this information in your summary). Square 5 - UNDERGROUND RAILROAD ROUTES (MAP) Contrary to popular belief, the underground railroad was not one set route. Use the sites below to explore the many routes that escaped slaves took. In this square you will draw a map of an escape route that you may have taken if you were slave trying to reach freedom in the north. Give a brief explanation of why you chose the route that you chose and include why you chose your final destination. Square 6 - JOURNAL ENTRY OF AN ESCAPED SLAVE In this square you will write a journal entry depicting what it would have been like to be an escaped slave traveling on the underground railroad. Use what you already know about the underground railroad to make your journal entry realistic. Be sure to include what you see, who you meet, and how you feel. The information should be based on the route that you have chosen to take (square 3). The site below provides excerpts from journals of slaves, railroad conductors, and abolitionists to help guide you. Square 7 - SIGNIFICANT FIGURES IN THE CIVIL WAR PERIOD Choose one of the famous people from the Civil War period. Use the link provided to research that person. On this square, you will include a picture of this person which can be downloaded from the internet. You must write a brief biography of this person. Be sure to include his/her role in the Civil War. Squares 8 and 9 - ADVANTAGES AND DISADVANTAGES How prepared was the North and the South to fight the war? Each side had advantages and disadvantages at the beginning of the Civil War. Square 8 should focus on the North and square 9 should focus on the South. Explain the advantages and disadvantages of each side on these two squares. Your teacher will provide you with the rubric. You will receive a timeline for completing each of the squares from your teacher. Congratulations!! If you have made it to this point, you have successfully navigated your way through the WebQuest. You have gained a much broader understanding of the causes of the Civil War and of those who were involved. You've probably heard the term major scale many times. Many of you play scales in some shape or form and may not even realize it. Although scales were mentioned earlier, now it's time to look at them more closely. For starters, a scale is a grouping of notes that makes a key. Most of the scales you will encounter have seven different pitches (but a total of eight notes, including the repeated octave). Some scales contain more than seven notes, and some contain fewer. A scale is defined as a series of eight notes (seven different pitches) that start and end on the same note, which is also called the root. The root names the scale. If a scale starts and ends on C, the root is C and the scale's name is the C something (major, minor, etc.) scale. What that something is depends on its intervallic formula. Aren't you glad you know a thing or two about intervals? Since this chapter focuses on major scales, take a look at a very basic C major scale in FIGURE 3.1. FIGURE 3.1 The C Major Scale As you can see, the scale starts and ends on C and progresses up every note in order. Since C major contains no sharps and no flats, it's an easy scale to understand and remember. On the piano, it's simply all the white notes. The C scale contains seven different notes: C, D, E, F, G, A, and B. Although the last C isn't counted because it's a repeated note, the major scale has eight notes in total. What makes this a major scale is not the fact that it uses the notes C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C. That only tells you that it's one particular key. Music theory looks for larger-scale ideas and tries to tie them together. What makes that scale a major scale are the intervals between the notes. If you look at the distance from each note in the scale to the next, you see a pattern of half or whole steps in a series. This series, which you can also call a formula, is exactly what you are going to learn about now. FIGURE 3.2 shows the C major scale with the intervals defined. FIGURE 3.2 Intervals of the C Major Scale What you come up with is the interval series of Whole, Whole, Half, Whole, Whole, Whole, Half, or WWHWWWH. This is what makes one scale different from any other: the formula of the intervals. As long as that interval formula is present, you have a major scale. It's a perfect system, because you can start on any of the twelve chromatic notes and follow these rules: Pick a root note. Progress up seven notes until you reach the octave. Use the formula of WWHWWWH between your notes to ensure that you have the correct spelling. Make sure that you use any letter only once before the octave. If you can follow these rules, you can spell any scale. Here's how. "My! It is very soft. The carbon atoms in it make rings, which are on top of each other and slide very easily. Diamonds are the hardest natural mineral. Fullerenes are a "soccer ball" shape of carbon. They are mostly of interest to science. A special, man-made, tube-shaped allotrope of carbon is the carbon nanotube. Carbon nanotubes are very hard, so they might be used in armor. Nanotubes might be useful in nanotechnology. [1.6 sec; then S and D overlap] Title: How to Complain Effectively and Get Results Introduction: While no one enjoys complaining, there are times when voicing your concerns is necessary to address issues and bring about positive change. However, effective complaining requires tact, assertiveness, and strategic planning. This guide will walk you through the process of composing a well-crafted complaint that can lead to satisfactory results. Remember, the goal is not just to vent but to resolve the problem at hand. Step 1: Identify the Issue Before lodging a complaint, make sure you have a clear understanding of what went wrong and why it bothers you. Articulating the issue precisely allows you to present a strong case and prevents misunderstandings during discussions with customer service representatives or other parties involved. Key Tip: Focus on specific events rather than generalizations. For example, instead of saying "Your product is terrible," say something like "I received a damaged item in my recent order." Step 2: Gather Documentation Collect all relevant information related to the incident, such as receipts, invoices, photographs, emails, or chat logs. Having concrete evidence strengthens your claim and demonstrates your preparedness. It also shows the recipient that you take the matter seriously and expect them to do the same. Guideline: Keep your documentation organized by creating a folder or binder where you store everything related to the issue. This makes it easier to access and share the required documents when needed. Step 3: Determine Your Desired Outcome that's a silly fish 'cause he has his hat on. Title: How to Activate Google Voice Typing on Your iPhone or iPad Google's voice typing feature is an efficient and convenient way to dictate text messages, emails, or search queries without having to manually type them out. Although this feature was initially exclusive to Android devices, it has since been made available for iOS users as well. This tutorial will guide you through enabling and using Google Voice Typing on your iPhone or iPad with clear and concise steps. **Step 1:** Install the latest version of Google Docs or Gboard (the Google Keyboard) from the App Store. - Google Docs offers robust word processing capabilities while providing access to the voice typing tool. Gboard integrates directly into your keyboard experience across various apps. Choose either option based on your preference. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Make sure that both your device and the app are up-to-date to ensure compatibility and optimal performance. - If installing Gboard, remember that it replaces your default keyboard, meaning you can utilize its features within any application where inputting text is required. **Step 2:** Enable Microphone Access for the Chosen Application (Google Docs or Gboard). - Navigate to Settings > Privacy > Microphone. Toggle ON the switch next to the chosen app (Google Docs or Gboard). **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Granting microphone permissions allows these applications to recognize spoken words and convert them into written content. **Step 3a:** Using Google Docs: 1. Open the Google Docs app and create a new document or open an existing one. 2. Tap the "+" icon at the bottom right corner to add a blank page if needed. 3. Place your cursor where you want to insert speech-typed text. 4. Tap the three vertical dots located at the top right corner and select "Voice typing." **Step 3b:** Using Gboard: 1. Launch any app requiring text input (e.g., Messages, Notes, etc.). 2. Tap the text field to activate the keyboard. 3. Long press the mic icon until additional options appear and then tap the "Voice Input" button. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always position yourself in a quiet environment with minimal background noise for better accuracy during voice recognition. - Speak clearly and naturally, maintaining a steady pace throughout the process. **Step 4:** Begin Dictating Text. - Start speaking once prompted by the visual cues provided by the respective apps (red lines moving back and forth in Google Docs; a blue bar filling up in Gboard). **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Pause briefly between sentences to allow proper sentence structure formation. - Use punctuation commands such as "period," "comma," or "question mark" when necessary during dictation. For example, say "Period" instead of just stopping after a statement. **Step 5:** Review and Edit Transcribed Text. - After finishing your dictation, review the transcribed text for any errors or missing elements. - Correct mistakes manually and make adjustments accordingly before finalizing the document or sending the message. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - The more familiar you become with Google's voice typing, the fewer corrections you may need to perform over time. By following these straightforward steps, activating and utilizing Google Voice Typing on your iPhone or iPad becomes an effortless task, enhancing productivity and convenience for everyday tasks involving text entry. " What can "The Three Little Pigs" teach us about decision making? One pig wanted to hurry and build his house so he could go out and play. One pig spent a little more time building his house, but he still skimped on quality so he could go and play. When the wolf came, down went the houses! The cost of doing quick work hardly matched the benefit of being able to play! In this lesson, students will Identify the costs and benefits of decisions. A cost is what you give up when you decide to do something. A benefit is something that satisfies your wants. When making a decision, students should consider the costs and the benefits of that decision. Use this link to hear a story about "The Three Little Pigs." But what did the first little pig lose? Yes, he lost his house. He made a decision and the COST of that decision was that the wolf could blow his house down. The second little pig made a decision, too. He decided to build his house out of wood. What was the BENEFIT of that decision? What did he gain? Yes, he gained a nice wooden house and a few days to play. When the second little pig made the decision to build his house out of wood, there was also a COST. What was the COST? What did he give up? The third little pig built his house out of bricks. He made the decision to build his house out of bricks and he had a COST and a BENEFIT, too. When he built his house out of bricks, what was his COST? What did he have to give up that the other pigs got to do? When he built his house out of bricks, what was his BENEFIT? The first little pig built his house out of straw. His benefit was that he got to go out and play. His cost was that he lost his house. Do you think he would make a better decision now if he thought about his cost and benefit? The second little pig built his house out of wood. His benefit was that he got a nice house and he got to go out and play a few extra days. His cost was that he lost his house. Do you think he would make a better decision now if he thought about his cost and benefit? We all like to go and play. This story teaches us that we need to make good decisions. Find Yoshi in Super Mario 64: A Comprehensive Guide Step 1: Starting the Game To begin your quest to find Yoshi in Super Mario 64, you'll first need to start the game by selecting it from your Nintendo 64 menu or emulator. Once the game has loaded, select "Start" to proceed to the main menu. From here, choose "1 Player Game" to start playing. Step 2: Selecting a File In Super Mario 64, up to four different save files can be created, allowing multiple players to have their own progress saved. If this is your first time playing, there will only be one file available. Choose this file by pressing the corresponding button (A) and move onto the next step. Step 3: Entering the Castle You will now arrive at Princess Peach's castle. Your objective is to collect Power Stars, which are hidden throughout various levels within the castle grounds. However, before we dive into those adventures, let us focus on finding our beloved dinosaur friend, Yoshi! To do so, you must first enter the castle through its large entrance door. Walk towards the doorway and press the 'A' button when prompted to open the doors. Proceed inside. Step 4: Finding the Painting Once inside the castle, make your way to the center room where you see three paintings hanging on the wall - two smaller ones on either side and a larger one above them. You want to approach the bigger painting titled "Jolly Roger Bay." Stand directly in front of it and press the 'A' button to jump into the painted world. Step 5: Exploring Jolly Roger Bay Upon entering Jolly Roger Bay, take note of your surroundings. This underwater level requires swimming skills using the control stick for directional movement and the 'A' button to swim forward. The goal here isn't just to explore but also locate an island with a red flag located straight ahead from the starting point. Swim towards it while avoiding obstacles like eels and Cheep-Cheeps along the way. Step 6: Reaching the Island After reaching the island, look around for a small slope leading downwards. At the bottom of this slope, you should notice another painting similar to the one back in Peach's castle. Approach this painting and interact with it ('A' button). Step 7: Meeting Yoshi As soon as you touch the painting, congratulations! You've found Yoshi! Interacting with the painting brings out everyone's favorite green dino who then becomes playable. Now that you know how to find Yoshi, feel free to switch between controlling Mario and Yoshi whenever you revisit Jolly Roger Bay. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Save often: Always remember to save after collecting power stars or making significant progress. It saves you from losing hard work if something unexpected happens. - Practice makes perfect: Getting used to controlling both Mario and Yoshi takes some practice, especially considering their unique abilities. Spend time learning these controls to maximize enjoyment and efficiency during gameplay. - Observe environments closely: Many secrets lie tucked away in corners or disguised subtly amidst decorative elements. By observing carefully, you might discover shortcuts, extra lives, or other helpful items. - Utilize guidebooks or online resources: While following this tutorial provides substantial assistance, supplementary materials offer additional insights, strategies, and tips for navigating complex areas or challenges. They can help streamline game completion and enhance overall experience. What do you want to do with this? Title: How to Perform a Pas de Chat in Ballet Pas de chat is a fundamental leap in ballet that involves jumping from one foot to another while kicking out the free leg in mid-air. This movement requires strength, flexibility, balance, and precision. With practice, you can master this elegant and dynamic jump. Here's a step-by-step guide to performing a pas de chat: **Step 1: Preparation** Start by standing at the barre or with a partner holding your hands for support. Place your feet in first position (heels touching, toes turned out). Engage your core muscles and maintain a straight posture. Keep your arms rounded in second position (arms extended to the sides, elbows slightly bent). *Key tip*: Proper alignment and muscle engagement are crucial for executing clean jumps and preventing injuries. **Step 2: Plie and Releve** Bend your knees into a pli (a slight squat) while maintaining turnout in your legs. As you rise onto the balls of your feet (demi-pointe), simultaneously lift your heels off the ground and prepare to spring upward. *Guideline*: The smoother the transition between pli and relev , the more power and control you will have during the actual leap. **Step 3: First Jump - Left Leg Extension** Push off the floor using both feet, launching yourself airborne. Simultaneously extend your left leg forward and upwards, keeping it turned out. Your right knee should be tucked close to your chest. Aim for a 90-degree angle with your left leg. *Key tip*: Visualize lifting your body toward the ceiling rather than leaping horizontally. This mental image will help improve your elevation and overall technique. **Step 4: Mid-Air Adjustments** As you reach the peak of your jump, quickly adjust your body position if necessary. Make sure your hips remain level, and avoid tilting them to either side. Also, ensure your supporting leg (right leg) remains bent throughout the entire leap. *Guideline*: Practicing in front of a mirror can help develop spatial awareness and make mid-air corrections easier over time. **Step 5: Second Jump - Right Leg Extension** Upon landing briefly on the ball of your left foot, immediately push off again, switching the positions of your legs. Extend your right leg forward and upwards as you tuck your left knee close to your chest. Maintain proper turnout and hip alignment. *Key tip*: Focus on quickness and efficiency when changing leg positions. Speed and fluidity contribute significantly to the gracefulness of the pas de chat. **Step 6: Landing** Gently lower your extended leg back down towards the floor as you descend. Absorb the impact of the landing by bending your knees and returning to a demi-pli position. Remember to keep your weight centered over your feet and avoid leaning backward or forward. *Guideline*: Practice controlled landings to minimize joint stress and reduce the risk of injury. **Step 7: Recovery** After completing the pas de chat, return to the starting position - feet together in first position, arms rounded in second position. Take deep breaths and reset your focus before attempting another repetition. *Key tip*: Consistent practice and patience are essential for improving your pas de chat. Over time, you'll notice increased strength, flexibility, and coordination. By following these steps and incorporating regular practice, you'll soon be able to execute beautiful pas de chats like a true ballet dancer! No he can't pop their balloons any more like that. Choose Books at the Right Reading Levels "Just-right" books help your child find the right reading levels and make reading more fun. Have your children try the "five-finger test" to select a book at their reading levels: - Choose a book you would like to read. - Look at a page in the middle. - Put one finger down for every word you can't read or don't understand. If you have five fingers down, you will need to try different reading levels. - If you have fewer than five fingers down, the book is "just right"! Read it. Your child may want to read a book that is "too hard" based on this test. That's okay. Just make it a book that you read aloud to her, or that you read together (perhaps alternating pages or chapters; or she reads, but you sit alongside to answer questions or help her with challenging words). This will help your child climb to more advanced reading levels! Title: How to Add Your Location on Twitter Introduction: In this comprehensive guide, we will walk you through the process of adding your location on Twitter. By doing so, you can share your current whereabouts with your followers, engage more effectively with local communities, and potentially increase visibility for your tweets. This feature is particularly useful when attending events or visiting new places, as it allows others to join conversations specific to those locations. Before diving into the steps, let's clarify some important aspects regarding privacy and accuracy. Privacy Considerations: Twitter provides various options for controlling the visibility of your location data. You may choose to make your location visible only to yourself, to your followers, or to everyone. Be aware that sharing your precise location publicly could have potential security risks; therefore, consider carefully whom you want to grant access to this information. Additionally, ensure that your device settings are configured appropriately, especially if you wish to limit location-sharing capabilities outside the Twitter platform. Accuracy Guidelines: When adding a location to your tweet, Twitter uses Foursquare's database to provide suggestions based on your IP address or GPS coordinates. While most establishments should be accurately represented, there might be instances where the suggested location isn't entirely accurate. Always double-check the provided options before selecting one, and feel free to report any discrepancies directly to Foursquare. Step 1: Compose a New Tweet To begin, log in to your Twitter account using either the web version (twitter.com) or the mobile app. Once logged in, click on the blue feather icon located at the top right corner of the screen (web) or bottom center part of the interface (mobile). This action will prompt you to compose a new tweet. Step 2: Enable Location Services After writing your message, look for the "location" button - usually depicted by a small compass symbol - within the composer window. On desktop versions, this option resides below your text box adjacent to other features like poll creation and GIF insertion. For mobile users, find the paper plane icon next to the camera icon; tap and hold it until additional functions appear. Then, select the location pin. Before proceeding further, ensure that your device has permission to share its location data with Twitter. If necessary, enable this functionality via your browser settings (desktop) or general phone settings (mobile), then return to the composer window. Step 3: Choose an Appropriate Location Upon clicking the location button, Twitter will generate a list of nearby places based on your current position. Scroll through the options and select the most suitable choice from the presented alternatives. Keep in mind our earlier discussion about accuracy and privacy while making your selection. Step 4: Send Your Tweet Once you've chosen your preferred location, continue composing your tweet if needed, and hit the "Tweet" button once ready. Congratulations! You have successfully added your location to a tweet. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Remember that enabling location services may impact battery life due to constant background tracking. Disable these features when they aren't required. * Double-check your selected location before sending your tweet, ensuring it aligns with your intended audience and purpose. * Regularly review your privacy settings related to location sharing across all platforms, including Twitter, considering both short-term convenience and long-term safety implications. By following this extensive guide, you now possess the knowledge to confidently add your location on Twitter, fostering deeper engagement with local communities and enhancing overall user experience. Happy tweeting! 'Cause this all came on post bottle days. Once there was a little bunny. He loved to bounce around! He found a feather and picked it up. The feather was very flexible. Cognitive science studies how people make their ideas and what makes thoughts logical. It is often seen as the result of several different scientific fields working together. These fields are psychology (a study of the mind), neuroscience (biological study of the brain), computer science (the creation of programs and computers), and linguistics (the study of language). It does not refer to the sum of all these disciplines. It refers to their intersection on specific problems. No you can't draw if you put water in the cup. Are you saying hi to the baby? What's the difference? These terms can be very confusing. But learning to measure serving sizes can help you figure out how many carbohydrates ("carbs") and other foods you eat each day. They are also powerful tools for managing your weight. Many different words are used to describe amounts of food. If your healthcare provider uses a term you're not sure of, don't be afraid to ask. It helps to know the difference between servings and portions. A serving size is a fixed size. Food producers use this term to describe their products. For example, the label on a cereal box could say that 1 cup of dry cereal = 1 serving. A portion (also called a "helping") is how much you eat or how much you put on your plate at a meal. For example, you might eat 2 cups of cereal at breakfast. The portion you choose to eat (such as 2 cups of cereal) may be more than one serving as listed on the food label (such as 1 cup of cereal). That's why it helps to measure or weigh the food you eat. Because the food label values are based on servings, you'll need to know how many servings you eat at one sitting. When you're planning for a snack or a meal, keep servings in mind. If you don't have measuring cups or a scale handy, there are ways to "eyeball" serving sizes. If your weight is a concern, reducing your portions can help. You can eat more than one serving of a food at once. But to keep from eating too much at one meal, learn how to manage your portions. A portion is the amount of each type of food on your plate. See the plate diagram for an example of balanced portions. oh, you are going to put it in with the other ones. Day after day rolled away, and tedious as the present moment appeared, they passed in such an unvaried tenor, Maria was surprised to find that she had already been six weeks buried alive, and yet had such faint hopes of effecting her enlargement. She was, earnestly as she had sought for employment, now angry with herself for having been amused by writing her narrative; and grieved to think that she had for an instant thought of any thing, but contriving to escape. Jemima had evidently pleasure in her society: still, though she often left her with a glow of kindness, she returned with the same chilling air; and, when her heart appeared for a moment to open, some suggestion of reason forcibly closed it, before she could give utterance to the confidence Maria's conversation inspired. Discouraged by these changes, Maria relapsed into despondency, when she was cheered by the alacrity with which Jemima brought her a fresh parcel of books; assuring her, that she had taken some pains to obtain them from one of the keepers, who attended a gentleman confined in the opposite corner of the gallery. The Value of a Reputation for Integrity_ After the Libyan expedition Gracchus was elected quaestor, and it was his lot to serve against the Numantines under the Consul Gaius Mancinus, who had some good qualities, but was the most unfortunate of Roman generals. Thus unexpected situations and reverses in the field brought more clearly into light, not only the ability and courage of Tiberius, but--what was more remarkable--his respect and regard for his superior, who was so crushed by disaster that he hardly knew whether he was in command or not. After some decisive defeats Mancinus left his camp and attempted to retire by night, but the Numantines, being aware of his movements, at once occupied the camp, fell upon his troops as they fled, made havoc of the rear, and drove the whole army on to difficult ground, from which it was impossible to escape. You could XXX the floors right dere. ### Understanding Secret Keys and Hash Functions Hello young cryptographers! Today, let's learn about two important concepts in the world of coding and secrets: Pseudo Random Functions (PRF) and Hash Functions. These are special tools that people who study codes and secrets use to keep information safe. #### Pseudo Random Functions (PRF): Imagine you have a magical box that takes two inputs - a secret code (also called a "key") and any other random thing like a word or a picture. This magical box then changes your random thing into something new based on the secret code. Even if someone else finds out what was inside your box, without knowing the exact secret code, it would be impossible for them to predict what will come out next time because the magic box uses the secret code to create a brand new, unique outcome each time! That's exactly what a Pseudo Random Function does; it helps us create unpredictable results with our secret keys. #### Hash Functions: Now imagine another type of magical box, but this one doesn't need a secret code. You just put something in, say your favorite book title, and it always gives you back a fixed size jumble of letters and numbers which looks completely different every time you change even a single letter in the original book title. No matter how many times you try, this magical box always returns a fixed size response based on whatever you gave it. It also makes sure nothing gets changed while being transferred. Such boxes are known as Hash Functions. They ensure data integrity by generating consistent outputs for identical inputs, allowing easy verification. So, remember: * A PRF requires a secret key to generate unique outcomes, making it perfect for creating shared secrets. * A Hash Function transforms any input into a fixed size output without needing a secret key, ensuring consistency and security during transfers. While both PRF and Hash Functions seem similar, their applications differ significantly due to their fundamental differences. Now go ahead and explore more about these fascinating concepts! France ( or ; ), officially the French Republic (, ), is a country whose metropolitan territory is in Western Europe and that also includes various overseas islands and territories in other continents. Metropolitan France extends from the Mediterranean Sea to the English Channel and the North Sea, and from the Rhine to the Atlantic Ocean. It is often referred to as "L'Hexagone" ("The Hexagon") because of the shape of its territory. Every week at this time, we tell the story of a man or a woman who played an important part in the history of the United States. Today, Larry West and Warren Scheer begin the story of Chief Joseph of the Nez Perce Indians. He is remembered as a hero of all American Indian people. LARRY WEST: Chief Joseph loved peace. But he was forced to lead his people in war. He loved freedom and his land. But he lost both. Chief Joseph's story begins with his people and his land. WARREN SCHEER: An old man looks out at a green valley. Tall dark mountains stand above it. Snow covers the mountain tops. In the clear water of a lake dance the dark shapes of the mountains. The old man's name is Tuekakas. White men call him old Joseph. The Wallowa Valley is the old man's home -- and the home of the Nez Perce people -- for as long as anyone can remember. It lies in the northwestern part of the United States. Today, the land is part of the states of Idaho, Oregon and Washington. No one knows exactly when the Nez Perce first came to the valley. From earliest times, the people hunted and raised cattle there. They kept horses, the kind called Appaloosas. LARRY WEST: The Nez Perce did not own the land. They had not bought it from anyone else. They possessed no documents of ownership. But they believed the land was theirs, simply because that was where they lived. For almost seventy years, the Nez Perce showed friendship to the white farmers, churchmen and explorers who came to their land. Old Joseph, the chief, had been a friend to the white men. But in eighteen seventy-one, as he looked out across the valley, he could see a time of trouble coming. White people had discovered gold in mountains on Nez Perce land. More and more white farmers were asking the United States government to open the land for development. To do that, the Indians had to be moved. The government usually offered the Indians money or gifts to leave the area. Different deals were made with different Indian groups. WARREN SCHEER: Several years before, the white governor of the territory met with old Joseph. He asked the chief to sign a treaty. The governor said he wanted the land divided so the Indians and white men could live separately. "If the two groups are to live in peace," the governor said, "it is necessary for the Indians to have a country set apart for them. And in that country they must stay." Old Joseph was furious. "Take away your paper," he said. "I will not touch it with my hand." Other Nez Perce chiefs, however -- beyond the valley -- signed treaties to give up their lands. Those chiefs and their people became Christians. They cut their hair short. They forgot the ways of their tribe. Old Joseph's people did not forget. They wore their hair long. And they loved the land. LARRY WEST: Old Joseph had been chief for many years. Now he was dying. He called for his first son. The son, like the father, was named Joseph. Old Joseph spoke. His voice was the voice of a dying man. But his words were the words of a strong, proud spirit: "My son," the old man said, "when I am gone you will be chief of these people. They look to you to guide them. Always remember that your father never gave up his land. WARREN SCHEER: Young Joseph was thirty-one years old when his father died. His people called him Heinmot Tooyalaket. Those were the words the Nez Perce used to describe the noise that lightning makes in the mountains. The young man had a wide face. His hair was tied on both sides of his head and hung down on his chest like long, heavy ropes. He wore chains of seashells around his neck. Small pieces of colored glass shone brightly on his clothing. Already, the Nez Perce knew him for his good judgment, his kindness, and his ability with words. And now they would know him as their leader -- Chief Joseph. Chief Joseph remembered his dying father's words. He said: "This land has always belonged to my people. We will defend this land as long as Indian blood warms the hearts of our men." LARRY WEST: In eighteen seventy-three, Chief Joseph sent a message to the President of the United States, Ulysses Grant. He asked that no more white persons be permitted to live in the Wallowa Valley. President Grant agreed. But two years later, under pressure from farmers and gold-hunters, the president broke his promise. More white people came. Some stole cattle and horses. Some insulted the Indians. Always, Chief Joseph kept the peace. In May, eighteen seventy-seven, the government told General Oliver Howard to meet with the Nez Perce chiefs. He was ordered to tell the Indians that they must leave their land. The government had a place in Idaho for all Nez Perce people. It was called the Lapwai Reservation. General Howard did not like his orders. To his friends he said it was a great mistake to take the valley from Joseph. But the general had spent many years in the army. He obeyed his orders. To the Nez Perce chiefs he said: "I stand here for the president. My orders are clear and must be obeyed. You have thirty days to leave the valley. If you delay even one day," General Howard said, "the soldiers will force you to the reservation. And all your cattle and horses will fall into the hands of the white men." WARREN SCHEER: The chiefs had a difficult choice. They could leave. Or they could fight. Joseph and the other chiefs had only ninety warriors. They knew they could not defend the valley with such a small fighting force. Chief Joseph said: "I have carried a heavy load on my back ever since I was a boy. I learned then that we were but few, while the white men were many, and that we could not defeat them. LARRY WEST: Some of the Nez Perce chiefs wanted to stay and fight. They were willing to try, even if there was little chance of winning. But Joseph said, "To protect my people from war, I will give up my country. I will give up everything." So the Nez Perce prepared to leave the Wallowa Valley. To get to the reservation in time, they had to leave behind many of the things they owned. They took some cattle and horses, and what food and possessions they could carry. Chief Joseph had promised them peace. But peace would not follow them. That will be our story next week. SHEP O'NEAL: You have been listening to the Special English program, People in America. Your narrators were Larry West and Warren Scheer. Our program was written by Barbara Dash. The Voice of America invites you to listen again next week at this time, when we will complete the story of Chief Joseph of the Nez Perce Indians. Hey tell me what he's doing in there. Tom liked to gather bugs and put them in a jar. "Look, mom, I found a beetle!" Tom said, showing his jar. "It is big and shiny and has spots." Mom smiled and said, "That is a nice beetle, Tom. But do you think the beetle is happy in the jar?" Tom thought for a moment. He looked at the beetle, who was crawling and tapping on the glass. He said, "No, I think the beetle is sad. He wants to be free and play with his friends." Mom nodded and said, "That is right, Tom. Bugs are living things, just like us. They have feelings and needs. They like to explore and have fun. How would you feel if someone put you in a jar?" Tom said, "I would feel scared and lonely and bored. I would not like that at all." Mom said, "Then you should let the beetle go, Tom. He will be happier in his home, and you can still watch him from a distance. Maybe you can find another way to enjoy bugs, like drawing them or reading about them." Tom agreed and opened the jar. He gently placed the beetle on a leaf and said, "Bye, beetle. I am sorry I made you sad. You are adorable and I hope you have a good day." The beetle waved his antennae and scurried away. Tom felt happy and proud. He learned to respect and care for bugs. He gathered his crayons and paper and started to draw the beetle and his friends. Lily joined him and they had fun together. The following are the guidelines for spelling homework: Monday - Write the words two times each. Tuesday - Choose one activity from Spelling Menu A Pictures - Pick five words from the spelling list and draw a picture for each word. Write the spelling words under your drawings. Add It Up - Pick five words from the spelling list. Use the code below to write an addition fact and find out how much each word is worth. Example: cat 5 + 1 + 2 = 8 a = 1 f = 5 k = 5 p = 10 u = 5 b = 2 g = 1 l = 10 q = 5 v = 1 c = 5 h = 2 m = 5 r = 10 w = 2 d = 1 i = 5 n = 10 s = 1 x = 5 e = 2 j = 10 o = 5 t = 2 y = 1 z = 2 Triangle Words - Pick five words. Write one letter for a word at a time until the entire word is written. Wednesday - Choose one activity from Spelling Menu B Sentences - Pick three words from the spelling list and write a sentence using each word. Underline the spelling word in each sentence. ABC Order - Rewrite the spelling list in alphabetical order. Story - Write a story using five or more of the spelling words. Underline the spelling words in the story. Thursday - Practice Test Friday - Sign the Spelling test in the green booklet and return it on Monday. That night he had a stomach ache. Title: How to Display Contacts' Last Name First on an iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing your contact display settings on your iPhone so that contacts' last names are displayed before their first names. This can be particularly useful if you prefer this format or need it for organizational purposes. We will break down the steps into simple and easy-to-follow parts with clear explanations for each action. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Make sure you have the latest iOS version installed on your device. You can check for updates by going to Settings > General > Software Update. Installing any available update may include improvements to contact management features. 2. Familiarize yourself with the terms used throughout this tutorial (e.g., 'Phone app', 'Contacts app'). 3. Remember to save changes after editing contact information. 4. If possible, practice these steps using a test contact first to avoid making unintended modifications to important contact data. 5. Note that some third-party apps might override system settings; thus, results may vary depending upon the specific app being utilized. Step 1: Open the Contacts App The Contacts app is preinstalled on every iPhone and serves as the central hub for managing all your phonebook entries. To begin, locate the app on one of your home screens or within the alphabetical list of apps in the App Library (swipe right from your main screen). Tap on its icon to open it. Step 2: Navigate to Your Contact List Once inside the Contacts app, ensure you're viewing your entire contact list rather than individual contact details. The default view should show your contacts listed alphabetically based on their first names. Step 3: Select a Test Contact For safety reasons, we recommend selecting a test contact to experiment with during this tutorial. Choose someone whose contact info won't cause significant issues if accidentally modified. Step 4: Edit the Selected Contact Tap on the test contact's name to access their detail page. Then hit 'Edit' located at the top-right corner of the screen. This will allow you to modify the existing entry. Step 5: Rearrange Names Under "First" and "Last," rearrange the order of the names so that the last name appears first followed by a comma and then the first name. For example, instead of "John Smith," input "Smith, John." Step 6: Save Changes After reordering the names, make sure to tap 'Done' at the top-right corner of the screen to save your changes. Confirm that the new formatting displays correctly in both the Contacts app and other relevant applications like Messages and Phone. Step 7: Apply New Naming Convention Across All Contacts Unfortunately, there isn't a direct way to change the naming convention for multiple contacts simultaneously within Apple's native Contacts app. However, if most or all of your contacts require such modification, consider syncing your contacts with iCloud and applying the necessary adjustments via a computer. Alternatively, explore third-party contact management tools offering batch edits. Conclusion: By following these steps, you can successfully set up your iPhone to display your contacts' last names first. Although time-consuming when dealing with numerous entries, taking precautions and double-checking ensures accuracy and minimizes potential errors. Happy organizing! Title: How to Be a Potato for Halloween Halloween is an exciting time of year when you can dress up as your favorite character or object. If you're looking for something unique, fun, and simple, consider being a potato! This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to transform into this humble yet beloved vegetable. **Step 1: Gather Your Materials** ============================= To become a convincing potato, you'll need the following items: * A large cardboard box (big enough to fit your body) * Brown paint or spray paint * Paintbrushes * Scissors or a craft knife * String or yarn * Glue gun and glue sticks * Newspaper (for stuffing) * Potato-inspired clothing (optional) **Step 2: Prepare the Box** ========================== Start by cutting off the flaps at the top of the box so that they don't obstruct your head when you wear it. Then, using scissors or a craft knife, cut out holes for your arms and legs. Make sure these openings are wide enough for comfortable movement but not too big, as this could compromise the structural integrity of the box. **Step 3: Apply Brown Paint** ============================ Paint the entire exterior of the box brown using either spray paint or regular paint applied with a brush. Multiple thin coats may be necessary for even coverage. Allow adequate drying time between coats. Key Tip: To add texture and make your costume look more authentic, crumple pieces of newspaper into balls and stick them onto the painted surface using a hot glue gun. Once dry, paint over them with the same brown shade. **Step 4: Create Straps** ======================= Cut four equal lengths of string or yarn, approximately twice the width of your shoulders plus some extra length for tying knots. Attach one end of each strap inside the box near where your armpits would be, securing them with hot glue. After the glue has dried, try on the box and adjust the straps' lengths accordingly before tying them around your upper body. **Step 5: Dress Up** =================== For added effect, wear earthy-toned clothes such as jeans, khakis, or browns. You might also find a hat resembling a leaf or vine to complete the look. However, remember that the main attraction should still be your cardboard potato bodysuit. **Step 6: Accessorize** ===================== Consider adding additional details like fake leaves, vines, or even a stem made from construction paper or pipe cleaners attached to the top of your head or the box itself. These finishing touches will enhance the realism of your potato costume. **Step 7: Practice Poses** ======================== Since potatoes aren't exactly known for their mobility, practice striking funny poses that emphasize the stationary nature of a sack of spuds. Squat down low, lean against walls, or balance precariously on one foot - whatever makes you feel most tuber-like! By following these steps, you'll have successfully transformed yourself into a delightful display of edible elegance just in time for Halloween festivities. Happy haunting! "No--not very," she said. Title: Understanding Y-Intercept with a Special Parabola Hello young mathematicians! Today, we will learn about the "y-intercept" using a special parabola. Have you ever seen a parabola before? It looks like a curvy letter U! Imagine drawing a parabola on your paper; it could be wide or narrow, but let's think of one that opens downwards like a frowny face . Now, imagine putting your pencil through the center of the parabola and pushing it all the way down until it touches the ground - that's where our y-intercept lives! Y-intercept is just a fancy name for the point where the graph (our frowny face parabola) crosses the vertical line called the "y-axis." The x-coordinate of any y-intercept will always be zero because it's directly below or above the origin (the middle point of the x and y axis). So, we really care about finding out the y-value. Now, let's take a peek at our given problem: -2y = 3x - 3 Don't panic! We don't need to worry about those big words surrounding it yet. Our goal here is to find the y-intercept, which means setting x equal to zero and solving for y. This will tell us exactly where our curve kisses the y-axis. Let's give it a try! Step 1: Replace x with 0 in the equation: -2y = 3(0) - 3 Step 2: Calculate the result: -2y = -3 Step 3: Divide both sides by -2 to isolate y: y = (-3) / (-2) Step 4: Simplify: y = 1.5 So, the y-intercept for our unique parabola is (0, 1.5), or you can say, "zero points fifteen" when talking to your friends! Great job discovering the secret hiding place of the y-intercept within the world of graphs! Keep practicing these skills, as understanding them now will make more complex math problems seem easier later on. The first people to live in the country were Indigenous Australians but most of them died from smallpox during colonization. Beer is made by adding warm water to malted barley and other grains. The enzymes in the barley change the malted barley and other grains into simple sugars. This is called the mash. The water is then sparged (drained) from the grain. The water is now called wort. The wort is boiled and hops are added. Hops provide flavour and preserve the beer. After boiling the wort is cooled and yeast is added. The yeast turns the sugars into alcohol and the wort into beer. Well I'm waiting for Robin to be done. You'd like to drink out of a cup, wouldn't you? [as he pushes himself away from table] How To Read Frets On A Guitar Wondering how to read frets on a guitar? You might think that a great deal of music theory knowledge is necessary to read frets on a guitar, and although some sort of music background is helpful, it is definitely not a necessity. It's useful to know the names of the notes themselves: A, B, C, D, E, F, G, and all of the sharps and flats, but aside from that learning how to read frets on a guitar is just about memory and practice. The great thing about learning how to read frets is that you can use the skill on any type of guitar you might find. Acoustic, electric, bass guitars, and even many other fretted instruments can all be figured out using the exact same knowledge of how to read frets on a guitar. - Identify the string's "open" pitch. Before you begin learning all about the fretboard, it's important to know what each string's "open" pitch is. That is, what note will you hear when you pluck the string without pressing it down on the guitar neck. For a typical guitar in standard tuning, the pitches of the six strings are E, A, D, G, B, and E again, starting from the top string and going to the string closest to the ground. - Count the number of frets. Each fret represents a half step change in pitch. So the first fret of the sixth string on a guitar (it's open pitch is E) is F. The second fret of the sixth string is F#. The third fret is G. This continues all the way up the string. The sixth string becomes E again at the twelfth fret, so twelve frets represents one octave. - Repeat for all strings. No matter what string you choose, each fret represents a change of one half step. The second string of a guitar has an open pitch of B. It's first fret is a C, the second is a C# and the third is a D. - Memorize and practice. Find a diagram that will help you learn how to read frets on the guitar, and memorize where each note is. Practice your memorization of this diagram for a small amount of time every day, and before you know it, you'll be able to point out any string at any fret, and instantly know what note will play. Now you truly know how to read frets on a guitar. Michelle said you are the best student she has in her class. Um the books at this point seem to not interest him as much as all the other toys. He would sit and weep until his tears filled a river. One day a little girl walked by the river and heard the tiger. She was curious, so she bravely stepped into the deep woods. When the little girl got closer to the tree, the tiger stopped crying and looked at her. The little girl was scared at first, but when she saw the sad tiger, she felt sorry for him. She knew the tiger was hurting, so she smiled and carefully stepped closer. The tiger looked into the little girl's bright, caring eyes and soon he was no longer sad. Tom and Lily were playing in the garden. They saw a big tree with many green and yellow fruits. Tom wanted to pick one and eat it. "Look, Lily, these are pears. They are sweet and juicy. Mommy said we should not eat unknown things," Lily said. Tom did not listen to Lily. He climbed the tree and reached for a pear. He pulled it hard and it came off. He smiled and bit into it. "Mmm, this is good. You should try it, Lily. It is not bad, it is yummy," Tom said. Lily looked at Tom. She saw him eating the pear. She wanted to try it too, but she was afraid. [ROM tries to shake car as FAT directed] Copyright 2007 Dorling Kindersley Amphibians generally start life in water, but later change so that they can live on land. Most return to the water to mate. The life cycle involves three stages: egg, larva, and adult. The change from larva to adult is known as metamorphosis. Amphibians are ectothermic (cold-blooded) animals that have a bony internal skeleton. They have small lungs and can also breathe through their smooth skin, which must be kept moist. Amphibians can lose water through their skin. There are no scales or hair protecting the skin. Most live in damp places. There are about 5,000 species in three orders. A frog looks wet all the time because glands in its skin produce mucus to keep it moist. The frog can then absorb oxygen through its thin skin directly into the bloodstream. Frogs are the only amphibians that can hop. Some have webbed feet and are good swimmers. They live mainly on land and catch worms and insects with their long, sticky tongue. A frog lays its eggs in water, because they have no shell to stop them from drying out. Tadpoles hatch from the eggs and take about 12 weeks to grow and change into frogs. At first the tadpoles breathe like fish, using gills, but as they change, lungs replace the gills. The tadpoles grow back legs, front legs, a big head, and finally their tail disappears. Title: Do An Amazing and Easy Card Trick That Just Works Introduction: Performing magic tricks can be a fun and impressive way to entertain friends and family. This easy card trick requires no special skills or complicated sleight of hand, making it perfect for beginners. The trick relies on simple principles of misdirection and psychology. With some practice, you'll have everyone wondering how you did it! Things You'll Need: - A standard deck of 52 playing cards (no jokers) - Knowledge of four basic card manipulation techniques Step 1: Preparation Before performing this trick, remove three specific cards from your deck - let's say the Three of Hearts, Six of Diamonds, and Jack of Clubs. These will serve as your 'key' cards throughout the trick. Place them at the bottom of the deck in any order you prefer. Remember their positions because they won't change during the performance. Key Tip: Choosing seemingly random cards adds to the mystery; however, avoid using face cards as keys since they might attract unwanted attention. Step 2: Setting Up the Spectator Ask someone from your audience to participate by choosing a card. Have them cut the deck anywhere they want and remember their chosen card without showing anyone else. As they do this, secretly peek at the top card of the lower half of the deck after the cut (this is called a glimpse). It becomes your first key card. Step 3: First Reveal Announce that you know what their selected card is. Show the glimpsed card and claim it's their selection. There's about a 25% chance that you're right due to having three possible key cards. If correct, proceed to Step 6. Otherwise, continue to Step 4. Step 4: Second Glimpse & Misdirect If the spectator denies your initial guess, express mild surprise but assure them that you can still find their card. Subtly glance at the second key card while pretending to shuffle the deck. Try to make this action look casual so as not to raise suspicion. Now, deal two piles of around ten cards each onto the table. Position these piles strategically so that when viewed together, they form the shape of a capital 'L'. Key Guideline: Arranging the piles like an 'L' makes it easier for you to locate the next key card during the next phase. Step 5: Finding the Final Key Card Instruct the participant to place one finger on either pile, then choose one randomly by saying "higher" or "lower". Regardless of their choice, move your hand slowly over the corresponding pile while keeping eye contact with the spectator. Your goal here is to distract them visually and verbally. While doing this, quickly check the top card of the other pile - this should be your third and final key card. Announce its identity as the selected card. Step 6: Conclusion Regardless of whether you correctly guessed the card initially or found it through the process described above, act confident and pleased with yourself. Thank the spectator for participating and return their card to the deck. Practice this routine until you feel comfortable performing it smoothly and convincingly. Conclusion: This beginner-friendly card trick uses psychological ploys rather than complex manual dexterity. By understanding human behavior and employing subtle distractions, you can create moments of astonishment. Always maintain a friendly yet serious demeanor to sell the illusion effectively. Happy tricking! LE' 's turn on the tape recorder. Calling Cachema he said, "Why did you let this white boy beat you?" Cachema replied, "He is a springbok, and runs like one. Title: How to Calm a Cat Down and Relax Them During Fireworks Introduction: Fireworks can be a source of extreme stress and anxiety for cats due to their loud noises and bright lights. However, there are several steps you can take to help calm your cat down and relax them during firework displays. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to do so, emphasizing understanding, preparation, and implementation. Step 1: Understand Your Cat's Fear Cats fear fireworks because they produce unfamiliar and intense sounds that trigger their natural instinct to hide or flee. Recognize this behavior as a normal response to perceived danger rather than misbehavior. By acknowledging your cat's fear, you can approach calming techniques with empathy and patience. Step 2: Create a Safe Space Set up a safe space for your cat where they can retreat and feel secure during fireworks. Ideally, this should be a small, enclosed area such as a closet or bathroom with no windows. Provide familiar items like bedding, toys, and scratching posts to make the space comforting and inviting. Key Tip: Introduce the safe space gradually before fireworks begin to allow your cat time to associate it with safety and security. Step 3: Block Out Sounds and Lights Close curtains and blinds to block out flashes of light from fireworks. Turn on a TV, radio, or white noise machine at a moderate volume to muffle outside noises. Gradually increase the volume as the fireworks start to minimize sudden changes that could heighten your cat's anxiety. Step 4: Use Positive Associations Associate pleasant experiences with fireworks by offering treats, praise, or playtime around the anticipated display time. Start doing this well before the event to create positive associations over time. Be sure to maintain a cheerful and relaxed demeanor while interacting with your cat during these sessions. Step 5: Keep Your Cat Indoors Ensure your cat is indoors before fireworks commence. Secure doors, windows, and pet gates to prevent escapes. If possible, keep your cat inside for the entire evening to avoid exposing them to prolonged periods of distress. Step 6: Monitor Your Cat's Reactions Pay close attention to your cat's reactions throughout the firework display. Look for signs of fear or anxiety such as hiding, trembling, panting, or excessive vocalization. Respond accordingly based on your cat's specific needs and behaviors. Step 7: Distract and Comfort (if necessary) If your cat shows visible signs of fear or discomfort despite preparations, try distracting them with interactive toys or gentle petting. Avoid overwhelming physical contact; instead, let your cat come to you for reassurance. Maintain a calm and soothing tone of voice to help alleviate their stress. Step 8: Post-Firework Check-In After the fireworks have ended, check on your cat and offer extra affection if needed. Spend some quality time together to reinforce feelings of safety and security. Some cats may require additional recovery time after a stressful experience, so be patient and attentive. Conclusion: Calming a cat down during fireworks requires understanding, preparation, and implementation. By creating a safe space, blocking out sounds and lights, using positive associations, monitoring your cat's reactions, and providing distractions when necessary, you can significantly reduce your cat's stress levels during firework displays. Remember to always prioritize your cat's emotional wellbeing and respond with sensitivity and care. Deactivating User Account Control (UAC) in Windows 7 is a process that can increase the convenience of using your computer, as it reduces the number of prompts you'll encounter when performing certain actions. However, keep in mind that disabling UAC might expose your system to potential security risks. Therefore, only proceed with this tutorial if you understand the implications and accept the associated risk. **Disclaimer:** This tutorial is provided for educational purposes only. TechCrunch does not condone or endorse any misuse of these instructions. Always ensure you have proper security measures in place before making changes to your system settings. ### Step 1: Accessing the Start Menu Click on the **Start Orb** (also known as the Start Button), usually located at the bottom left corner of your screen, to open the Start Menu. The Start Orb resembles the Windows flag and has been part of the Windows operating system since its XP version. It provides quick access to various applications, tools, and settings. ![Start Orb](https://techcrunch.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/startorb.png?w=645) *Accessing the Start Menu grants entry to numerous features and customization options.* ### Step 2: Opening the Control Panel From the Start Menu, select **Control Panel**. The Control Panel contains essential configuration settings for managing hardware devices, software programs, user accounts, network connections, and other important aspects of your Windows 7 installation. By default, it opens in Category view; however, you may switch to Large or Small Icons view by clicking on **View by** near the top right corner and selecting either option from the dropdown menu. ![Control Panel Icon](https://techcrunch.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/controlpanelicon.jpg?resize=645%2C440&ssl=1) *The Control Panel houses crucial settings related to your system and user experience.* ### Step 3: Adjusting User Account Control Settings Within the Control Panel, find and click on **User Accounts**, then choose **Change User Account Control settings**. A new window will appear displaying a slider with four different levels of UAC protection. These range from 'Always notify' - which generates prompts even for minor changes to your system - down to 'Never notify', effectively deactivating UAC entirely. ![UAC Slider](https://www.top-password.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/win7uacssettings.png) *Adjusting the UAC slider allows users to fine-tune their notification preferences.* ### Key Tips & Guidelines #### Tip #1: Understand the Risks Before disabling UAC, consider the possible consequences carefully. Without UAC enabled, malicious software could potentially make unauthorized modifications to your system without alerting you. Keep antivirus software updated and perform regular scans to minimize such threats. #### Tip #2: Test Compatibility Before Disabling UAC Some older applications may behave unexpectedly or stop functioning altogether once UAC is disabled. If feasible, test critical software applications after adjusting UAC settings but before committing to permanent deactivation. #### Tip #3: Reverse Changes Easily Should issues arise following UAC deactivation, simply return to the same location within the Control Panel and move the slider back up to restore previous settings. Remember that reverting to more restrictive UAC configurations may require restarting some applications or even your entire PC. That looks beautiful what is it [laughing]? Title: How to Perform the Medicine Ball Drill: Single Arm Sit-Up and Pass The medicine ball drill: single arm sit-up and pass is an effective core exercise that combines strength, stability, coordination, and power. This advanced movement targets your abdominal muscles while also engaging your upper body and hips. By following this detailed guide, you will learn how to properly execute this exercise with correct form, alignment, and technique. Always consult a fitness professional or healthcare provider before starting any new workout regimen. ### Things You'll Need * A medicine ball (6-10 lbs) * Clear floor space * Optional: mat or towel for cushioning There are 12 tones in our Western musical system. There could be more, there could be less, but in Western music, the use of 12 tones is the given. We are given 12 tones to create music with. A tone can also be called a pitch. Pitch is the relative highness or lowness of a tone. This 12 tone system is called Equal Temperament (equally spaced half steps). Just Intonation is another system of tuning based on the pure vibrations of nature (where the harmonics of a given tone are the basis of the tuning - only that key is in 'perfect' tune). The 12 tones lie one-half step to the next or previous. On the guitar, one half-step is one fret. Every next tone (fret) is one half-step away. Half-steps can also be called semitones (where a tone is a whole step, which equals 2 semitones). We will be using the term half-step in every instance. For whatever tone we put at one, we cycle through the 12, then back to the original (whatever we put at one). Any of the 12 tones can be placed at 1. After 12, we go back to 1. So, the first piece of our music theory system: there are 12 tones [for teachers: "same number as months in a year" aids as a statement], and they lie step apart. One possible relationship between colors & music tones: The 12 consist of: The chromatic scale is a summary of 12 half steps, starting from any tone (our musical ruler for measurement): A note that is 2 half steps away from a given tone is called a whole step (w or 2; on guitar a whole step is 2 frets). Example: C to D. The whole step should not be confused with the whole note () in musical notation (rhythmic duration). There are two more naturals than sharps/flats; therefore, there must be two places where naturals touch. These naturally occurring half steps exist between B/C & E/F (no sharp or flat between). B goes directly to C, and E goes directly to F. [B# is C, Cb is B, E# is F, Fb is E]. The two pairs of naturally occuring half-steps are in the above. There isn't a sharp/flat between those tones (the two places on the piano with no black note between = B/C & E/F). A key is a tonal center. The key is the central tone that a piece of music finally rests (tonality) in our ear. Chords are a combination of '3' or more tones played together (harmony). 2 tones work just fine too. Ultimately, a single ringing tone is a chord [Harmonics]. Scales are a series of individual tones played one after another - alphabetically (whether naturals or sharped/flatted tones) ascending & descending. They are tone groups. Arpeggios are chords played one note at a time (ringing or not ringing together). An arpeggio is a 'broken chord.' A root is a tone that names a key, chord, or scale. It is the starting point for building something. It is the number 1 [or zero with Numera] in our traditional music theory system for building anything. It can also be denoted R or 1 (or 0 in numberical chromatics). We are running against music dictionarys with our definition of the word root. Dictionaries call the base tone of a chord, a root, while calling the base tone of a scale, a tonic. This is a semantic point, yet important. Our basic point is that if a tone of a scale can be a root of a chord, it can also be a root to a scale. We can build anything from a given root. Scale tones are often called degrees (& have their own corresponding names). When we play an instrument that can sound all the tones of a scale at the same time, is that not a chord? The naming of a scale degree as a root doesn't inflict any theoretical damage, rather, it creates one less boundary. You can call it Frank if musicianers you work with also call it Frank. The purpose of theory is to create a language that is capable of communicating musical ideas. The only worthwhile theory for us, is applied theory. In musicianer's applications, root is used as we are defining it. Usage dictates meaning, not musicologists defining boundaries. With our definition, there is more intellectual freedom & the understanding that chords & scales are ultimately the same thing. Linking Verbs versus Action Verbs English verbs are divided by function into two basic categories: linking verbs and action verbs. Linking verbs are also called being verbs because they express states of being. Action verbs, well, they describe an action. Linking verbs are like giant equal signs plopped into the middle of your sentence. For example, you can think of the sentence Ralph's uncle is a cannibal with a taste for finger food. Ralph's uncle = a cannibal with a taste for finger food. Or, in shortened form, Ralph's uncle = a cannibal Just as in an algebra equation, the word is links two ideas and says that they are the same. Thus, is is a linking verb. Here are more linking verbs: Lulu will be angry when she hears about the missing bronze tooth. Lulu = angry (will be is a linking verb) Stan was the last surfer to leave the water when the tidal wave approached. Stan = last surfer (was is a linking verb) Here are some linking verbs that are not forms of the verb to be: With his foot-long fingernails and sly smile, Big Foot seemed threatening. Big Foot = threatening (seemed is a linking verb.) A jail sentence for the unauthorized use of a comma appears harsh. jail sentence = harsh (appears is a linking verb in this sentence) The penalty for making a grammar error remains severe. penalty = severe (remains is a linking verb in this sentence) Seemed, appears, remains, and stays are similar to forms of the verb to be in that they express states of being. They simply add shades of meaning to the basic concept. You may, for example, say that With his foot-long fingernails and sly smile, Big Foot was threatening. Now the statement is more definite. Seemed leaves room for doubt. Similarly, remains adds a time dimension to the basic expression of being. The sentence implies that the penalty was and still is severe. Sensory verbs may also be linking verbs: The ten-year-old lasagna in your refrigerator smells disgusting. lasagna = disgusting (smells is a linking verb) The ten-year-old lasagna in your refrigerator also looks disgusting. lasagna = disgusting (looks is a linking verb) Needless to say, the ten-year-old lasagna in your refrigerator tastes great! lasagna = great (tastes is a linking verb) Verbs that refer to the five senses are linking verbs only if they act as an equal sign in the sentence. If they aren't equating two ideas, they aren't linking verbs. In the preceding example sentence about Ralph's double chin, feel is a linking verb. Here's a different sentence with the same verb: With their delicate fingers, Lulu and Stan feel Ralph's chin. In this sentence, feel is not a linking verb because you're not saying that Lulu and Stan = chin. Instead, you're saying that Lulu and Stan don't believe that Ralph shaved, so they went stubble hunting. Linking verbs are important, but unless you're in some sort of hippie commune left over from the sixties, you just can't sit around being all the time. You have to do something. Here's where action verbs come into the picture. Everything that is not being is action, at least in the verb world. Unlike the giant equal sign associated with linking verbs, something happens with an action verb: Drew slapped the offending pig right on the snout. (Slapped is an action verb.) Fred will steal third base as soon as his sneezing fit ends. (Will steal and ends are action verbs.) According to the teacher, Roger has shot at least 16 spitballs in the last ten minutes. (Has shot is an action verb.) Don't let the name action fool you. Some action verbs aren't particularly energetic: think, sit, stay, have, sleep, dream, and so forth. Besides describing the ideal vacation, these words are also action verbs! Think of the definition this way: if the verb is not a giant equal sign (a linking verb), it's an action verb. Now you have to lay on my bed. Mom you want me to show you some pictures? Are we going to say our prayers before we eat? I made the one Rudolph the red-nosed reindeer reindeer. Do you think she'll be having any of those any time soon? The caterpillar ate through one nice green leaf... What do you want to know about Eric? The medication can reduce hyperactivity and inattention in people with autism. The medication can have many side-effects. Examples are Methylphenidate, Clonidine and Guanfacine.
