Scrabble Rules - Scrabble Official Rules Links to Scrabble rules and Scrabble official game instructions.Two to four players play in any game of Scrabble. The object of Scrabble is to score more points than one's opponent. A player collects points by placing words on the game board. Each letter has a different point value, so the strategy becomes to play words with high scoring letter combinations. The Scrabble Board A Scrabble game board is made of up cells in a square grid. The Scrabble board is 15 cells wide by 15 cells high. The Scrabble tiles fit within these cells one to a cell. Scrabble is played with exactly 100 tiles. 98 of these tiles contain letters on them, while there are 2 blank tiles. These blank tiles add a wildcard aspect to Scrabble. The blanks substitute for any letter in the alphabet. Once played, a blank tile remains for the remainder of the game the letter for which it was substituted when first played. Various letters have different point values, depending on the rarity of the letter and the difficulty in playing it. Blank tiles have no point value. Here are the point values for each letter in Scrabble. 0 Points - Blank tile. 1 Point - A, E, I, L, N, O, R, S, T and U. 2 Points - D and G. 3 Points - B, C, M and P. 4 Points - F, H, V, W and Y. 5 Points - K. 8 Points - J and X. 10 Points - Q and Z. Extra Point Values Some squares on the Scrabble board represent multipliers. If a tile is placed on this square, then the tile's value is multiplied by a factor or either 2x or 3x. Certain tiles multiply the point value of an entire word and not simply the tile on that space. Double Letter Scores - Light blue cells are found isolated on the board. When a tile is placed on this space, that tile's point value is multiplied by two. Triple Letter Score - This is a dark blue cell on the Scrabble. The tile placed on this square has its points multiplied by three. Double Word Score - Light red cells are found running diagonally towards the four corners of the board. When a player plays a word on one of these squares, the point value of the entire word is multiplied by two. Triple Word Score - This is a dark red square on the Scrabble board. These are found on the four sides of the board equidistant from the four corners of the board. When a word is played using this square, then the points for the word are multiplied by three. One Single Use - Note that extra point squares are only usable once. If one player plays a word using this cells, then the next time that space is used to make a word, the point value is not multiplied. Starting the Game Without looking into the tile bag, each of the Scrabble players takes one tile out of the tile bag. Whichever player has the letter closest to the beginning of the alphabet goes first. A blank tile is considered better than an "A". These tiles are placed into the bag once more. Starting the game, each player begins their turn by drawing seven tiles apiece from the bag. The player can do one of three things on a turn. The player can place a word, exchange tiles or pass. Most of the time, the player will take the option of playing a word. Exchanging tiles allows a player to replace anywhere between one and all of the tiles on the player's rack. If this option is taken, the player cannot do anything else that hand. Therefore, a tile exchange cannot be performed on a turn when the player places a word on the board. A player may pass at any time. If all player's pass twice in a row, then the game ends. The First Word Score A player begins the game by placing a word on the star square at the center of the board. This star acts as a double word score. The star cell does not act as a double word score for subsequent players playing off the center square. Play continues in a clockwise direction around the Scrabble board. Replacing Scrabble Tiles When a player places tiles on the Scrabble board, that player draws new tiles from the tile bag, adding until that player's number of tiles equals seven. The player must not look at the tiles when choosing new ones. Tile selection is usually done by holding the bag above the eye line, then reaching into the bag to add tiles one by one. The Fifty Point Bonus When a player is able to place all seven tiles from the tile rack on the board at the same time, that player receives a 50 point bonus. In end game scenarios, when the players hold less than the standard seven tiles, a player does not get the 50 point bonus for using all the tiles on the rack. The End of a Scrabble Game When all of the tiles have been taken from the bag and one player has used all of the tiles on their rack, then the game ends. Tallying Scrabble Scores Once the game has ended, each player counts the points on the tiles left remaining in their rack. Each player has that number deducted from their final score. The player who ended the game and who therefore has no more tiles remaining is given an added bonus. Add the total of all points of all remaining players with tiles left on their racks. This number is added to the final score of the player who is "out" of tiles. The Scrabble player with the highest score after all final scores are tallied wins. Accepted Scrabble Words Players may place any word which can be found in a standard English language dictionary. Official Scrabble dictionaries also can be found in bookstores and online. Types of words which cannot be used are abbreviations, prefixes and suffixes. Words that require a hyphen or an apostrophe cannot be played. Words that are spelled with a capital letter cannot be used. Generally speaking in an English-language game of Scrabble, foreign words cannot be placed on the Scrabble board. If those words appear in a standard English dictionary, then the word is allowable. This is because the word is spoken often enough by native English-speakers that it has become a part of the English language. All contents copyright 2009 - 2012, Scrabble Pages. A "stylus" is a pen that cannot write on paper, but can write on clay or wax. Styli of wood or metal have been used for thousands of years, for example in cuneiform writing. A modern computer stylus is usually made of plastic and may have electronics inside. It is used with a touchscreen. Sir Richard saw that I was in earnest, and his countenance cleared. [D seems to have forgotten he has the tape running] The venous system of the lower limbs The veins carry the blood back to the heart and lungs. Its blue color is due to the low oxygen content. When standing, nearly half of the venous blood is in our legs. The blood transit from the legs to the heart has to overcome gravity. This requires the action of a pump. The pump function is mainly achieved by the foot and ankle and calf movement. During each step the muscles compress the veins located inside the calf muscle thereby allowing the venous blood to rush back to the heart. The venous blood return In healthy individuals, venous blood flows mainly from the superficial veins (located right under the skin) to the deep veins, through the muscles. Once in the deep veins, the blood moves up to the heart and the lungs to be re-oxygenated. In order to direct the blood flow from the foot to the upper leg, and to prevent it from falling back, the leg veins have thin valves that work as a lock system. In a normal vein, one-way valves are located every 2-5 cm to aid in the proximal flow toward the heart. When calf muscles relax, the valves close to prevent blood from flowing backward into the lower part of the veins. Thus, they play a major role in the blood return. These valves are fragile and can be easily damaged. Defective valves are mainly responsible for the development of venous disease. An' they just' have one face that's polished. Title: How to Make a Call to Scotland Introduction: Making an international call can be intimidating if you've never done it before. However, with this easy-to-follow guide, you will learn how to make a successful call to Scotland using any phone service provider. This article covers both landline and mobile calls. Let's get started! Step 1: Find Out Your Recipient's Number Before making a call, ensure that you have the correct number of your recipient. It should include their area code (e.g., 01xx xx xxxx or 03xx xxx xxxx). Note that Scottish numbers do not start with '4', so avoid confusion with UK-wide services. Key Tip: If you are unsure about the area codes within Scotland, refer to this list: <https://www.scotlandinfo.com/telephone_codes.asp> Step 2: Check Your Phone Service Provider's International Calling Rates Different providers offer varying rates for international calls. Familiarize yourself with these charges to avoid unexpected bills. Some companies may also provide special deals or add-ons for overseas calling. Guideline: Most providers require dialing specific access codes (like *84 or #321) followed by the country code (+44) and then the full Scottish number. Consult your carrier's manual or customer support for accurate information. Step 3: Dial the Exit Code For US-based callers, the exit code is '011'. For other countries, please check here: <http://www.countrycallingcodes.com/> Explanation: The exit code allows your call to leave your home country and connect to another nation's network. Step 4: Enter the Country Code Following the exit code, enter the United Kingdom's country code which is '44'. Remember, no need to put a leading zero when including the country code. Step 5: Add the Area Code Now, input the recipient's area code without the initial zero. For example, if the number begins with 0131, just type 131. Step 6: Input the Local Number Lastly, dial the remaining digits of the local telephone number. Combine all components thus far - exit code + country code + area code + local number. Example: Suppose you want to reach a Edinburgh business whose phone number is 0131 555 1234. Here's what you would actually dial from the U.S.: *84 + 011 + 44 + 131 + 555 1234 Step 7: Wait for Connection After following these steps correctly, wait for the connection. Depending on various factors like time zones and operator traffic, there might be some delay before the line rings. Key Tips: - Be patient; international connections sometimes take longer than domestic ones. - Speak clearly and slowly during the call, especially at the beginning. Conclusion: With practice and familiarization, placing a call to Scotland becomes as simple as dialing locally. Always confirm details with your service provider and remember to respect time differences. Happy calling! [serves up a plate of eggs without tails] Title: Understanding Euclid's Division Lemma and Cube Numbers Hello young mathematicians! Today, we will explore a fun property of numbers using something called "Euclid's Division Lemma." Don't worry if it sounds complicated - I promise it isn't! We'll break it down together step by step. First, let's talk about what a positive integer is. These are just whole numbers greater than zero, like 1, 2, 3, 4, etc. Now, imagine taking one of these positive integers and multiplying it by itself three times. That gives us the cube of the number (we write this as n ). For example, the cube of 2 would be 2 x 2 x 2 = 8. Easy peasy, right? Now, onto Euclid's Division Lemma. This fancy term actually means something quite simple: when you divide any positive integer by another positive integer, there will always be a quotient and remainder. You may have already learned about this concept with the phrase "dividend = divisor quotient + remainder." Let's try an example: If we divide 13 by 4, our dividend is 13, our divisor is 4, and after some calculations, we find out the quotient is 3 and the remainder is 1 because 4 x 3 = 12, which is very close but not equal to 13; hence, 13 - 12 = 1 is the nonzero remainder. So, applying Euclid's Division Lemma states that every natural number can be expressed as N = dQ + R where N is the dividend, d is the divisor, Q is the Quotient, and R is the Remainder between 0 and d-1. But why does all this matter for cube numbers? Well, here comes the exciting part! Using Euclid's Division Lemma, we can prove that no matter which positive integer we choose, its cube will always be either in the form of 9m, 9m + 1, or 9m + 8. How cool is that?! Let me give you a quick idea of how this works: When cubing a two-digit number ending in 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7, you get a new two-digit number whose last digit follows a pattern: 0, 1, 8, 7, 4, 5, 6, and 3 respectively. And interestingly, those four possible patterns match up perfectly with the shapes of 9m, 9m+1, 9m+8, and 9m+7 cubes. The case of single digit numbers also work similarly, making them fall into one of those categories too. So next time you come across cube numbers, remember this little trick! It's amazing how powerful understanding small concepts like Euclid's Division Lemma can uncover hidden secrets within bigger mathematical ideas. Happy problem solving! One of the frustrations for teachers and parents of children with ADHD is getting a child to stop, listen, and understand what is being taught or asked of him. These tips and strategies will enable students to listen and learn in school and at home. Tools for Teachers >Get your students' attention! Clap out a rhythm and have your students clap the rhythm back until the class is quiet. This signals that it is time to move to the next activity. During group lessons, keep students involved by asking them questions. Play music or sing a song to keep them focused on the material being taught. >Repeat it back. When giving instructions, limit the number of steps involved and have the students repeat the steps back to you, one at a time. Use the words first, next, and last to give order and structure. >Go beyond textbooks. Relate concepts to real-life experiences through visuals, sign language, or gestures. Bring vocabulary words and stories to life by giving students examples from everyday life. If you are starting a story about a supermarket, bring in items that you buy there. >Make directions concrete. Make sure your commands and directions are precise. "Do careful work" or "Be respectful" are too vague. Be specific in what you expect to see: "Eyes looking at me, bottoms in your chairs, book open to page 21, and desks cleared except for a pencil." >Take small steps. Read a few pages of an article or story at a time. Teach students how to stop and ask themselves questions about what they have read. Allow them to draw a picture or write a key word on a sticky note and attach it to the page. >Use attention-getting strategies. Make your voice go up or down, or make it louder or softer while doing a read-aloud or giving directions. Buy a garden glove and write a story element on each finger. Pointers for Parents >Keep it predictable. Be consistent with the words you use to give directions, and stick to established schedules in your household. This will increase a child's listening comprehension because he knows what to expect and feels secure and calm. >Show him what you want him to do. Walk through the steps of a task. Check for understanding of directions. Write down the task you want done (in words or pictures) and give it to your child for reference. >Check in with a child to make sure he isn't tuning out. Before, during, and after chores, homework, or a task, have your child tell you specifically what he is doing. This continuous reminder of the task at hand keeps your child focused. It may seem redundant, but it works! >Move to remember. Get your child up and moving to help with listening skills. Use hand gestures, exercises, or dance moves to help him remember what to do. >Help a child recall. If your child is watching TV, ask her about what she is watching. When your child gets off the phone, ask him what he talked about -- and don't interrupt. This article appears in the Fall 2012 issue of ADDitude. SUBSCRIBE TODAY to ensure you don't miss a single issue. To discuss and exchange techniques that enable listening, visit the Parents of ADHD Children support group on ADDConnect. he commanded. Okay let's do the next thing. Title: How to Construct a Regular Heptagon A regular heptagon is a seven-sided polygon with equal side lengths and interior angles. Unlike equilateral triangles or squares, constructing a regular heptagon by hand can be challenging due to its complexity. However, with patience and careful adherence to these steps, you'll find that creating an accurate representation isn't as daunting as it seems. This guide will walk you through the process using only compass and straightedge - no protractors allowed! ### Materials Needed 1. A pair of compasses 2. Title: How to Enable the Chrome Password Generator The Google Chrome web browser comes with a built-in password generator that can create strong and unique passwords for your online accounts. This feature is particularly useful in enhancing your online security by helping you avoid using weak or reused passwords. However, this feature is turned off by default. In this tutorial, we will guide you through enabling the Chrome password generator and explain why this feature is essential for maintaining good online security practices. **Step 1: Launch Google Chrome** First, open your computer and launch the Google Chrome web browser. If you haven't installed Chrome yet, download and install it from <https://www.google.com/chrome/> before proceeding with these steps. **Step 2: Access Chrome Settings** Click on the three vertical dots located at the top right corner of the Chrome window. From the dropdown menu, select 'Settings.' Alternatively, type `chrome://settings` directly into the address bar and hit Enter. **Step 3: Search for Autofill settings** In the Settings page, scroll down until you find the 'Autofill' section. Click on the 'Passwords' option under this section. To quickly access the password settings, type `chrome://settings/passwords` in the address bar and press Enter. **Step 4: Turn on Offer to Save Passwords** Ensure that the toggle switch next to 'Offer to save passwords' is enabled (switched to blue). Enabling this setting allows Chrome to remember your usernames and passwords when you log in to websites. The Chrome password generator relies on this functionality to work correctly. ![Turn on Offer to Save Passwords](javascript:void(0)) Key Tip: It is crucial to set up a master passphrase or screen lock on your device to protect saved passwords from unauthorized access. **Step 5: Enable Password Generation** Under the 'Save passwords' heading, click on the 'Advanced' link. A new panel will appear displaying additional options related to autofill and password management. Look for the 'Auto-generate and fill passwords' option and enable the toggle switch next to it. ![Enable Auto-Generate Passwords](javascript:void(0)) Key Guideline: Using complex and unique passwords significantly reduces the risk of having your online accounts compromised due to credential stuffing attacks or brute force attempts. **Step 6: Test the Chrome Password Generator** Navigate to any website registration form where you need to create an account. Fill out all required fields except for the password field. When clicking inside the password input box, look for the suggested generated password displayed as a greyed-out text above the keyboard. You may also see a small key icon within the password field itself. Click on either the greyed-out text or the key icon to accept the auto-generated password. ![Generated Password Suggestion](javascript:void(0)) Congratulations! You have successfully enabled the Chrome password generator, which will help you generate secure and randomized passwords for your online accounts. By following these simple but effective measures, you are well on your way towards implementing sound online security best practices. Sometimes don't you dream about the witch? They left them on the floor, on the couch, on the table and everywhere. One day, their mom came home from work and saw the mess. She was not happy. She said to Sam and Mia, "You need to return your toys to their places. The living room is not organized. It is hard to walk and find things. And it is not safe. You can trip and fall and hurt yourself or break something. Please, help me clean up." Sam and Mia did not want to clean up. They wanted to keep playing. They said to their mom, "But mom, we are having fun. We will clean up later. Can we play for a little longer?" Their mom said, "No, you cannot play for a little longer. You have played enough. It is time to return your toys to their places. You can play again tomorrow, but only if you keep the living room organized. Now, come on, let's do it together. It will be faster and easier." Sam and Mia sighed. They knew their mom was right. They did not want to trip and fall and hurt themselves or break something. They did not want to make their mom unhappy. They decided to help their mom clean up. They returned their toys to their places. They put the cars in the box, the dolls in the basket, the blocks in the bag and the books on the shelf. They made the living room organized. Their mom smiled. She said to Sam and Mia, "Thank you, Sam and Mia. You did a good job. The living room looks much better. I am proud of you. Now, how about we have some cookies and milk as a reward?" Sam and Mia smiled too. They said to their mom, "Yes, please. We love cookies and milk. And we love you, mom." Title: How to Determine If a Tooth Needs to Be Pulled Introduction: Deciding whether or not a tooth needs to be pulled is a serious decision that should be made with the help of a dental professional. However, understanding the process and being able to identify potential issues can help you make an informed decision about your oral health. This tutorial will guide you through the steps necessary to determine if a tooth needs to be extracted. Step 1: Assess Pain Levels One of the most common reasons people consider having a tooth pulled is due to pain. Keep track of any discomfort or pain levels associated with the tooth in question. Persistent pain, especially when chewing or applying pressure, could indicate a problem such as decay or infection that may require extraction. Key Tip: Mild sensitivity after consuming hot or cold foods is normal; however, severe and constant pain requires immediate attention from a dentist. Step 2: Examine Swelling Check for swelling around the gums near the painful tooth. Redness, tenderness, or visible swelling might suggest an abscess or infection, which could necessitate removal of the tooth. Guideline: If you notice pus drainage accompanied by bad taste or breath, seek emergency dental care as soon as possible. Step 3: Evaluate Movement Gently touch the tooth with your tongue or finger to see if it feels loose. A loose tooth can often signify advanced periodontal disease, injury, or decay, all of which may lead to extraction. Key Tip: Healthy teeth shouldn't move significantly within their sockets. Some movement can occur during orthodontic treatment but consult your dentist if concerned. Step 4: Look for Signs of Decay Inspect the tooth visually for signs of decay like dark spots, holes, or cavities. Untreated decay can cause significant damage to the pulp inside the tooth, leading to infection and potentially requiring extraction. Guideline: Regular dental check-ups are crucial for early detection and prevention of tooth decay. Step 5: Check Alignment Assess whether the tooth in question aligns properly with others. Misaligned teeth can contribute to bite problems, jaw pain, and difficulty cleaning, making extraction a viable option in certain cases. Key Tip: Overcrowded mouths sometimes need extractions before getting braces or other corrective treatments. Step 6: Consult a Dental Professional If you suspect that a tooth may need to be removed based on these self-assessment steps, schedule an appointment with a dentist or oral surgeon. They'll perform a thorough examination using tools such as X-rays to confirm the diagnosis and discuss options. Tip for Success: Bring along any notes or observations from your home assessment to aid communication with the dental professional. Conclusion: While this tutorial provides guidance on determining if a tooth needs to be pulled, only a licensed dental practitioner can provide a definitive answer. By staying vigilant regarding changes in your oral health and following good hygiene practices, you're taking important steps towards maintaining a healthy smile. Atria is talking about two chambers; atrium is talking about one chamber. There is a right atrium and right ventricle. These get blood that comes to the heart. They pump this blood to the lungs. In the lungs blood picks up oxygen and drops carbon dioxide. Blood from the lungs goes to the left atrium and ventricle. The left atrium and ventricle send the blood out to the body. The left ventricle works six times harder than the right ventricle because it carries oxygenated blood. The inhibitors fit loosely or partially into the enzyme's active site. This prevents or slows down an enzyme-substrate complex being formed. Many pupae are easy for predators to attack. Some caterpillars (in the family Hesperiidae and the subfamilies Parnassiinae and Satyrinae) make shelters out of silk and leaves to protect themselves when they become pupae. In the next 2 lessons there is an explanation of the constructions [di ], [m th], and [w n th], meaning "how", "which?" and "where?" Someone comes and tells you of a conversation he has just had with a 3rd person who perhaps is at your door, and you go to find out for yourself because it isn't clear, or he hasn't explained enough to suit you. So you begin by saying: What is it you were saying? ||Ki i m! n ta a k ri k. C gatd n du l wal m n wa a r ru n k c maath, k t m ci du d w di t pu nyd . Doctor! I have trouble. I brought my child to the clinc this morning and he drank, but now his (condition) went big (has increased) in his body. ||K gatdu gat m th? Ci gaat ti ny n uan b n wal . But your child is which child? Many children came today. ||C al di , n gat? How is he called, the child? ||C al i di , n gat? He is called De . De K r. ||K du d u ? Du d ki l ki u ? And what is his (condition)? Is his (condition) a cough or what? || n, du d ki l. Yes, his (situation) is a cough. ||K j n a ni t m , n gat pu nyd ? And where is he now, the child himself? ||J n a wan . He is here. ||J n a thaar jiaath m . He is under that tree. || n, k w r, no o ni j wan m . Yes, and go, bring him here. ||B j w n . will go bring him. - This consonant is a voiced th- and is spoken in the position of the tongue for the English word "the". - Practice drills. - [di ] is an interrogative word. It is not a pronoun. It is commonly translated to mean: "how?" "how's that?" (meaning you didn't hear), "what do you mean?", "how many?", or "how much?". The meaning depends on the context and/or the grammatical environment. - In an elliptical sentence [i di ?] which means "how's that?", "what were you saying?". The full sentence would be [ j n di ? ] and finds its meaning in whatever the [ ] refers to. As for example, what do you think about it? What do you say about it? How do you feel about it? In other words, the setting defines - In accompaniment with the numbers particle [da ] to mean "how much?" [da ] may be omitted. - [Ti ti k yio w da di ?] -- These are with how much money? i.e. How much do these cost? - [Go o ri p k da di ? (or) Go o ri p k di ?] -- You want the limit how much? i.e. How much do you want? - With the noun [gu th] -- places to mean "how many times?". e.g. [Ci b n k gu th di ?] -- You came with how many times? i.e. How many times did you come? - With the Nuerized Arabic word [thaa] -- watch, to mean "what time is it?" - [ thaa di t m ?] -- What time is it now? - [Ci b n k tha di ?] -- What time did you come? - With the clausal particel [i ] (implying "you tell me") following all aspects. - [Bi i i di ?] -- How do you come? - [Ci b n di ?] -- How did you come? - [Bi b n di ?] -- How will you come? - In the same type of construction as above with the verb [t ] -- exist/be, to mean "how is it?" "how is so and so?" etc. It is used when wanting to inquire as to someone's health or the state of something. - [Ti i di ?] -- How are you? - [T di ?] -- How is it, etc? - [Pu nydu t di ?] -- How is your body? - Notice in the dialogue that the verb [ ] is often omitted in the sentences initiated with [du d ]. This is because the verb [ ] is slurred together with the [-d ], or else is simply dropped. Do you think it is fun to celebrate someone else's cultural festivities? Why or why not? 3. Introduce the art activity: Making Chinese Lanterns and Dancing Dragons . a. Show the students your completed art products. Tell the students that these art projects represent some of the creative projects the Chinese make in anticipation of their Chinese New Year celebration. b. Allow each student to select to make one or both of the art products. c. Then, ask the students to take a seat in a semi-circle around the table-booth or puppet theater you set up for them. 4. Ask for 4 volunteers to READ the skit parts: TRACE, GAIL, JESSIE and CONNIE (NOTE: The readers will be expected to read at a 3rd grade or higher level. If reading is a problem for your age group of children, assign 2 readers per part or ask an older child to assist a younger child with his or her part. You can also allow very young readers to 'act out' the parts with the characters while you read all the parts. Or, simply substitute a scenario about the Chinese New Year without the skit that follows.) CONNIE CELEBRATES THE CHINESE NEW YEAR You're Invited! CONNIE: Hi everyone! Would you all like to celebrate the Chinese New Year with me? GAIL, TRACE, JESSIE: (All together) YES! We would love to! GAIL: But hey, Connie, what is the Chinese New Year? CONNIE: I'm glad you asked me that, Gail. JESSIE: Well, I want to know too. TRACE: Yeah Connie, me too! CONNIE: OK guys. I'm happy to 'tell all.' Remember, this is a really important celebration for my family. We prepare months in advance for it and really look forward to the celebration each year. The Chinese New Year starts with the first New Moon of a New Year and ends with the FULL MOON 15 days later. So, we celebrate the Chinese New Year for 2 entire weeks plus one day. TRACE: You mean, your new year starts with a new moon like the moon in the sky? CONNIE: Yes Trace. You know that the moon has complete cycles, right? TRACE: Well, of course, I knew that! JESSIE: (whispering) How did you know that! CONNIE: So, my family celebrates the Chinese New Year according to what is called the Lunar Year which means 'moon year'. This celebration is considered a way to get rid of the old year and celebrate the new one with great thoughts, prayers and happy times. GAIL: So, Connie, what is it that you do for an entire 15 days to celebrate? CONNIE: Actually, Gail, that is a good question. It is still a little confusing to me to keep all those days straight. Let me give you an overview: New Years Eve - We celebrate family and our ancestors on this day. The Chinese believe that it is our ancestors who 'laid-the-foundation' for our family as we know it today. New Years Day - We usually have a feast. You may call it a Thanksgiving feast that includes our family and what we are thankful for. On this day, many Chinese do not eat meat because they believe it will make them live longer and be healthy to do without it. JESSIE: In America, on New Years day, many people eat black-eyed peas for good luck. Isn't that strange? Well, at least we all have that no-meat thing in common so far CONNIE: I never thought of that you're right. Ok, let me go on. 2nd day - Dog day. Title: How to Distract Yourself from Physical Pain **Disclaimer:** While distraction can be an effective tool for managing pain, it is important to consult with healthcare professionals for proper diagnosis and treatment. This guide is intended to provide suggestions for coping with minor physical discomforts and should not replace medical advice. If you experience severe or chronic pain, seek professional help immediately. 1. **Engage in Deep Breathing Exercises** *Deep breathing exercises can help take your mind off the pain by focusing your attention on something other than the sensation.* 1. Find a quiet and comfortable place. Sit or lie down with your back straight. 2. Place one hand on your chest and the other on your stomach. 3. Slowly inhale through your nose, counting to four as you fill your lungs completely. Feel your diaphragm rise as your abdomen expands against your hand. 4. Hold your breath for a count of seven. [sound of rattling paper at this point] Title: How to Eat Bananas Bananas are one of the most popular fruits worldwide due to their delicious taste, versatile uses, and numerous health benefits. They're packed with essential vitamins and minerals such as Vitamin C, Potassium, and dietary fiber. This article will guide you through the simple yet rewarding process of eating bananas while providing helpful tips along the way. **Step 1: Select Ripe Bananas** Choose bright yellow bananas without any green or brown spots. A ripe banana is easier to digest because its starches have converted into natural sugars. Moreover, overripe bananas (with small brown patches) can be used for baking recipes like banana bread! *Key Tip*: If your bananas aren't quite ripe but you want to eat them immediately, place them in a paper bag at room temperature. The ethylene gas they release will speed up the ripening process. **Step 2: Wash Your Hands** Before handling food, especially when it's something you plan to consume raw, make sure to wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm water to prevent spreading germs or bacteria. **Step 3: Hold the Stem End** Hold the banana by the stem end - this part is also known as the 'handle'. It's where the fruit begins and makes it easy to peel away from the rest of the bunch if necessary. **Step 4: Pinch and Peel Back the Top Layers** Using your thumb and index finger, pinch and pull downwards on the top layers of the skin around the stem area until you see about an inch of the white flesh. **Step 5: Continue Peeling Down the Sides** Once you've started the initial tear, continue pulling downward along both sides of the banana using your fingers or thumbs till all the skin comes off. Try to keep the stringy strands attached to the peel rather than the fruit itself for less mess. **Step 6: Discard the Peel Properly** After successfully removing the peel, ensure that you dispose of it properly in a trash bin to avoid creating litter and attracting pests. **Step 7: Enjoy Your Banana!** Now you're ready to enjoy your fresh, clean, perfectly ripened banana! You could eat it whole, slice it onto cereal, yogurt, oatmeal, or blend it into smoothies. *Guideline*: Remember to chew slowly and mindfully. Chewing well aids digestion and allows you to savor the flavor more fully. By following these steps, you now know how to select, prepare, and consume bananas safely and hygienically. Happy snacking! Eating an apple with braces can be a daunting task, but with the right techniques and precautions, you can enjoy this delicious fruit without causing any damage to your orthodontic appliances. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to eat an apple with braces: **Step 1: Choose the Right Type of Apple** opt for apples that are softer and easier to bite into, such as Gala or Fuji varieties. These types have a thinner skin and break down more readily than firmer varieties like Granny Smith. The softer texture reduces the risk of damaging your brackets or wires. **Step 2: Clean Your Teeth Beforehand** Before eating an apple, brush and floss your teeth thoroughly. This removes food particles stuck between your braces which could otherwise get pushed deeper into your gum line while biting into the apple. **Step 3: Cut the Apple Into Slices** Instead of biting directly into an entire apple, cut it into small slices. Use a sharp knife to make thin wedges; aim for about inch thick. This size allows you to comfortably chew each piece using only your back molars, thus minimizing potential harm to your braces. **Step 4: Take Small Bites** Take tiny bites from each slice, ensuring that most of the chewing happens at the back of your mouth where there are no brackets or wires. Chewing slowly also ensures proper breakdown of the food, reducing the chance of anything getting trapped in your braces. **Step 5: Avoid Crunchy Textures** Steer clear of overly crunchy parts of the apple, especially near the seeds and stem areas. These sections tend to be tougher and may cause unnecessary strain on your braces. Stick to the fleshy middle portion of the apple slices instead. **Step 6: Rinse After Eating** After finishing your apple, rinse your mouth vigorously with water. Swish it around all sides of your teeth and through the gaps between your braces. This simple action dislodges any stray bits of apple that might be lingering. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always listen to your orthodontist's advice regarding diet modifications during brace treatment. They know what's best for your specific situation. - Be mindful of other hard or sticky foods that could potentially damage your braces (popcorn kernels, nuts, candies, etc.) - Carry a travel toothbrush and dental floss for quick cleanups after meals, particularly if fresh fruits aren't immediately available post-mealtime. - Regular dental checkups are crucial while wearing braces. Make sure you keep up with them! By following these steps and adhering to these guidelines, you'll maintain healthy teeth and intact braces - enabling you to continue enjoying apples throughout your orthodontic journey. Our local science museum actually has a mini-golf course that was designed so kids can learn about rivers while putting. As we played on this specialized course, our minds wandered from holes-in-one to how real rivers work. Our DragonflyTV question: What do rivers do to the land? What did we do? We spent time at the museum exhibit on rivers, and chose some things we wanted to learn more about. We packed up our canoe and headed for a local river to check it out! What did we find out? Our canoe ride showed us how rivers meander; or twist and turn, and how the water deposits rocks and pebbles on the inside of each curve. We also learned how sediment layers develop in water; and how humans change river flow with dams. - Collect some sand and pebbles, and do a sediment investigation. Make sure you have pebbles and sand grains of various shapes and sizes. Mix them all up. Get a clear jar or bucket with water in it. Pour in the mixture of sand and pebbles. Watch them settle to the bottom. Which grain size settles farthest down? Why? Stir up the sand and pebbles with a stick and let them settle again. Do the same size grains always settle farthest down? - Study how water meanders on a driveway. This works best if your driveway has a little slope. Stand at the top of the driveway, and then slowly pour water from a small bucket onto the driveway, letting it run down the slope. Watch the water's path. The water won't carve a path in the driveway, of course, but it will "choose" a path to follow. Watch the leading edge of the water, as it runs into stuff like cracks, dips or pebbles, and see how it moves left or right to get past the obstacles. Draw a picture of the path the water leaves behind. - Use this rivers investigation as a science fair project idea for your elementary or middle school science fair! Then tell us about it! It is the center of mass (center of gravity) and therefore is always located within the triangle. The centroid divides each median into a piece one-third the length of the median and two-thirds the length. To find the centroid, we find the midpoint of two sides in the coordinate plane and use the corresponding vertices to get equations. Title: How to Assemble Jigsaw Puzzles **Things You'll Need:** 1. A jigsaw puzzle 2. A flat, clean surface (like a table or the floor) 3. Tray or box lid (optional but recommended) 4. Time and patience **Step 1: Choose Your Puzzle** Start by selecting a puzzle that suits your skill level and interests. Consider factors such as piece count, shape complexity, image difficulty, and theme. This will help ensure an enjoyable and rewarding experience. **Step 2: Find a Suitable Workspace** Find a spacious, well-lit area with enough room to spread out all the pieces. Using a tray or box lid can be helpful to contain the scattered pieces during assembly and make transportation easier if needed. **Step 3: Sort the Edges First** Begin by separating edge pieces from interior ones. Edge pieces typically have one side that forms part of the frame around the complete picture. Organizing them together allows you to create a boundary for the rest of the puzzle. **Step 4: Assemble the Border** Connect the edge pieces to form the outer perimeter of the puzzle. Pay attention to straight edges, corners, and any distinctive patterns depicted on these pieces. Once assembled, this border serves as a framework for arranging the remaining internal sections. **Key Tip**: If there are multiple colors in the border, consider sorting those segments separately before connecting them. **Step 5: Divide the Interior into Sections** Analyze the big picture and break it down into smaller manageable areas based on different colors, shapes, textures, or objects within the image. Group corresponding puzzle pieces accordingly. For instance, group sky blue pieces apart from green grass pieces or separate car parts from building components. **Step 6: Start Building Inside Sections** Select a section and start fitting its puzzles together using visual cues provided by their respective groups. Match similar shades, sizes, shapes, and directions of lines or curves until they fit perfectly. Repeat this process for every segment identified earlier. **Step 7: Refine Individual Segments** Once individual sections begin taking shape, refine them further by checking for missing pieces or potential misplacements. Continuously compare new additions against existing pieces to maintain accuracy. **Step 8: Merge Completed Segments** When individual segments are mostly completed, merge them gradually into larger wholes while matching adjoining sides carefully. Remember, some portions might need rearrangement due to overlaps or gaps created when joining two or more clusters. **Step 9: Fill Remaining Gaps** After merging most segments, focus on filling up leftover spaces with appropriate pieces. At this stage, trial-and-error becomes crucial since many empty spots could potentially accommodate several alternatives. **Step 10: Review and Finalize** Review the entire puzzle meticulously, ensuring every single piece is correctly positioned and no gaps remain unfilled. Double-check alignment along seams and boundaries. Make final adjustments where necessary. **Guidelines:** * Always handle puzzle pieces gently to prevent bending, breaking, or losing small fragments. * Regularly take breaks to avoid fatigue and frustration; returning refreshed often brings renewed perspective. * Use additional tools like tweezers, magnifying glasses, or soft brushes if required, especially for complex, tiny pieces. * Don't rush! Assembly should be a relaxing, engaging activity rather than a stressful chore. Understanding Subject and Verb Agreement In this lesson, students will learn about subject and verb agreements. They will have a worksheet, so they can complete it independently after you have explained the lesson. To begin this lesson, you need to copy the worksheet I have included. Then, you can explain about subjects and verbs and how they need to agree in the sentences. After that, you can pass out the worksheets so the students can work on them independently. When they have completed their worksheets, you can collect them, so you can grade them. Study Sheet - Singular Subjects and Verbs 1. Megan went to get her journal out of her secret hiding place. 2. The rainbow was colorful. 3. Jenny's favorite hobby is writing. 4. I enjoy reading different kinds of literature. 5. Christy enjoys writing in her journal. Plural Subjects and Verbs When you have a plural noun, then you need a plural verb 1. Tom and Jim like to go to basketball games more than football. 2. Nancy and Penny enjoy staying at home and watching videos. 3. The children were glad that school was over for summer vacation. 4. The shoes were too tight. 5. Timmy and Susie broke their toys within two days. Singular Subjects and Verbs When you are writing a sentence, you need to make sure you use a singular subject with a singular verb and a plural subject with a plural verb. Subject and Verb Agreements Your subjects and verbs need to agree when you write a sentence. Let's look at the following sentence: 1. They were going to go shopping. (correct) 2. They is going to go shopping. (incorrect) In the first sentence, the subject is plural and the verb is plural. In the second sentence, the subject is plural and the verb is singular. That's why the second sentence is incorrect. Worksheet on Subject and Verb Agreement Directions: Choose the correct verb in each sentence. 1. They (was, were) not sure of the class assignment. 2. He (was, were) not wanting to go shopping with his mother. 3. I (am, are) excited about my new teacher. 4. We (have, has) been looking forward to the weekend. 5. She (is, are) taking online writing courses. 6. Tom and Mary (is, are) going to be married. 7. Sadey (likes, like) to play with her squeaky ball. 8. Christy (enjoys, enjoy) reading mysteries. 9. Megan (wants, want) to read Cozies and Capers. 10. The squirrel (plays, play) on the squirrel-go-round everyday. 11. The computer (work, works) great since Mary had it fixed. 12. I (wants, want) to read different subgenres of Crime Fiction. 13. The basketball player (score, scores) twenty points. 14. We (needs, need) you to memorize two Bible verses. 15. We (like, likes) to look at fiction books in the discount rack. Directions: The following sentences have some errors. See if you can locate all of the errors. 1. Ron likes to play the guitar, but he don't knew how to play bar chords. 2. Christy and Megan like going for walks, but they like watching movies 3. He doesn't enjoy coloring with markers, but he do like colored pencils. 4. The football game isn't going the way the coach wanted it to go. 5. The graduating class goes to the party after they graduated that night. 6. Abby and Sadey plays after supper until they went to bed. 7. The dark clouds form above and it started to thunder. 8. After the rain, Megan sees a rainbow in the clouds. 9. Abby brings in coffee for them and set it on the table. 10. The thunder was so loud that it feels like a sonic bomb. You can grade the students on the total number correct out of the total number possible. - Physical Education - Reading & Writing - Social Studies - Special Education [tape recorder starts making a strange noise] You want it out of the way? that's right, and he's using a... Title: How to Excuse Yourself from Homework Introduction: While homework is an essential part of learning and academic growth, there are situations where you may need to excuse yourself from completing assignments due to various reasons such as personal emergencies, health issues, or excessive workload. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive approach to excusing yourself from homework while maintaining your academic integrity and relationships with teachers. It's important to note that misusing these strategies can lead to negative consequences; therefore, they should be used responsibly and sparingly. Step 1: Assess Your Workload Before deciding to excuse yourself from homework, evaluate your current workload. Make a list of all pending tasks and assess their urgency and importance. Consider factors like upcoming deadlines, examinations, project submissions, etc. By doing this, you gain insight into whether skipping a particular assignment would significantly impact your overall performance. If so, prioritize other alternatives before resorting to excusing yourself entirely. Key Tip: Develop good time management skills to help balance your schoolwork and personal life more effectively. Allocate specific hours daily for studying and stick to them consistently. Step 2: Identify Valid Reasons If after evaluating your workload, you still find it necessary to excuse yourself from homework, identify valid reasons. These could include personal illness, family emergencies, participation in extracurricular activities representing your school, religious observances, or mental health days. Remember, fabricated stories might damage trust between you and your teacher, which can have longer-term implications. Guideline: Always aim for honesty when explaining your situation. However, remember that certain details are private and don't necessarily need sharing (e.g., medical conditions). Politely explain only what's required to convey your predicament appropriately. Step 3: Communicate With Your Teacher Once you've identified a legitimate reason, communicate promptly with your teacher. Request a meeting during break times or after class, depending on what suits both parties best. Explain your circumstances clearly and concisely without being overly dramatic. Express understanding about the value of the assignment but emphasize why it isn't feasible under current conditions. Key Tip: Be respectful and professional throughout your conversation. Show appreciation for their understanding by promising to catch up once things return to normal. Step 4: Propose Alternatives Instead of completely abandoning the task, propose alternative solutions if possible. Suggest submitting the assignment late, requesting a simplified version, or combining efforts on another project instead. Demonstrating willingness shows commitment and responsibility towards your education. Guideline: Don't make unreasonable demands. Understand that teachers also operate within set rules and policies. They may accommodate requests based on compassion and fairness, but extreme ones could strain relations. Step 5: Follow Up After Absence After missing school or failing to submit an assignment, ensure to follow up afterwards. Catch up on any missed lectures, review notes provided by peers, and initiate communication regarding making up for lost work. This proactive attitude reinforces your dedication to academics despite extenuating circumstances. Key Tip: Keep track of all correspondence related to your absence. Having written records provides proof of engagement and seriousness towards studies. Conclusion: Excusing yourself from homework requires careful consideration and tactful handling. While sometimes inevitable, regular occurrences reflect poorly on one's character and study habits. Therefore, utilize this option judiciously and focus primarily on improving time management skills and balancing commitments. Don't put your tongue out like a snake. Tropical rainforests are aptly named, as it rains here on about half the days. The only season in a tropical rain forest is summer, so plants grow for all 12 months of the year. Trees are tall and thick in the rain forest and they grow so close together that they seem to form a big umbrella of greenery called a canopy. This blocks out most of the sunlight. The air is muggy as it filters through the dense canopy cover of the trees. The light that filters through this tree cover is dim and green. Only along river banks and in places that have been cleared does enough sunlight allow plants to grow on the forest ground. She was very proud of her license. One day, Lila wanted to drive her car to the park. She asked her mom if she could go. Her mom said yes, but she had to be careful and follow the rules. Lila said okay and put on her helmet. She took her license and her car and went outside. On the way to the park, Lila saw a big, shiny car. It was a modern car with many buttons and lights. It looked very cool and fun. Lila wanted to see it closer. She drove her car next to the modern car and stopped. She looked at the driver. He was a man with a beard and glasses. He smiled at Lila and said hello. "Hi," Lila said. "I like your car. It is very modern." "Thank you," the man said. "I like your car too. It is very cute." "Can I see your car?" Lila asked. He said goodbye to Lila and thanked her for being his friend. He gave her a hug and a kiss. Lila got out of the modern car and walked to her car. She got in and put on her helmet. She drove her car back to her house. She parked her car in the garage and took her license. She went inside and found her mom. She told her mom about her adventure and showed her her license. Her mom said she was very brave and adventurous. She said she was proud of her. She gave her a hug and a kiss. Lila said she was hungry and sleepy. Her mom said she would make her some food and then tuck her in bed. Lila said okay and smiled. She ate her food and drank her milk. She brushed her teeth and put on her pajamas. She went to her room and climbed into her bed. She put her license under her pillow and hugged her teddy bear. She closed her eyes and fell asleep. Mount Garibaldi is an eroded, dacitic strato volcano in southwestern British Columbia. Both Garibaldi and Mount Baker to its south are part of the Cascade volcanic arc. Mount Garibaldi, 80 km due north of Vancouver, is made up of Mount Garibaldi, Atwell Peak,and Dalton Dome. This Pleistocene volcanic center is part of a volcanic field that contains some 13 vents in an area 30 km long by 15 km wide, much of which is in Garibaldi Provincial Park. Hydroelectricity is electricity made by generators that are turned by the movement of water. It is usually made with dams that partly block a river to make a reservoir of water. Water is released, and the pressure of the dam forces the water down pipes that lead to a turbine. This causes the turbine to turn, which turns a generator which makes electricity. The idea of what is a suitable place differs between species, but most build bird nests. Robins will make a beautiful little round nest of woven grass and carefully line it with feathers, bits of fluff and other soft things. Swallows like to nest near other swallows. They make nests from little blobs of clay, often on a beam near the roof of a building where it is well sheltered. Many birds like a hollow tree to nest in. Eagle's nests are often just piles of dead wood on the top of the tallest tree or mountain. Scrub turkeys scratch together a huge pile of leaves that may be 10 metres across. Guillemots lay their eggs on rock shelves with no nest at all. Their eggs are shaped so that they roll around in circles and do not fall off cliffs. A cuckoo does not make its own nest. It lays its egg in the nest of another bird and leaves it for them to care for. The cuckoo eggs are camouflaged to look like the host's eggs. I can be on America's Funniest Home Videos. Several lines of evidence also suggest that a rapid cosmic inflation of the Universe occurred very early in its history (roughly 10 seconds after its creation). Recent observations also suggest that the cosmological constant (" ") is not zero, and that the net mass-energy content of the Universe is dominated by a dark energy and dark matter that have not been characterized scientifically. They differ in their gravitational effects. Dark matter gravitates as ordinary matter does, and thus slows the expansion of the Universe; by contrast, dark energy serves to accelerate the Universe's expansion. [picks up a silver stick and stands it up on the silver circle on the black base] Lesson Three of the Bully-Busting Program: Using "I" Messages. An "I" message is a response to conflict that begins with the word "I", tells the other person how you are feeling, when you feel that way and what you would like them to do. We use "I" messages to be assertive and to help keep communication open. They replace negative messages that we often send that increase conflict instead of reduce it. Examples of "I" messages are as - I feel lonely when you leave me out. I want to play too. - I feel embarrassed when you laugh at me. I want you to stop. - I get annoyed when you kick the back of my seat on the bus. I want you to keep your feet still. Please use this blank form to practice "I" messages with everyone at home. I feel ____________ when you_________________________________. I want you to _______________________________________________. Even his simplest of grids can helps students fill up an entire paper when drawing. In this case, just four equal sections (by dividing the paper in half each way) helped set up the basic shapes of this kitty.1. Students draw a very light line down the middle of each paper both ways. A head is drawn in the top left section as shown. 2. A round body is added below. 3. Legs that curve back are drawn. 4. Arms that wrap around are added as shown. 5. A heart is drawn, behind the paws. 6. The tail is added. 7. Wings are drawn near the top of the body. 8. Details to the face are drawn, along with the clouds. 9. All the pencil lines are traced with a crayon, and kitty stripes are added as desired. 10. Liquid watercolor paints are used to fill in the shapes. [Disney Phone says "oh hi this is Mickey Mouse"] But I want the green shoes on 'cause there's green on my pants. " Money Comes and Goes This lesson printed from: A budget is a plan that shows how much money comes in (income) and how much money goes out (expenses). We use a budget to make sure we have enough money to buy the things we need and really want. A budget also helps us set aside money for things that we can't afford to buy right now. The money we set aside is called savings. You will learn the different parts of a budget. You will also create a budget you could use to reach a savings goal. Read the story Tim's Turn to Learn and answer the questions on the worksheet. When you are finished, you will discuss the worksheet answers with your class. Tim and Money Mouse reduced their spending in order to save money for the future. Another way they could have saved more money is by increasing their income. Read the story Heather Learns About Earning to find out how Heather increased her income. Then, answer these questions about the story: 1. What was Heather's problem? 2. How did she earn the money she needed? 3. What else might Heather have done to earn the money she needed? Now that you know what income and expenses are, can you find the income and expenses in this budget? A budget helps us keep track of our money so that we can use it on things we really need and want. A budget also helps us save for things that we can't afford to buy right now. A balanced budget has money in (income) equal to money out (spending and saving). Now it is your turn to create a budget!: www.econedlink.org/interactives/EconEdLink-interactive-tool-player.php?filename=em483_budget2.swf&lid=483 - Read the story, Alexander Who Used to Be Rich Last Sunday. Discuss with your class what happened to Alexander's money. Also discuss how you can keep from buying things that you don't need. - Think of something special that you would like to save money for. Use the "Spending Tale" to keep a personal spending diary. Then create a budget that will help you reach your savings goals. Title: How to Deal With In-Laws: A Comprehensive Guide ===================================================== Introduction ------------ In-law relationships can be challenging, but they don't have to be contentious or difficult. By following these steps, you can build positive relationships with your in-laws that foster mutual respect, love, and understanding. Remember, every family is unique; thus, some strategies may work better than others depending on individual circumstances. This guide aims to provide helpful advice while encouraging open communication and empathy throughout all interactions. ### Step 1: Establish Boundaries * **Explanation:** Clearly defining boundaries allows both parties to understand what behavior is acceptable and expected from one another. It sets the stage for healthy interaction by preventing misunderstandings and potential conflicts down the line. * **Key Tips & Guidelines:** + Discuss expectations early on in the relationship. + Be assertive yet polite when expressing personal limits (e.g., frequency of visits, involvement in decision-making). Tweetie was very happy in her birdcage. She had food and water and toys to play with. One day, a little girl named Lily came to visit Tweetie. Lily said, "Oh, Tweetie, you look so pale. Don't you want to come out and play with me?" Tweetie said, "I can't come out. I'm stuck in my birdcage." Lily said, "Don't worry, I'll help you." So, Lily opened the birdcage and Tweetie flew out. They played together and had a lot of fun. But then, the sun got very hot and Tweetie started to feel very warm. She said, "Oh no, I think I'm starting to melt!" Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's talk about a fun puzzle involving infinite sums of numbers. You might have seen or played with number sequences before - just like climbing stairs, where each step takes you up by a certain number. In mathematics, we call these "infinite series," which are expressions made by adding up lots (and LOTS) of numbers, one after another, forever. It may sound strange, but believe me, they can be quite fascinating! Now imagine two friends, Alex and Jamie, who came up with their own special infinite series using different sets of numbers: * Alex has $x\_1, x\_2, x\_3, ... , x\_n, ...$, and when they add them all up, the result goes on increasing forever without stopping - this means Alex's series **diverges**. * Jamie also created a sequence, $y\_1, y\_2, y\_3, ... , y\_n, ...$, and similarly, their series keeps growing bigger and bigger without end - so Jamie's series **diverges** too. But here comes the interesting part! When they decided to multiply their individual numbers together, and then added those products up instead, something magical happened... The new infinite series, formed by the products ($x\_1 \times y\_1$, $x\_2 \times y\_2$, $x\_3 \times y\_3$, ...), surprisingly started behaving well and actually settled down to a fixed number! This time, unlike Alex and Jamie's original separate series, the combined one **converges**, meaning it approaches some definite value as more and more numbers are included. To give you a better idea, think of having 50 sticks of equal length lying around. Now, even though you cannot pile them perfectly into a square shape due to gaps between them, there exists a way to arrange these same sticks into several smaller squares, leaving no gap nor stick unused! That's similar to our story today; sometimes combining things work out wonderfully despite initial hurdles faced individually. Isn't it amazing how two badly behaved series could produce a nicely behaved outcome? Remember, math often surprises us in mysterious ways, making it worth exploring further. Keep practicing your problem-solving skills, observe patterns, ask questions, and enjoy uncovering mathematical mysteries! This sheet contains instructions to help guide students through the experiment. Go through this experiment with students to help them visualize what might change on the earth's surface when the tectonic plates move. - Divide students into groups of two partners. - Have every group fill a pie pan with 1 inch of water. - Pass out a Styrofoam cup to each child. - Have each student tear the cup into about 12 pieces to represent the major tectonic plates underlying the earth's surface, and float them on the water, in turns. They have just modeled the lithosphere-the place deep below the surface of the earth where the tectonic plates are located. In the real lithosphere, the tectonic plates are floating on magma. Here they are floating on water. - Students should gently experiment with their Styrofoam tectonic plates. First, they should pull them apart. Ask them, "What do you see in the space where the Styrofoam pieces once touched?" (Water. In real life, this is magma.) What might it create? - Now, students should gently bump two plates together. Ask them, "What might happen on the surface from a bump like this below? Could it push magma into a mountain range? Cause an earthquake?" (Yes.) - Now, students should push one plate under the other (water squirts a bit). Tell students, "Remember, water is magma in our model. So what might you get here on earth from magma shooting up-a volcano?" - Now model the Haiti earthquake that struck in January 2010. It was caused by the motions of two plates grinding past each other in opposite directions. In the case of Haiti, the Caribbean plate moved east past the North American plate. It's called a strike-slip fault. - Ask students to experiment for five minutes in different ways with all 12 plates. Ask them to think of what would happen on the surface. - Now, ask students to turn to the nearest person who isn't their partner and show him/her one plate interaction. The partners should interpret it-that is tell what happens on the surface when tectonic plates behave the way you have shown them. Then students should trade places. Describing the Clothes We Wear - To use naming and describing words - To use increasingly complex sentence structure - To build vocabulary Dolls or stuffed animals; doll clothes Read any book about clothing. Make up riddles about clothing that the children are wearing. Examples: Mike is wearing this. It is red and has long sleeves. What is it? Juana is wearing these on her feet. They are blue and match her shirt. What are they? - For English Language Learners, help children identify and name articles of clothing. - Dress up a doll or stuffed animal in doll clothes. Pretend to hold a discussion with the toy, talking about the clothes it is wearing. Invite children to join in. Example: Well, Mr. Teddy Bear, don't you look nice! What's that you're wearing on your head? (in deeper voice) It's a hat -- a bright red hat. (in first voice) I see you're wearing the hat to keep your head warm... - Tell children to choose a doll or stuffed animal to dress up. Encourage them to engage in imaginative play, talking about the clothes the toy is wearing. Ask children what clothes are good for certain types of weather. Relate the discussion to today's weather. For example: Flora, why did you wear a coat today? - Proficient - Child describes at least three items or details about an outfit clearly enough that he or she is understood by most listeners. - In Process - Child has difficulty naming more than a single item or two of clothing without prompting. - Not Yet Ready - Child points to articles of clothing, but does not yet name them. Okay I will scratch broom broom on your hand. Title: How to Change the SIM Pin on Your iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the SIM pin on your iPhone. The Subscriber Identity Module (SIM) card is a small, removable smart card that stores data needed to identify and authenticate subscribers on mobile phone devices. A SIM pin adds an extra layer of security by requiring entry of this four-digit code before accessing the network services or making changes to the SIM settings. If you suspect someone else knows your current SIM pin or want to enhance your device's security, changing the SIM pin can be beneficial. Follow these steps carefully to change the SIM pin on your iPhone securely and effectively. Step 1: Open Settings App On the home screen of your iPhone, locate and tap on the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings.' This action opens the main configuration app where users manage various system preferences and settings. Step 2: Access Cellular Options Scroll down until you find the section named 'Cellular' or 'Mobile Data,' depending on your region and iOS version. Tap on this option to view cellular-related features and options. Note that if you are using dual SIM cards, select either one from the list shown here. Step 3: Select SIM Card Management Option Tap on 'SIM PIN' within the Cellular menu. Here, you can enable or disable the SIM PIN as well as modify its associated settings. Changing the SIM pin requires entering both the old and new pins; thus, ensure you have them handy before proceeding further. Step 4: Enable SIM PIN Toggle the switch next to 'SIM PIN' into the ON position if it isn't already enabled. Once activated, the toggle turns green, signifying that the SIM pin protection feature is now active. You may see a prompt asking for the existing SIM pin - enter it correctly to continue. Key Tip! Keep your SIM pin confidential and avoid sharing it with others unless necessary. Misuse could lead to unauthorized usage of your mobile services. Step 5: Modify Current SIM PIN After successfully enabling the SIM PIN, tap on 'Change PIN.' Enter your current SIM pin when requested, followed by tapping 'Done'. Remember, accuracy matters while typing the digits because incorrect entries might lock your SIM card temporarily after several failed attempts. Step 6: Set New SIM PIN You will then need to input your chosen new SIM pin twice consecutively for verification purposes. After confirming the new SIM pin, press 'Done.' From this point forward, whenever inserting the same SIM card into any compatible device, you must provide this newly created SIM pin. Guideline! Choose a strong yet memorable SIM pin comprising numbers only. Refrain from reusing previously used combinations or predictable sequences like birth dates or anniversaries. Conclusion: Congratulations! By following these simple yet effective steps, you have successfully changed the SIM pin on your iPhone, enhancing its overall security against potential misuse. Regularly updating your SIM pin along with other essential passwords contributes significantly towards safeguarding sensitive information stored on your device. Thessaloniki was a part of Ottoman Empire for approximately 500 years and in 1912 it became part of Greece when Greece acquired Macedonia following the Balkan Wars. Many famous people have lived or were born here, such as the Roman statesman and philosopher Cicero, many saints like Cyril and Methodius, who invented the Cyrillic alphabet, and Saint Demetrius, who is the patron saint of the city, and many politicians, such as the founder of modern-day Turkey, Mustafa Kemal Atat rk. Most of the city was completely destroyed in a large fire in 1917 and was rebuilt in the 1920s. Thessaloniki was place to both the Salonica and Macedonian fronts during World War 1, this fronts were made of French, Serbian, English, Italian, and a Russian division. This fronts were key in 1918 to the Entente victory over both Bulgaria and Austria. It was later occupied by Nazi Germany during the Second World War, during the Greek Invasion. Kansas has a total population of 2.9 million, with an area of , making Kansas the 34th largest state by population and the 15th largest state by area. The name of the state comes from the Kansa Native Americans, whose name comes from a Siouan-language phrase meaning "people of the south wind". The land that would become Kansas was bought in the Louisiana Purchase in 1803. Kansas became the 34th state to be admitted to the United States on January 29, 1861. Idaho is bordered by Washington and Oregon on the west, Montana and Wyoming on the east, Utah and Nevada on the south and the country of Canada (British Columbia) on the north side. Idaho is known for its vast mountainous landscapes, and it is literally covered from north to south by the Rocky Mountains. Idaho is also home to the massive Snake River, which comes from the Grand Tetons in Wyoming and eventually flows to meld with the Columbia River in Washington state. This river dissects Hell's Canyon, the deepest gorge in America, which lies the state's Oregon border, and flows through south central Idaho. This river provides a fertile plain which is the base for most of the state's agricultural industries, and the production of the potatoes for which Idaho is known. Title: Convincing Your Parents to Get You a Double Bed Introduction: Transitioning from a single bed to a double bed can be an exciting prospect for any child or teenager. A double bed offers more space, comfort, and can even become a cozy sanctuary for reading, studying, and relaxing. However, convincing your parents might require some planning, communication, and negotiation skills. This guide will help you navigate through this process with ease and increase your chances of getting that coveted double bed. Step 1: Understand Their Perspective Before making your case, put yourself in your parents' shoes and consider their potential concerns: - Cost: A double bed may be pricier than a single one. Be prepared to discuss budget options or ways to offset the cost (e.g., using allowance money). - Space: If your room is small, they might worry about whether there's enough space for a larger bed. Show them that you have thought about the layout and measured your room accordingly. - Age appropriateness: They might think you are too young for a double bed. Explain how you plan to use the bed responsibly and maintain its cleanliness. - Sleep quality: Address any concerns about sleep disturbances if siblings or pets share the room. Offer solutions such as separate blankets or designated areas within the bed. Step 2: Research and Prepare Gather information about double beds, including sizes, prices, benefits, and various styles available. Make a list of pros and cons tailored specifically to your situation and needs. For instance: Pros: - More comfortable for growing bodies - Allows for sleepovers without needing extra mattresses - Provides additional study or relaxation area during daytime Cons: - Higher initial investment compared to a single bed - Requires adequate space in the bedroom Step 3: Choose the Right Time Select an appropriate moment to approach your parents. It could be after dinner when everyone is relaxed, or during a family meeting where open discussions are encouraged. Avoid discussing it during arguments or times of high stress at home. Step 4: Present Your Case Using your research and preparation materials, present your argument calmly and respectfully. Emphasize the benefits of having a double bed while addressing their possible concerns. Use clear examples and explain why you believe a double bed would improve your sleeping arrangements and overall wellbeing. Step 5: Negotiate and Compromise Be open to negotiating terms with your parents. Some possible compromises include: - Contributing financially towards the purchase - Agreeing to specific rules regarding bed usage and maintenance - Waiting until a special occasion (e.g., birthday, holiday) to make the transition Key Tips & Guidelines: - Stay patient: Changing someone's mind takes time and persistence. Don't give up after one conversation; keep revisiting the topic tactfully. - Demonstrate maturity: Show responsibility by maintaining a clean and organized living space. This will reinforce the idea that you are ready for the added commitment of taking care of a double bed. - Highlight positive changes: Share stories about friends who made similar transitions successfully or improvements observed in their own lives since switching to a double bed. - Remind them of safety: Stress the importance of having ample space for restful sleep, which contributes to better health and reduced risk of accidents due to fatigue. By following these steps and guidelines, you can effectively communicate your desire for a double bed while showing consideration for your parents' viewpoints. With patience, determination, and diplomacy, you'll be well on your way to enjoying a spacious new addition to your bedroom! You want the kitty to take home? Yes Daddy wants to do the wash tonight. Creating tab groups in Safari is an excellent way to organize your web browsing experience, making it easier to navigate through multiple tabs at once. This tutorial will guide you through creating, managing, and using tab groups effectively in Safari. **Step 1: Open Safari** Begin by launching the Safari browser on your Mac or iOS device. Familiarize yourself with its layout before proceeding with creating tab groups. **Step 2: Understand Tabs vs Tab Groups** Tabs are individual windows within Safari that allow users to open multiple websites simultaneously. Tab groups provide a more organized method for grouping related tabs together under one title, facilitating efficient navigation between them. **Key Tip:** Use Command + T (Mac) or Control + T (Windows) shortcut keys to quickly open new tabs. **Step 3: Create a New Tab Group** On macOS Big Sur or later versions, click 'File' from the menu bar, then select 'New Tab Group.' Alternatively, right-click any existing tab and choose 'New Tab Group With [Tab Name].' On iPhone/iPad, tap the square icon next to the address bar, followed by 'New Empty Tab Group,' or create a tab group based on currently opened tabs. **Guideline:** Choose relevant names for your tab groups to easily identify their contents. **Step 4: Add Existing Tabs to a Tab Group** To add existing tabs to a newly created group, simply drag and drop those tabs onto the desired tab group in the sidebar. If you want to move a single tab, hover over its name until a small contextual menu appears, then pick the appropriate destination group. **Step 5: Manage Multiple Tab Groups** Switch between different tab groups via the sidebar located either on the left edge of the screen (macOS) or accessible via the tab switcher button (iPhone/iPad). You can close all tabs within a group by clicking 'Close All Tabs' when hovering over the group's name. **Step 6: Customize Your Tab Group View** Customize what information displays alongside your tab groups by control-clicking anywhere within the sidebar and selecting 'Customize Sidebar....' Here, you can opt to show favorites, reading list items, downloads, or other options as separate sections within the sidebar. **Step 7: Share Tab Groups** Sharing tab groups comes in handy when collaborating with others. To do so, control-click on a group's name and hit 'Share Tab Group.' From there, choose your preferred sharing method (Message, Mail, AirDrop, etc.) and recipient(s), who will receive a link granting access to view and edit the shared tab group. **Step 8: Sync Across Devices** With iCloud enabled, your tab groups automatically sync across Apple devices logged into the same Apple ID. Access synced tab groups similarly to local ones - they appear listed in the sidebar. In conclusion, mastering tab groups in Safari allows for improved productivity and streamlined workflow while multitasking online. By understanding the differences between regular tabs and tab groups, learning creation techniques, management strategies, customization options, collaboration features, and cross-device synchronization, you now possess the knowledge necessary to harness this powerful organizational tool effectively.
