Four basic forces govern the flight of an aeroplane: Gravity is the natural force that pulls a plane towards the ground. Lift is the force that pushes a plane upwards against the force of gravity. It is created by the movement of a plane's wing through the air. Drag is the natural force of air opposing an aeroplane's forward movement. Thrust is the force that opposes drag and moves a plane forwards. Thrust is created by a plane's propeller or by its jet engines. When a plane's lift equals the force of gravity and its thrust equals the drag, the plane is in level, cruising flight. When any of the four forces changes, the plane begins to climb, turn, or change its direction or position in some other way. GRAVITY AND LIFT Gravity tends to keep an aeroplane on the ground or to pull it to the earth when in flight. The force of gravity on the ground equals the weight of the plane on the ground. For a plane to become airborne and to stay in the air, its wing must create a lifting force greater than the downward force of gravity. Lift is created by a change of air pressure around an aeroplane's wing as the plane moves along the ground or through the air. Early attempts to fly with wings failed because people did not yet understand that it is the curved shape of a bird's wings that creates lift. After this fact was discovered, people began to build aeroplane wings that were slightly curved and so created lift in much the same way as a bird's wings do. When a plane stands on the ground, the air pressure above and below its wing is the same. As the plane starts to move forward, air begins to flow over and under the wing. The air moving over the curved upper surface flows in a curve. As it does so, its speed increases and its pressure drops. The air moving under the flat bottom of the wing moves in a straight line. Its speed and pressure are not changed by the wing. A high-pressure area always moves towards a low-pressure area, and so the air under the wing tries to move upwards to the air over the wing. But the wing is in the way. Instead of meeting the low-pressure area, the high-pressure area lifts the wing into the air. The faster the aeroplane moves, the greater the lift its wing produces. As an aeroplane increases its speed down the runway before take-off, its wing builds up more and more lift. The air pressure beneath the wing finally becomes greater than the weight of the plane, and so the force of the lift becomes greater than the force of gravity. The plane then takes off. DRAG AND THRUST A wing can produce lift only if it is moving forwards through the air. A plane needs engine thrust to create the required forward movement. As thrust increases, it moves a plane forwards faster than before. However, as a plane's speed increases, drag increases also. To oppose drag, the plane needs still more thrust. In a jet plane, the rapid movement of gases through the jet engine produces thrust. Propellers produce thrust in turboprop planes and planes powered by reciprocating engines. Propeller blades are shaped much like aeroplane wings. As the propeller spins, the air pressure on the front surface of the blades is reduced. The higher pressure on the back of the blades moves towards the lower pressure on the front. As it does so, it pushes against the propeller blades and moves the plane forwards. The faster the jet engine works or the propeller spins, the greater the force of thrust. To help increase thrust, engineers design aeroplane bodies to be as streamlined as possible. They give them a sleek, trim shape, and they design every part on the outside so that it will knife through the air easily and smoothly. CHANGING ALTITUDE - An aeroplane cruising in level flight has lift balanced against gravity and thrust balanced against drag. To make the aeroplane descend, the pilot must decrease the power of the engine. The propeller or jet engines slow down, reducing the plane's thrust. The reduction in thrust also reduces lift, and the aeroplane begins to move downwards. At the same time, drag increases its effect, which further slows the aeroplane and adds to the rate at which the plane descends. In order to climb, the pilot increases the engine power. The propeller or jet engines work faster, creating more thrust. The increased thrust also increases lift, and the plane begins to climb. However, climbing increases drag, and so the plane needs still more lift. To get the added lift, the pilot increases the plane's angle of attack--that is, the angle at which the wing cuts through the air. The pilot uses the controls to make the nose point up slightly so that the wing is at an upward angle to the path of the plane's flight. The flow of air over the upper part of the wing increases in speed, and the air pressure over the wing becomes less than the pressure over the wing in cruising flight. The area of high pressure under the wing moves towards the area of lower pressure over the wing, producing lift. But increasing the angle of attack disturbs the flow of air over the wing, which increases drag. The pilot brings the four forces of flight into balance again by increasing engine power to gain more thrust. CHANGING DIRECTION - A pilot turns a plane by increasing the lift on one wing or the other. To make a left turn, for example, the pilot operates controls that put the aeroplane into a left bank: that is, the left wing dips lower than the right one. Lift always occurs at a right angle to the surface of the wing. When the wing is not horizontal to the ground, lift takes place at an angle to the ground. As the left wing dips, the lift on the right wing increases, which pulls the plane around the turn. The pilot uses the rudder to keep the aeroplane's nose steady. The rudder is not used to turn the plane. It is the lifting force of the wing, occurring at an angle to the horizon, that makes the aeroplane turn. When a plane makes a turn, the amount of lift opposing the force of gravity is reduced. As a result, the plane begins to lose altitude. To bring the four forces back into balance, the pilot can do one of two things. The pilot can increase the angle of attack and thus increase the lifting force of the wing. The pilot can increase the engine power to increase thrust. The increased thrust produces greater lift. In making a steep turn, a pilot increases both the angle of attack and the engine power at the same time in order to keep the plane from losing altitude. Looking at the dwellings scattered amongst the woody flats and the plowed uplands, under the low gray sky which overhung them with an unchanging stillness as if Time itself were pausing, it was easy for the traveller to conceive that town and country had no pulse in common, except where the handlooms made a far-reaching straggling fringe about the great centres of manufacture; that till the agitation about the Catholics in '29, rural Englishmen had hardly known more of Catholics than of the fossil mammals; and that their notion of Reform was a confused combination of rick-burners, trades-unions, Nottingham riots, and in general whatever required the calling out of the yeomanry. It was still easier to see that, for the most part, they resisted the rotation of crops and stood by their fallows: and the coachman would perhaps tell how in one parish an innovating farmer, who talked of Sir Humphrey Davy, had been fairly driven out by popular dislike, as if he had been a confounded Radical; and how, the parson having one Sunday preached from the words, "Break up your fallow-ground," the people thought he had made the text out of his own head, otherwise it would never have come "so pat" on a matter of business; but when they found it in the Bible at home, some said it was an argument for fallows (else why should the Bible mention fallows?), but a few of the weaker sort were shaken, and thought it was an argument that fallows should be done away with, else the Bible would have said, "Let your fallows lie"; and the next morning the parson had a stroke of apoplexy, which, as coincident with a dispute about fallows, so set the parish against the innovating farmer and the rotation of crops, that he could stand his ground no longer, and transferred his lease. The coachman was an excellent travelling companion and commentator on the landscape: he could tell the names of sites and persons, and explain the meaning of groups, as well as the shade of Virgil in a more memorable journey; he had as many stories about parishes, and the men and women in them, as the Wanderer in the "Excursion," only his style was different. His view of life had originally been genial, such as became a man who was well warmed within and without, and held a position of easy, undisputed authority; but the recent initiation of railways had embittered him: he now, as in a perpetual vision, saw the ruined country strewn with shattered limbs, and regarded Mr. Huskisson's death as a proof of God's anger against Stephenson. Also selected are the president of Finland, members of town and city councils and Finnish members to the European Parliament. The elections are secret and direct. People vote directly for the person they want to be elected. In presidential elections votes are only cast for a person, not for a political party. All the other elections are proportional. The system is a combination of voting for individuals and parties. The right to vote is universal and equal. In general elections everybody has one vote. Not by the hair of my gum drop. This book was written to explore a fascinating piece of Canadian history, the construction of a navigable waterway between Lake Ontario and the Ottawa River, in the middle of the Canadian wilderness, during the early part of the 19th century. Attention is paid to the actual construction of the lockstations, looking at why each spot was chosen, problems that had to be overcome, and how they did it. This book can be enjoyed by those with a general interest in Canadian history, but it is best appreciated by actually visiting the locks. The history of the Rideau lockstations isn't just of academic interest. The locks are still working today, for the most part just as they were 170 years ago. You can go to any of the lockstations and look around, investigate, explore. After almost two centuries of use, many changes have taken place to the surrounding landscape. But if you look hard, you can still visualize what it might have looked like when the canal was being built. Stand on a grassy embankment and look down. It's not a natural embankment; it's a dam, built of rock, gravel and clay hand hewn from the ground, carried to the site by cart and wheelbarrow. It's holding back a head of water. Look around, see why it was placed there, what its purpose was and continues to be to this day. The water from the upstream side is flowing somewhere. If not through the lock, where is it going? The locks don't need power to operate, everything is done by hand. How does it work? How are the canal staff able to swing those massive gates, which weigh several tonnes, open and shut by hand? How are the locks filled and emptied of water? Why is the lock there in the first place? You see tranquil water both above and below the lock. What existed here prior to the lock being built? Why was it put in this particular location? This document answers some of these questions. For the rest, visit a lockstation. Talk to the lockstaff. Have them show you in detail how everything works. If you've come by boat, have a close look at the charts to see the layout of the waterway today. And, while you're visiting one of the locks, if you press your ear to the stone of the lock, you can still hear, very faintly, the sound of chisels on stone and the creaking of the ropes and pulleys that lowered these massive blocks into place. Randolph Peter Best or Pete Best (born Randolph Peter Scanland; 24 November 1941) was The Beatles's original drummer. He was born on 24 November 1941 in Madras, India to an Indian mother and an English father. His mother, Mona, later owned the Casbah Club in Liverpool, where The Beatles would sometimes perform. Best was drummer for the group from 1960 until 16 August 1962, when the band and their new manager, Brian Epstein, fired him and replaced him with Ringo Starr. The reason was because when the band auditioned for EMI, record producer George Martin was not satisfied with Best's drum skills, and planned to replace him on their recordings. Title: How to Deal with Escalator Emergencies Escalators are generally safe and reliable modes of transportation within public spaces such as malls, airports, and subway stations. However, emergencies can occur due to various reasons like mechanical failure, overcrowding, or passenger misuse. This article will guide you through handling escalator-related emergencies systematically and safely. **Step 1: Familiarize Yourself with Common Escalator Hazards** Before discussing specific emergency scenarios, let's review some common hazards associated with escalators: * **Entanglement:** Clothing, shoes, laces, jewelry, etc., getting caught in moving parts. * **Overcrowding:** Too many people on an escalator at once leading to pushing, shoving, or falling. * **Mechanical Failure:** Power loss, sudden stops, improper alignment, etc. * **Slip and Fall Accidents:** Due to wet floors, spills, loose items, or uneven steps. Being aware of these potential dangers is crucial in preventing them from turning into serious accidents. **Step 2: Stay Calm During An Emergency Situation** In case of any emergency situation involving an escalator, staying calm should be your first priority. Panic often leads to chaos and confusion which may worsen the situation. Maintain composure so that you can think clearly and make rational decisions. **Step 3: Stop Using The Escalator Immediately** If you notice something unusual about the escalator (like strange noises, vibrations, smoke, etc.), stop using it immediately. Press the emergency stop button if available and alert others around you to do the same. **Step 4: Move Away From The Danger Zone** Once the escalator has stopped, carefully move away from the immediate area towards safety. Keep children close and avoid stepping onto the still-moving handrails or exposed machinery. **Step 5: Assess The Situation And Call For Help** Try to determine what caused the emergency. If there are injuries, call emergency services right away. Even if nobody appears hurt initially, it's always better to have medical professionals check everyone involved. **Step 6: Follow Instructions Given By Authorities** Listen carefully to instructions given by authorities during an escalator emergency. They are trained to handle such situations effectively. Whether they ask you to evacuate the building, stay put, or provide first aid assistance, comply promptly and orderly. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Never try to fix an escalator problem yourself unless you are qualified to do so. Interfering with machinery could result in further damage or personal injury. * Always hold the handrail while riding an escalator but never pull or tug at it roughly. * Make sure all belongings are secure before boarding an escalator. Loose items can fall off and become projectiles or tripping hazards. * Avoid standing too closely behind someone else on an escalator. Give enough space for easy disembarkation. * Don't run, jump, or play on escalators. Such behavior increases the risk of accidents significantly. * Teach kids about escalator safety rules early on. Supervise young children near escalators at all times. By understanding these steps and following our key tips and guidelines, you'll be well prepared to deal with escalator emergencies professionally and responsibly. Remember, prevention is always better than cure - familiarizing yourself with basic escalator etiquette and precautions goes a long way in ensuring safety for everyone! Title: How to Be a Successful School Gossip Introduction: Gossiping can be a fun way to pass time and connect with others, but it's important to approach it responsibly. Being an effective school gossip means gathering information, sharing it tactfully, and maintaining relationships without causing harm or damaging reputations. This guide will help you become a successful school gossip while adhering to ethical boundaries. Step 1: Develop Good Listening Skills To gather juicy details, hone your listening skills by paying close attention when people speak. Show genuine interest in their stories and ask relevant questions to encourage them to share more. People are often more willing to confide in someone who listens attentively. Key Tip: Avoid interrupting; instead, nod along and make appropriate facial expressions that convey understanding and engagement. Step 2: Observe Your Surroundings Pay attention to non-verbal cues like body language, tone of voice, and emotions expressed through facial expressions. These subtle signs may provide clues about potential topics worth investigating further. Guideline: Always respect privacy and avoid prying into personal matters unless given permission explicitly. Step 3: Choose Appropriate Confidants Select individuals who are trustworthy and reliable as recipients of your gossip. Sharing sensitive information with the wrong person could lead to hurt feelings, broken friendships, or escalated drama. Key Tip: Before disclosing any secrets, ensure that the other party understands the importance of discretion and confidentiality. Step 4: Share Information Strategically When discussing rumors or private details, do so subtly and diplomatically. Use phrases such as "I heard this from a friend" or "Somebody told me..." rather than revealing your sources directly. This helps maintain anonymity and reduces the risk of backlash. Guideline: Refrain from spreading harmful or malicious gossip that could cause distress or damage someone's reputation. Step 5: Validate Emotions Acknowledge the feelings of those involved in the gossip. By validating their emotions, you demonstrate empathy and create a safe space for open communication. Key Tip: Use reflective listening techniques, such as paraphrasing what they said, to show that you understand and care about their perspective. Step 6: Keep Up With Current Events Stay informed about happenings within the school community by reading announcements, attending events, and participating in extracurricular activities. Knowing what's going on allows you to contribute valuable insights and stay relevant in conversations. Guideline: Steer clear of speculation and focus on factual information whenever possible. Step 7: Maintain Relationships Nurture your connections with fellow students, teachers, and staff members. Building strong relationships ensures a steady supply of gossip and enhances your social standing within the school environment. Key Tip: Offer support and encouragement to those around you - being known as a kind and caring individual can make your gossip more palatable and less likely to be met with negativity. Conclusion: By following these steps and guidelines, you can become a successful school gossip without resorting to cruelty or unethical behavior. Remember always to prioritize kindness, respect, and honesty while engaging in gossip to maintain positive relationships and uphold your own integrity. Find Out What Makes You Happy: A Comprehensive Guide ===================================================== Introduction ------------ Discovering what makes you happy is an essential aspect of living a fulfilling life. By identifying your sources of joy, satisfaction, and contentment, you can cultivate these experiences and create a more balanced, rewarding lifestyle. This comprehensive guide will walk you through various steps, techniques, and considerations that will help you find out what truly makes you happy. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Be honest with yourself during this process. It's crucial to acknowledge both positive and negative emotions without judgment or censorship. * Embrace self-compassion and avoid self-criticism when reflecting upon your happiness factors. Understand that everyone has unique needs and preferences. * Dedicate time and effort to this exercise. Discovering what makes you happy requires introspection, exploration, experimentation, and patience. * Regularly reassess your list of happiness factors as you grow and change over time. People evolve, so revisiting your findings periodically allows you to adapt accordingly. Step 1: Identify Core Values --------------------------- Understanding your core values provides insight into the aspects of life that matter most to you. These principles shape your thoughts, decisions, behaviors, and overall well-being. To identify your core values: 1. **Research common value categories**: Explore lists of core values online or consult resources such as books or articles about personal development. Common categories include relationships, career, health, learning, spirituality, creativity, leisure, and community involvement. 2. **Reflect on your priorities**: Consider which areas from the previous step resonate most deeply with you. Ask yourself questions like: * Which activities do I prioritize? * How do I want others to remember me after I pass away? * When am I at my best, happiest, or most fulfilled? 3. **Rank your top values**: From the reflections above, compile a list of your ten highest priority values. Organize them based on their significance to you. Remember that no two individuals have identical rankings-this is a highly personalized activity. 4. **Revisit your ranked values regularly**: As previously mentioned, people evolve throughout their lives. Periodic reviews ensure that your listed values continue to align with who you are and where you aspire to be. Step 2: Analyze Past Experiences ------------------------------ Examining past moments of genuine happiness sheds light on recurring themes and patterns. By focusing on instances where you felt genuinely satisfied, engaged, or elated, you begin to understand the circumstances surrounding those feelings. Here's how to analyze past experiences effectively: 1. **Create a timeline**: Document significant events in your life since childhood. Divide this chronology into manageable sections (e.g., elementary school, high school, college, early adulthood). 2. **Identify peak experiences**: Within each section, pinpoint occasions where you experienced intense pleasure, excitement, or deep connection. Examples might include achievements, celebrations, travel adventures, creative breakthroughs, or meaningful conversations. 3. **Analyze shared elements**: For every highlighted event, ask yourself probing questions: * Who was present? * Where did it take place? yeah, there are all sorts of pretend food over here. In this section, we're going to review some of the most basic rules. You can't avoid it. There are a number of basic rules that really do help you learn how to spell. Once you have mastered them, these rules will help you learn to spell a great majority of words correctly. Though many people will admit the wide variety of words in the English language can be a challenge to spell correctly, most will agree that a large number of words are obedient. In other words, they like to follow the rules! So, put these next few rules to memory and be prepared to find success. Do you know any of the rules already? A Quick Rule Let's start with one of the easiest rules to remember. When you spell words that have a q in them, always follow that q with a u. Some examples are queen and quiet. Two other words are quiz and quaint. There are only a few times when following a q with a u isn't the right thing to do, and that's when the word ends in a q, like Iraq. Boy, things would sure be simple if every spelling rule was so easy to learn. Check for Vowels Another rule that's easy to remember is the Vowel Rule. That is, each syllable in a word must have a vowel in it. Here are three examples: Each syllable in these rather long words has a vowel in it. Even short words follow this example, such as the word able. Able is two syllables, a ble. Notice that even the second syllable has a vowel, even though it is the sneaky silent e. If you have a syllable in a word without a vowel, it is probably misspelled. Studying three-syllable words is good practice not only with spelling, but with listening for vowel and consonant sounds. Here are 16 three-syllable words you might want to add to your ongoing spelling list. Some of these words, such as dinosaur, would be perfect to illustrate. Pick four of the words and draw a picture to help you remember the word. Here's a rhyme to remember when you are adding -ing to a word. For example, frame turns into framing. Can you think of more words where the e disappears when -ing is added? Here's another rule that's pretty easy to remember. When you have a word that ends in c, such as picnic or traffic, you have to add a k before adding -ing or -y to the word. Picnic becomes picnicking. We are going on a picnic in March. We enjoy picnicking in the mountains. Panic becomes panicking or panicky. I start to panic if I'm going to be late. Sometimes, my panicking makes me forget things and then I'm even later! My family tells me not to be so panicky. Traffic becomes trafficking. The policeman had to help guide traffic in the snow and rain. He was also watching for people who were trafficking, or transferring, fruits and vegetables across the state border. Words to Know Words are divided into syllables. Say a word out loud, and clap its beats. Good has one beat, or one syllable. Afternoon has three syllables, so it is divided as: af ter noon. You can also divide a word between the two middle consonants. The word dinner is divided in between the two n's: din ner. Again, notice how each beat of the word has a vowel. If you're not sure where to divide a word, the dictionary is the perfect tool to help you! There are five vowels:There are five vowels: a, e, i, o, and u. In addition, y is sometimes considered a vowel, like in by or hardy. Each word needs a vowel in order to be called a word. One popular word, I is made up of only one vowel! Lily and Ben were playing hide and seek in the park. Lily was the seeker and Ben was the hider. Lily counted to ten and then looked for Ben. She saw a big tree with a hole in it. She thought Ben might be hiding there. She ran to the tree and peeked inside the hole. It was very dark and she could not see anything. She heard a small voice say, "Boo!" Lily jumped back and screamed. She saw Ben come out of the hole, laughing. He had a flashlight in his hand. "Ben, you scared me!" Lily said. "Why did you hide in the dark hole?" Ben smiled and said, "I wanted to find a good hiding spot. The dark hole was perfect. No one can see me there." Lily frowned and said, "But it is not fun to hide in the dark. It is scary and cold. And you can't see anything." Ben shrugged and said, "I don't mind the dark. I have my flashlight. And I can see you when you come close." Lily shook her head and said, "I don't like the dark. It makes me feel alone and sad. And I can't see your face." Ben looked at Lily and saw that she was upset. He felt sorry for scaring her. He said, "I'm sorry, Lily. I didn't mean to make you feel bad. Do you want me to explain why I like the dark?" Lily nodded and said, "Yes, please. Explain to me why you like the dark." Ben took Lily's hand and said, "Come, I'll show you." He led her to the hole and turned on his flashlight. He said, "Look, the dark is not so bad. It is like a secret place where you can imagine anything. You can pretend you are in a cave, or a spaceship, or a castle. You can make up stories and have fun." Lily looked at the hole and saw that it was not so scary with the flashlight. She saw some rocks, some leaves, and some bugs. She said, "I see. The dark is like a game. You can use your flashlight to make shapes and shadows." Ben nodded and said, "Yes, exactly. The dark is like a game. And you can play with me. We can be friends in the dark." Lily smiled and said, "OK, Ben. I'll try to play with you in the dark. But can we also play in the light sometimes?" Ben smiled and said, "Of course, Lily. We can play in the light too. We can play hide and seek anywhere. As long as we are together." Lily hugged Ben and said, "Thank you, Ben. You are a good friend. You explained to me why you like the dark. And you made me feel better." Ben hugged Lily and said, "You're welcome, Lily. You are a good friend too. You listened to me and you gave me a chance. And you made me happy." They both laughed and said, "Let's play again!" They ran out of the hole and looked for a new hiding spot. They played until it was time to go home. They had fun in the dark and in the light. They were happy. [Spencer St.; at home with Daddy, S is just waking up] Title: How to Block and Accept Cookies in Internet Explorer Introduction: Cookies are small text files stored on your computer by websites you visit. They help websites remember information about your visit, such as login credentials or preferences, making browsing more convenient. However, cookies can also be used for tracking user behavior across multiple sites, raising privacy concerns. This tutorial will guide you through blocking and accepting cookies in Internet Explorer (IE), providing step-by-step instructions with clear explanations. Step 1: Open Internet Explorer Start by launching IE from your desktop, taskbar, or Start menu. Familiarize yourself with its layout, as understanding the location of various settings is crucial for this process. Key Tip: If you're using an older version of Windows, like XP, consider upgrading to a newer browser that receives regular security updates, like Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, or Microsoft Edge. Step 2: Access Internet Options Click on the gear icon at the top right corner of the window or press Alt + T to open the Tools menu. Select 'Internet options'. A new dialog box will appear. Explanation: The Internet Options panel allows customization of various aspects of your browsing experience, including cookie handling. Step 3: Navigate to Privacy Tab In the Internet Options dialog box, click on the 'Privacy' tab located near the bottom. It contains sliders and settings related to internet privacy, including control over cookies. Step 4: Adjust Slider according to Preference The default setting should be Medium, which allows most cookies but blocks some. To accept all cookies, drag the slider all the way down to 'Accept All Cookies'. Conversely, if you want to block all cookies except those necessary for website functioning, move the slider up to 'Block All Cookies'. Intermediate positions provide varying levels of cookie filtering. Guideline: Be aware that choosing extreme positions might break certain functionalities on some websites due to their reliance on cookies. Step 5: Advanced Button for Detailed Settings If you need finer control over specific types of cookies, click the 'Advanced' button below the slider. Another dialog box titled 'Advanced Privacy Settings' will pop up. Step 6: Override Automatic Cookie Handling Under 'Settings', uncheck 'Always allow session cookies' if you don't want any temporary cookies saved during a browsing session. Check 'Always allow session cookies' if you prefer seamless browsing without having to log into sites repeatedly. Step 7: Manage First-party and Third-party Cookies Decide whether to accept, block, or prompt before accepting first-party cookies (cookies set directly by visited websites) and third-party cookies (cookies placed by other entities via embedded content). Generally, first-party cookies enhance user experience while third-party ones may infringe upon privacy. Key Tip: Remember, restrictive settings could affect site functionality. Test different configurations until you find one balancing convenience and privacy. Step 8: Save Changes Once satisfied with your selections, click 'OK' on both dialog boxes to save changes. Restart IE for good measure. Your updated cookie policy should now be active. Conclusion: By following these steps, you have successfully managed cookie permissions in Internet Explorer, enhancing your online privacy. Regularly review these settings as part of maintaining healthy digital habits. She drew back quickly with a cry, half of fear, half of chagrin. "It won't hurt you," said Paul. She flushed crimson and started up. "I only wanted to try," she said in a low voice. Title: Access Smart Stay Using the Notification Panel on a Samsung Galaxy Device Smart Stay is a feature available on Samsung Galaxy devices that uses the front camera to detect your eyes and prevent the screen from dimming or turning off while you are looking at it. This can be particularly useful when reading, browsing, or using apps for extended periods. By accessing this feature through the notification panel, you can quickly enable or disable Smart Stay without having to navigate through multiple menus. Here's a detailed guide on how to do so: 1. Unlock your device: To start, ensure your phone is unlocked and you are on the home screen or an app. This allows you to swipe down from the top of the screen to access the notification panel. 2. Swipe down from the top of the screen: Place your finger just above the middle of the screen and swipe downwards with a smooth motion. This action will open the notification panel, which displays quick toggles for various features as well as recent notifications. The exact location of the swiping gesture may vary depending on your device model and software version. 3. Locate the 'Smart stay' toggle: Scroll through the list of quick toggles until you find the one labeled 'Smart stay'. On some devices, this toggle might be grouped under the 'Device maintenance' or 'Advanced settings' category. If you cannot locate the toggle directly, look for these categories and expand them by tapping on their respective headers. Keep in mind that if you don't see the Smart Stay option, it could mean that your device does not support this feature. 4. Enable or Disable Smart Stay: Tap on the 'Smart stay' toggle to enable or disable the feature. When enabled, the toggle should appear highlighted (e.g., colored blue) or have a checkmark next to it. Conversely, a disabled toggle will lack any highlight or display a gray background. Once activated, Smart Stay will utilize the front-facing camera to monitor your eye contact with the screen and maintain optimal brightness levels accordingly. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be aware that enabling Smart Stay may consume more battery life due to increased usage of the front-facing camera. However, disabling automatic screen timeout settings may offset this additional power consumption. * While Smart Stay works effectively in most scenarios, certain factors like low light conditions, wearing glasses, or sitting far away from the device might interfere with its ability to accurately detect your gaze. * For users who frequently switch between different Samsung Galaxy devices, note that not all models come equipped with the Smart Stay feature. Therefore, it's essential to confirm compatibility before relying on this functionality for daily use. I can stand them in bobby pins though. You going to have a red nose on your Halloween suit? Title: How to Clean Your American Girl Doll Cleaning your beloved American Girl doll can be a delicate task, but with careful steps and patience, you can restore its original beauty. This guide will walk you through the process of cleaning both the doll's face and body, as well as her clothes and shoes. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Soft cloths or paper towels 2. Mild dish soap (like Dawn) 3. Lukewarm water 4. Small bowl or basin 5. Toothbrush (soft-bristled) 6. Q-tips 7. Hair conditioner (for hair care) 8. Fabric softener sheets (for stuffing) 9. Sink or bathtub **Step 1: Gather Necessary Items** Before starting, gather all necessary items to ensure a smooth cleaning process. Having everything at hand prevents potential damage caused by rushing around looking for supplies while handling the doll. **Step 2: Prepare a Cleaning Solution** In a small bowl or basin, mix a few drops of mild dish soap into lukewarm water. Stir until suds form; avoid making the solution too soapy as this may irritate the doll's materials. **Step 3: Spot Test Before Full Cleaning** To prevent unintended color changes or damages, perform a spot test first. Dip a corner of a clean cloth into the soapy solution and gently rub an inconspicuous area of the doll's clothing, skin, and hair. If there are no adverse reactions after several minutes, proceed to full cleaning. **Step 4: Clean the Face** Dampen a soft cloth with the soapy solution and carefully wipe down the doll's face, avoiding the eyes and mouth. For tougher spots, lightly scrub with a dampened Q-tip. Remember to rinse often to remove dirt. Dry thoroughly with another dry cloth. Key Tip: Never submerge the head in water or wash the face roughly, as these actions could ruin the paint and inner mechanisms. Ya see the milk washes the French toast down. Title: How to Help the Homeless Enjoy Thanksgiving Thanksgiving is a time to express gratitude and share love with those around us. Unfortunately, many individuals experiencing homelessness are often overlooked during this holiday season. This guide aims to provide practical steps on how you can make a difference in their lives by helping the homeless enjoy Thanksgiving. **Step 1: Research Local Shelters and Organizations** Before taking any action, research local shelters, soup kitchens, or organizations that serve the homeless community. Understanding what services already exist will help prevent duplication of efforts and ensure your contributions have maximum impact. Contact these establishments directly to ask about their specific needs for Thanksgiving. *Key Tip:* Many cities have dedicated websites or social media pages listing all relevant charities and volunteer opportunities. Utilize these resources to streamline your search. **Step 2: Donate Food Items** One straightforward way to contribute is through food donations. Non-perishable items like canned goods (vegetables, fruits, soups), grains (rice, pasta), and packaged meals (macaroni and cheese, instant mashed potatoes) are always appreciated. Perishable foods such as turkeys, hams, pies, and fresh produce can also be donated but should be arranged ahead of time with the shelter or organization. *Guideline*: Check the expiration dates on all products before donating. Expired food cannot be used and wastes valuable resources. **Step 3: Volunteer Your Time** Shelters often need extra hands during holidays. Offering to serve meals, clean up afterwards, or even just spend time conversing with residents can mean so much. If cooking is more your speed, consider preparing dishes at home and bringing them to a communal meal at a shelter. *Key Tip*: Sign up early if there's an online registration system; volunteering spots fill quickly, especially around holidays. **Step 4: Create Care Packages** Put together care packages containing essential hygiene items (toothbrushes, toothpaste, soap, shampoo, deodorant, razors, etc.), socks, gloves, hats, hand warmers, granola bars, bottled water, and other useful items. These can be distributed directly to people living on the streets or added to the resources available at shelters. *Guideline*: Tailor the contents based on weather conditions and regional necessities. For example, include sunscreen in warmer climates and blankets in colder ones. **Step 5: Advocate for Policy Changes** Homelessness isn't solely addressed by charity; policy changes play a crucial role too. Use your voice to advocate for affordable housing initiatives, increased funding for mental health services, and improved access to substance abuse treatment programs within your community. *Key Tip*: Writing letters to local representatives emphasizing the importance of addressing homelessness can create lasting change beyond one holiday. **Step 6: Educate Others** Share your experiences and newfound knowledge with friends, family, and colleagues. Encourage others to get involved in similar activities, fostering a culture of compassion and understanding towards the homeless population. *Guideline*: Remember to approach conversations delicately, avoiding stereotypes or stigmatizing language when discussing homelessness. By following these steps, you're contributing significantly to making the homeless feel seen, valued, and cared for during Thanksgiving - and hopefully, throughout the year. Every effort counts, no matter how small it may seem. Happy Thanksgiving! thank you, God, for being great amen. You mash the orange one, and they come out here for change. Title: How to Create Stunning Wedding Hairstyles Introduction: Weddings are special occasions that call for elegant and sophisticated hairstyles. This tutorial will guide you through creating various wedding-worthy hairdos, suitable for brides, bridesmaids, or guests. We will cover three popular styles: an updo, a half-up do, and loose curls. Each section includes detailed steps, helpful tips, and guidelines to ensure clarity and ease of application. Section 1: Creating an Updo Step 1: Preparation Gather necessary tools and products, including a rat-tail comb, hairbrush, hair spray, bobby pins, hair ties, hairspray, and any styling products (such as texturizing spray, mousse, or gel) based on your hair type and texture. Key Tip: Start with clean, dry hair for better hold and longevity. Apply a small amount of product to add volume, smoothness, or grip, depending on your needs. Step 2: Sectioning Divide the hair into three sections - top, middle, and bottom. Secure the bottom section with a hair tie or clip. Guideline: Aim for even sections to distribute tension and create balance in the final look. Title: How to Change a User Name in Windows XP Windows XP is an older version of Microsoft's operating system, but many people still use it. If you want to change your user name in Windows XP, this tutorial will guide you through the process with clear, detailed steps. **Step 1:** Log in as an administrator To change a user name, you need administrative privileges. Make sure you are logged in as an administrator or have access to an account with these permissions. This allows you to modify settings related to other users on the computer. **Step 2:** Open Control Panel Click the **Start** button (usually located at the bottom left corner of the screen) and select **Control Panel**. The Control Panel provides access to various settings and configurations for your computer, including user accounts. **Step 3:** Switch to Classic View In the upper right corner of the Control Panel window, click on **Switch to Classic View**. This displays all available options in a simple list format instead of categorized groups. It makes finding specific settings easier, especially when dealing with user accounts. **Step 4:** Access User Accounts Scroll down the list until you find **User Accounts**, then click on it. A new window containing information about user accounts on your computer will appear. **Step 5:** Choose the account to rename From the Users section, click on the user account that needs renaming. You may see two types of user accounts - Local User and Limited User. Both can be renamed following the same procedure. **Step 6:** Click 'Change the name' link After selecting the account, look for the **Properties** option under Related Tasks in the left pane. Clicking on Properties will open another small window where you can edit the details of the selected account. Here, click on the **Change the name** link. **Step 7:** Enter the new username A dialog box labeled **New User Name** will pop up, asking for the new name of the user account. Type the desired username into the text field provided. Be mindful of character limits and valid characters while choosing a username. Keep it short, avoid spaces, and stick to letters, numbers, hyphens (-), and underscores (_). **Step 8:** Confirm changes by clicking OK Once you have entered the new username, hit **OK** to confirm the change. Another confirmation message might appear, informing you that changing the username does not affect any files associated with the account. Go ahead and press **OK** again to close the windows one-by-one. **Step 9:** Check if the change took effect Log out from the current session and log back in using the newly changed username. Verify that the correct display name appears during login. Also, ensure that all applications recognize the updated username correctly. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always remember to save important data before making significant changes like modifying usernames since there could be unforeseen issues leading to loss of unsaved work. * Use strong passwords after changing user names to secure your personal information and prevent unwanted intrusions. * Avoid sharing admin credentials unless absolutely necessary; maintaining separate user accounts ensures better security practices. Lane-Clayton's study was published by the British Ministry of Health. Her work on cancer epidemiology was continued by Richard Doll and Austin Bradford Hill. They used the same ways of studying cancer as Lane-Clayton, but they looked at a different kind of cancer: lung cancer. In 1956, they published their results in a paper called "Lung Cancer and Other Causes of Death In Relation to Smoking. A Second Report on the Mortality of British Doctors" (also called the British doctors study). Later, Richard Doll left the London Medical Research Center (MRC), and started the Oxford unit for Cancer epidemiology in 1968. By using computers, this unit was able to do something new and very important: it brought together large amounts of cancer data (pieces of information about cancer). This way of studying cancer is very important to cancer epidemiology today, and it has also been very important in shaping what we now know about cancer and what the rules and laws about the disease and public health are today. Over the past 50 years, many different people have done a lot of work to collect data from different doctors, hospitals, areas, states, and even countries. This data is used to study whether different kinds of cancer are more or less common in different areas, environments (for example, in big cities compared to the countryside), or cultures. This helps people who study cancer to figure out what makes people more or less likely to get different kinds of cancer. I think they're counting on you here. Part of Lesson Plan: Blood Spatter - Understanding the Clues Activity Overview / Details Let's start with the obvious. Blood, in the body is liquid and it is under pressure. Blood can be easily categorized by blood type and it also contains DNA. Knowing the blood type can help the investigator limit the possible source of the blood to a segment of the population. The DNA in the blood can often be analyzed to identify to a mathematical certainty, the individual who left the blood. By sampling the blood stains that were left in our crime scene we can determine which individual left the blood on the phone, the couch, the hearth and Sid's hands. Blood can also tell us whether or not there are drugs or poison present in the donor's system. This will take time however, and will most likely be done at the crime lab. At the crime scene we have blood stains and they form patterns. John Glaister, a pathologist in Scotland broke down blood patterns into six categories. Considering violent crime scenes in terms of the categories of blood patterns present allows the investigator to begin assessing how the crime may have occurred. The categories are: 1) Blood drops - Gravity acts on the blood until it impacts a horizontal surface. 2) Blood splashes - Blood that has been thrown through the air until it struck a surface at an angle. 3) Pools of blood - These pools are next to the body and may indicate if the body has been moved. 4) Blood spurts - This is the result of arterial bleeding. 5) Blood smears - These happen when a bleeding person is moved. 6) Blood trails - Blood that is deposited when a wounded person walks or runs while dripping blood. It can also happen from carrying or dragging a body. Let's see if we can associate these categories to a simple event. A person, let's call him Ed, is trying to make a sandwich. While cutting the roll he accidently cuts his thumb enough that it hurts and bleeds a little. Startled, he quickly lifts his hand from the roll and shakes it. Some blood is flung from the wound and hits the side of the refrigerator. That is a "blood splash" category 2. Ed takes a close look at the cut. It isn't bad. As he examines the wound another small drop of blood forms. As it grows and becomes heavier gravity overcomes the blood's surface tension and it falls striking the counter top. That is a "blood drop," category 1. Well, Ed thinks, at least he didn't cut anything major, like an artery. If that had happened the blood would be spurting out under pressure. That would be category 4, blood spurt. As Ed stands there thinking about what happened and trying to remember where he put the band aids, he continues to bleed. The drops begin to accumulate and form a puddle on the counter. This is becoming a "pool of blood," category 3. Ed leans forward and puts his hand on the counter as he opens the drawer where he keeps the band aids. His hand comes in contact with the small pool of blood and smears it; category 5. He starts to open the band aid but then remembers that he needs to wash the cut and put some disinfectant on it. The hydrogen peroxide is in the bathroom. As he walks to the bathroom a few drops of blood fall to the floor forming a "blood trail" which is category 6. Ed's mother comes home and sees the mess. If she thinks about it for a few minutes in terms of the categories that we have discussed she will understand where Ed was when he hurt himself. She will see that he stood there for a few seconds that he leaned on the counter and then walked down the hall to the bathroom. One further note should be made here. The faster the blood is moving, the smaller the drops will be. Speaking in generalities, blood spatter stains where the individual diameters are 4 mm or greater will be consistent with the velocity of the droplets moving at 5 feet per second or slower. This is called low-velocity impact spatter. When the diameters are in the 1 to 3 mm in size, the speed will have been in the range of 5 to 25 feet per second. When the stains are < 1 mm, the blood was traveling more than 100 feet per second. The resultant stain will look like a dried mist and will be consistent with a gunshot. Now let's conduct an experiment. Materials / Resource - Blood Spatter Terms.doc [ Download ] Terms commonly used in Blood Spatter analysis. My dearest! it won't last many months. Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's talk about something called "numbers." You might think, "Numbers? I already know all about numbers!" Well, today we'll explore some special kinds of numbers called "real," "imaginary," and "complex" numbers. Don't worry, this will be fun! First, let's remember regular numbers, like 3, 5, or 12. These are what we call "real numbers." They live on a straight line, where bigger numbers are further along the line than smaller ones. We can add, subtract, multiply, and divide these without any trouble. Now, imagine drawing a map with two axes - one horizontal (left-right) and another vertical (up-down). Real numbers take care of the horizontal axis beautifully! However, we need something new for the vertical axis. That's where our second kind of number comes in: the "imaginary number." It sounds mysterious, but it's not too complicated once we get the hang of it. An imaginary number is created by taking the square root of a negative real number, which isn't possible on the real number line. So, we made up a whole new set of numbers! The most common imaginary number is i, short for "imagi nary unit," which equals the square root of -1. Using i, we can now place numbers on the vertical axis, like 3i or 7i. With these two types of numbers, we can make a grid-a combination of the real and imaginary lines-called the "complex plane." So, here comes the big question: "Is 0 an imaginary number?" At first glance, it seems tricky because 0 has no effect when added or multiplied with other numbers. Also, it sits perfectly still on both axes since it is neither positive nor negative. While many people consider 0 as a real number, others say it could be both a real and an imaginary number. Why? Because it doesn't change anything! In fact, if someone said 0 was an imaginary number, it wouldn't affect math in any way because squaring 0i still results in 0. To sum up, numbers can surprise us sometimes, even seemingly simple ones like 0. By understanding different kinds of numbers, we expand our mathematical thinking and creativity. And who knows, maybe someday you'll discover your own unique type of number! Keep exploring and having fun with math! Who goes scratch scratch against the fence? Inspired by The Examiner's reports this is an un-missable opportunity to find out where precious marks are dropped. The leaves are the part of a plant where most photosynthesis takes place. If you cut a leaf in half and look at the cut end you will see something like this: - Waxy cuticle, this gives the leaf a waterproof layer, which lets in light. - Upper epidermis - provides an upper surface. - Palisade cells, which are packed full of chloroplasts. - Spongy mesophyll. Collection of damp, loosely packed cells. - Lower epidermis is the layer of cells on the lower surface. - Air space inside the leaf, allows contact between air and moist cell surfaces. - Stoma, a hole in the leaf which gases diffuse through. - Guard cells, which change shape to close the stoma. See the section on for more details on plant structure. One amazing feature of leaves is that they have tiny holes in them to let the gases carbon dioxide and oxygen enter and exit. One of the holes is called a 'stoma', although just to be confusing, when there are more than one they are called 'stomata'. A stoma is just a hole. It is controlled by two guard cells which change shape to either open or close the hole. Something makes water enter the cells by osmosis and so they swell up and change shape, but no-one is quite sure of the trigger. Why do the swollen cells bend? One side is thicker than the other, a bit like having a piece of tape on the side of long balloon and then blowing it up. Try this at home! The stomata (air holes) on plants are normally open during the day and closed at night. Photosynthesis happens during the day. Photosynthesis is the way that plants make their food using energy from sunlight. What is the word equation for photosynthesis? Plants use the green dye (or pigment) called chlorophyll to pick up the energy from the sunlight. Plants make sugar and use some of it for energy to keep them alive (respiration) but they also use some for growth and repair by making fats and proteins. However, it is not always sunny so plants need to be able to store some ofthe sugar they make, so they convert it to a storage carbohydrate. Why bother storing it as starch? Plants could use starch or glucose. Starch is insoluble (it does not dissolve in water) while glucose is soluble. This means that if starch is used, less water is required to keep its food stored. What things might effect how well plants can carry out photosynthesis? The amounts of water, carbon dioxide, sunlight and temperature. The amount of water is effected by how much is taken up through the roots and how much is lost from the leaves. If less water is available in the leaf then photosynthesis will occur more slowly. Similarly, if there is less carbon dioxide around then photosynthesis will occur more slowly. There wont be enough of the fuel (substrate) to get the reaction to work. If there is less sun, which usually means it is cooler too, then there is less energy for photosynthesis and it occurs more slowly. So photosynthesis works best when it is warm and sunny - don't we all! How could you measure how quickly photosynthesis is occurring? You could wait ages and then see how much glucose is in the plant. Boring and slow! Alternatively, one favourite way is to measure how much oxygen gas is given off. This can be done easiest by counting the bubbles of gas as they come off a water plant like the pondweed, Elodea. When plants have their stoma open during the day, they can let carbon dioxide and oxygen diffuse through them. However, water can also diffuse out from the moist inside of the leaf to the drier air outside. This movement of water is called 'transpiration'?. Transpiration is a bit like the sweat on our skin and may also help to cool the leaves. Large trees may lose nearly 200 litres of water a day by transpiration. On a hot sunny day plants could become dehydrated as they lose water even though photosynthesis works best in these conditions. They cannot draw up water from their roots quickly enough so photosynthesis starts to slow down. A big problem! So how do plants stop themselves drying out? They close their stomata. This happens because the guard cells lose water too and go floppy. A very neat trick! His wife is a rare help to him; they are doing all right." "I suppose he has some framed pictures now?" "Yes, lots of them; but you come in and look at the room the poor lad died in." I went in, and truly there had been a transformation. The ceiling was spotless, the walls were nicely coloured, the room was simply but nicely furnished, and there were some unframed pictures on the wall, but not those I had previously seen. He wanted to explore it, so he asked his mom if he could. She said yes. He walked to the edge of the pit and stared into it. He felt so small. He put his hands on the edge and looked down. It was so deep, but he wasn't scared. The boy knew he wanted to climb down. He asked his mom if he could and she said yes. He was so excited. He grabbed a big stick and started to carefully climb down. He looked around as he went. He could see all the way to the bottom and there were lots of interesting rocks and pebbles. Reaching the bottom, the boy felt so proud. He knew he had achieved something great. He showed his mom what he had discovered and she was so proud of him too. One surgery is removal of breasts (mastectomy). Some transsexual men also get surgery on their genitals. One surgery is removal of the uterus and ovaries (hysterectomy). Some may also get surgery on their outside genitals. One way takes their skin and tissue and makes it into a penis (phalloplasty). Another way takes the clitoris (which grows bigger with hormones) and puts it where the penis usually is (metoidioplasty). The place where urine comes out (urethra) can be run through the new penis. The phalloplasty looks more like a penis. A metoidioplasty has more sexual function, but it is 5 or 6 centimeters long. Some ways also make a scrotum from the labia. Title: How to Get to LAX: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the busiest airports in the world, serving millions of passengers every year. This guide will provide you with detailed information on how to get to LAX from various locations, offering different transportation options along with their advantages and disadvantages. By following these steps, you'll be able to reach LAX smoothly and efficiently. Step 1: Determine your starting location Before choosing a mode of transportation, determine where you are coming from. Different areas may require distinct routes or transport methods. Key locations include Downtown Los Angeles, Santa Monica, Hollywood, Universal Studios, Disneyland, Long Beach, and other parts of LA County. Step 2: Choose the best mode of transportation Based on your origin point, select an appropriate way to reach LAX. Here are some popular options: a) Taxi or Rideshare Services (Uber/Lyft): These services offer door-to-door convenience and can accommodate multiple passengers and luggage. However, they might be more expensive than public transit alternatives. To use this option, download the Uber or Lyft app, request a ride when needed, and follow the GPS navigation provided by the app. b) Public Transportation: The Metro Rail system connects downtown LA and other regions to LAX via the Green Line and free shuttle service called G Shuttle. While economical, this method requires transfers and additional time due to longer travel distances compared to private vehicles. Plan your route using metro.net and allow extra time during rush hours. c) FlyAway Bus: This express bus service operates between LAX and several locations such as Union Station, Van Nuys, Westwood, and Santa Monica. It offers comfortable seating and dedicated lanes but has limited schedules. Visit flylax.com/en/flyaway-bus for details. d) Driving & Parking at LAX: If you own a car or rent one, driving to LAX could be convenient if parking space is available near your terminal. There are numerous parking lots ranging from hourly to daily rates. Be prepared for heavy traffic during peak times. Check official LAX websites for updated parking information. e) Carpooling / Ride-sharing: Sharing rides with friends, family members, or colleagues reduces costs and eases congestion. Arrange meeting points ahead of time to avoid confusion. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always check real-time traffic updates before departing to estimate travel duration accurately. * Familiarize yourself with airport terminals and respective pickup/dropoff zones. * When possible, book taxis/rideshares in advance to secure availability. * For public transit, carry cash for fares and consider purchasing reusable TAP cards for seamless commuting. * If flying out internationally, allocate ample time for security checks and immigration procedures. Conclusion: By understanding all available options and considering factors like cost, comfort, and personal preferences, you can successfully plan your trip to LAX while avoiding potential complications. Central Writing Center Handout To, Two, and TO HAS DUAL MEANINGS: As a preposition: I am going to the store. Used before a verb: He is going to sing. TWO IS A NUMBER: I have two dogs. TOO HAS DUAL MEANINGS: Also: I am going too. To what extent, or how: How fat is he? He's too fat. CORRECT ALL ERRORS IN THE USE OF TO, TWO, OR TOO. 1. John is getting to comfortable. 2. He told me that he gives to the Salvation Army to. 3. If you want to go, you will have too talk to the captain. 4. This sweater is to old to wear to the party. 5. He is taking too courses this semester. 6. She invited me to, but I'm to busy to go. 7. I'm not to sure that I want to go. 8. To many cooks spoil the broth. 9. He remembered to late that he was supposed to buy too pizzas. 10. Be sure to tell her that I will write to her to. WRITE TWO SENTENCES USING THE WORD TO. WRITE TWO SENTENCES USING THE WORD TOO. The process of preparing a meal creates waste, much of which is thrown out as garbage. You have been studying Earth's resources and how humans can use them most wisely. This activity explores how the common task of preparing a meal at home can create an amazing amount of trash. - ingredients for making dinner (of any kind) - pen or pencil As you and family members prepare dinner, ask an adult to help you form a "waste pile." The pile is made up of all the scraps of food, paper, metal, or plastic packaging that would otherwise be discarded. Do not forget to put paper labels and lids for cans and jars in your pile. After you have eaten dinner, add to the waste pile all the uneaten food that would be thrown away. Now sit down and make a list of everything in the waste pile. Is your list long? Are any of the paper/glass/metal items recyclable? Could some of the food be used as leftovers? Could the rest of the food scraps be composted instead of thrown away? How would this benefit Earth? Think about the list you produced from your family's meal and about the number of families in your town. How much waste is produced each night an entire town as dinner is prepared? One useful feature offered by Expensify is the ability to enable digital signatures for expense reports. This adds an extra layer of security and authenticity, as well as streamlining the approval process. Here's our comprehensive guide on enabling digital signatures for your Expensify account. Step 1: Log into Your Expensify Account Visit <https://www.expensify.com/> and enter your email address and password associated with your Expensify account. Once logged in, you will be directed to your dashboard. Key Tip: Make sure you are using a secure internet connection (HTTPS) when accessing your Expensify account to ensure data privacy. Step 2: Access Policy & Settings From your dashboard, navigate towards the upper right corner where your profile picture or initial is located. Click on this icon and select 'Settings' from the dropdown menu. From there, click on 'Policies' found along the left sidebar. Choose the policy name you want to modify for adding digital signatures. Guideline: Remember that only administrators have permission to change settings within the policies; if you don't see these options, please contact your organization's admin. Step 3: Navigate to Advanced Settings Under the selected policy page, locate the 'Advanced' tab at the top center area. Click on this section to reveal additional customization options. Step 4: Locate Electronic Signature Option Scroll down until you find the subheading labeled "Electronic Approval". Underneath this heading, toggle on the option titled "Require electronic signature upon submission." Save changes made by clicking the green checkmark button at the bottom-right corner of the screen. Explanation: By toggling this option, all submitted expense reports under this particular policy will now require a digital signature before they can move forward in the approval workflow. It ensures approvers verify and confirm every report accurately and prevents unauthorized alterations after signing off. Step 5: Notification Alert Setup (Optional but recommended) In the same advanced setting page, look for the subheading called 'Notifications'. Within this category, turn on the notification alert for both 'When a new report requires my signature' and 'When someone signs one of my reports.' These alerts notify concerned parties about pending actions related to their digital signatures via emails. Explanation: Setting up these alerts guarantees timely responses regarding signed documents, reducing delays in the overall reimbursement process. Step 6: Test Run Create a sample expense report and submit it through the newly configured policy. Check whether the required digital signature prompts appear correctly during submission. Also, double-check receiving appropriate notification alerts concerning signature requests. Conclusion: Congratulations! You've successfully enabled digital signatures for your expense reports on Expensify. With proper implementation, expect smoother transactions, enhanced security measures, and faster approval cycles. Always remember to keep your login credentials private and regularly update your browser for optimal user experience. Happy expensing! On a baseball field, there are four bases. Let me try my best to simplify this concept so that it can be understood by grade-school students. Imagine you have a square piece of paper. You can do many things to this square - rotate it, flip it horizontally, or flip it vertically. Each way of transforming the square while keeping it congruent (meaning same size and shape) is called a symmetry. In fact, there are exactly eight symmetries of the square, including doing nothing! Now let's talk about something called groups. A group is just a bunch of actions that follow four rules when combined. Imagine having some toys, actions, or moves - like spinning a toy car or flipping a card - these can form a group too! Just make sure that combining any two moves gives another move within your set, every move has an opposite move that undoes its effect, starting with no moves applied should leave everything unchanged, and lastly, applying the same move multiple times doesn't change anything after a certain point. For example, think of clock hands moving in one-hour increments. Going forward ten hours then twenty hours would be the same as going forward thirty hours directly; also, turning the hour hand backward by three hours will reverse the effect of moving it forward by three hours. This set follows the group rules! Back to our square, we discovered earlier that there were eight symmetries. So, we actually have a group here! It's called the Dihedral Group of degree 4, often written as D4 or Dih(4), since squares have four vertices or corners. Now comes the exciting part - comparing this group with others! First up, consider Z8, the Cyclic Group of order 8. Its elements are numbers arranged in a circle, where each number points to its successor, and following the chain around returns us to the start. But unlike the symmetries of our beloved square, this cycle allows endless repetition without ever getting back to zero - quite different indeed! Next, S8 refers to the Symmetric Group of degree 8, dealing with all possible ways of rearranging eight distinct items. That's huge compared to our modest eight symmetries of the square! Our square's group isn't even close to being able to represent all those combinations. Lastly, let's explore whether the Dihedral Group of degree 4 could be a subset of S8. Well, yes! Any collection of movements taken from S8, which still obeys the group rules, creates a smaller group known as a subgroup. However, not every aspect of S8 fits neatly into our square's symmetries, making them only a tiny portion of S8. So, wrapping up our whirlwind tour of abstract algebra through arts & crafts: * The symmetry group of a square is NOT isomorphic to Z8 because their operations don't match despite sharing the same number of elements. * Nor is it isomorphic to S8 since the symmetries cannot cover nearly enough ground compared to the vast array of possibilities offered by S8. * Yet, the symmetry group of a square IS isomorphic to a subgroup of S8, albeit merely occupying a small corner among countless configurations represented by S8. Most seeds germinate underground where there is no sunlight. The plant does not need the nutrients in soil for a few days or weeks, because the seed has all the things it needs to grow. Later, though, it will begin to need sunlight. If there is sunlight, the plant will use it to grow healthy. If there is no light, the plant will still grow for a while, but its plastids will not mature: the chlorophyll does not turn green. If the plant does not get enough light, it will eventually die. It needs light to make food for itself when the reserve in the seed runs out. Title: How to Change the Time Zone on Your Apple Watch The Apple Watch is a versatile device that can be customized to meet your specific needs. One such customization is changing the time zone, which can be helpful when you travel or if you need to keep track of multiple time zones. This tutorial will guide you through the process of changing the time zone on your Apple Watch in detail. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** 1. Make sure your iPhone is nearby and connected to Wi-Fi or cellular data. Changes made to the time zone on your iPhone will automatically sync with your Apple Watch. 2. Keep your Apple Watch updated to the latest watchOS version for optimal performance and access to new features. 3. Be aware that some third-party apps may also display time based on their own settings; check those app's individual settings for adjustments as needed. 4. The steps below assume you have already set up your Apple Watch and paired it with your iPhone. If not, refer to Apple's official user guide for pairing instructions before proceeding. **Step 1: Access Settings on your iPhone** On your paired iPhone, locate and open the **Settings** app. It's represented by a gray gear icon and is typically found on the home screen. **Step 2: Select 'General' from the list** In the Settings menu, scroll down until you find the 'General' option and tap on it. This section contains various system settings for your iPhone. **Step 3: Tap 'Date & Time'** Within the General settings, look for 'Date & Time' and select it. Here, you can manage date, time, and time zone settings for both your iPhone and synced devices like the Apple Watch. **Step 4: Enable 'Set Automatically' (Optional)** If you want your iPhone and Apple Watch to determine the correct time zone based on location services, ensure the switch next to 'Set Automatically' is turned ON (green). If you prefer manual control over the time zone, proceed to Step 5. **Step 5: Turn OFF 'Set Automatically' (if applicable)** To manually change the time zone, toggle off the 'Set Automatically' feature. Once disabled, the 'Time Zone' field becomes editable. **Step 6: Enter Desired Time Zone Manually** Tap on the 'Time Zone' label and type in the name of the city or region corresponding to the desired time zone using the virtual keyboard. As you begin typing, iOS suggests matching cities and regions to help narrow down the search. After selecting the appropriate entry, the Apple Watch will update its clock accordingly. **Step 7: Confirm changes on Apple Watch** Check your Apple Watch to see if the time has been adjusted according to the selected time zone. To do this, press the side button to activate the power reserve slider, then press and hold the crown until the watch face appears. Confirm the correct time is displayed. By following these steps, you should now have successfully changed the time zone on your Apple Watch! Remember, any further modifications to the time zone via your iPhone will instantly reflect on your Apple Watch due to automatic synchronization between devices. Once there was a little girl named Sarah. She was three years old. One day, Sarah was in the kitchen. She saw some candy that was in a jar. "I want to eat the candy," she thought. But Sarah knew she wasn't allowed to have any candy without asking her parents first. She knew she had to use the words 'Please' and 'Thank you'. So Sarah asked her mom if she could have some candy. "No, Sarah," her mom said. "It's too much candy. You can have something else." Sarah was not happy. She really wanted the candy. But she was ashamed to ask her mom again. So Sarah used her sneaky plan. She reached into the jar and grabbed a handful of candy. Then Sarah heard a loud voice. It was her mom. "Sarah! Put the candy back! We don't take things without asking!" Sarah felt embarrassed. She put the candy back in the jar, and she knew she had been wrong. She never used candy without asking again.
