Title: How to Block Someone Who Has Already Blocked You on Instagram Instagram is a popular social media platform that allows users to share photos and videos with their followers. However, sometimes you may need to block someone who has already blocked you. This can be frustrating as you cannot directly block them from your account since they are no longer visible to you. But don't worry, there are workarounds to this issue. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to block someone who blocked you on Instagram. **Part 1: Using a Mutual Friend's Account** This method requires access to another Instagram account that follows both you and the person who blocked you. Make sure this is done with permission and respect for privacy. 1. **Log into the mutual friend's account.** Open Instagram on your device and log into the account of your friend who follows both you and the person who blocked you. * Rationale: Since the person who blocked you isn't following the mutual friend, you will have access to their profile through this account. 2. **Navigate to the person's profile.** Use the search function or visit their page via direct link if known. * Tip: If you don't know their username but remember some details about their profile (like bio text, picture theme etc.), try searching those keywords instead. 3. **Tap on the three dots ('...') at the top right corner of their profile.** A dropdown menu should appear. 4. **Select 'Block'.** Confirm when prompted. The person will now be blocked from the mutual friend's perspective which indirectly achieves your goal. 5. **Log out of the mutual friend's account.** For security reasons and to maintain privacy, ensure you log out once you're done. **Part 2: Creating a New Account** If method one doesn't work due to lack of a mutual friend or unwillingness to use someone else's account, creating a new account could be an alternative solution. Remember though, having multiple accounts goes against Instagram's Terms of Service so proceed with caution. 1. **Create a new Instagram account.** Download the app if needed, then sign up using a different email address than what you used for your original account. 2. **Follow the individual.** Once your new account is set up, find and follow the person who blocked you. 3. **Wait until they accept your request.** They might recognize your name or handle and allow the follow request. 4. **Block them from your new account.** After being accepted, go to their profile, tap on the three dots ('...'), and select 'Block'. Remember, these methods only prevent the blocked user from seeing your posts and stories. It won't stop them from messaging you directly unless you also report and block them from your main account or any other accounts you control. Always prioritize kindness and understanding while dealing with such situations online. You mean you're going to have everybody play? Or you could just climb on it. Once there was an old bird. He lived in the sky and loved to soar high. Every morning he would spread his wings and fly around. He was very alert and always kept his eyes open. One day while he was soaring he saw a young bird below him. He wondered why he was flying so slowly. He dropped down and asked the young bird why he was not soaring. The young bird told him that he was too scared and that he had never soared before. The old bird smiled and said, "Do not worry, I will help you." So he taught the young bird how to spread his wings and soar high. The young bird was scared at first, but then he had fun flying with the old bird. Soon the young bird was flying fast and high like the old bird. They soared together in the sky until they had to go home. Getting into a second hidden menu on a Motorola V3 can be an exciting discovery for any user looking to explore their device's advanced settings and features. This hidden menu is also known as the Test Menu or Engineer Mode, which contains various diagnostic tests and tools that are typically used by technicians during repairs or firmware upgrades. Before proceeding with this tutorial, please note that accessing these menus may void your warranty or even potentially damage your phone if you don't know what you're doing. Always exercise caution when making changes to system-level settings. With that said, here's our comprehensive guide: ## Step 1: Preparation Before starting, ensure that your battery level is above 50% to prevent unexpected shutdowns while navigating through the menus. Also, make sure you have enough time set aside since exploring these options might take some time due to their complexity. ## Step 2: Accessing the Secret Code The first step towards entering the second hidden menu is dialing a specific code sequence on your Motorola V3's keypad. Dial *#*#4636#*#*. Note that there should be no spaces between characters; they're just added here for readability purposes. ## Step 3: Understanding the Interface After inputting the correct sequence, you will see several tabs appear - Phone Information, Battery Information, Usage Statistics, and Wi-Fi Information. These tabs provide valuable insights about different aspects of your phone. Let's briefly look at them: - **Phone Information**: Displays general information about your phone such as model number, IMEI number, software version, etc. - **Battery Information**: Shows current battery status like voltage, temperature, health, etc. - **Usage Statistics**: Reveals usage data including call duration, SMS sent/received count, screen unlock counts, etc. - **Wi-Fi Information**: Provides details related to Wi-Fi connections like IP address, signal strength, MAC address, etc. ## Key Tips & Guidelines - Be cautious while changing any settings within these menus as incorrect configurations could lead to instability or malfunction of your device. - Remember all changes made in these menus. It would be helpful to write down the original values before modifying anything so you can revert back if needed. - Avoid sharing sensitive information (like IMEI numbers) publicly as it poses privacy risks. By following this detailed tutorial, you now understand how to access and navigate the second hidden menu on your Motorola V3. [3.4 sec silence and D coughs; evidently S wants to play in the water] Title: Understanding Averages through the Discrete Uniform Distribution Hello young learners! Today we are going to talk about something called "Discrete Uniform Distribution." Don't worry, it sounds complicated but it's actually quite simple and fun! This concept will help us understand averages better. Imagine you have a bag full of numbered balls, each ball has a different number from 1 to some number 'n'. You don't know yet what this special number 'n' is, but let's find out together! Now, you randomly pick one ball from the bag. The number on the ball could be anything from 1 to 'n', right? That's exactly what a discrete uniform distribution looks like - all possible outcomes (numbers on the balls) are equally likely. Now, here comes the exciting part - finding the average of these numbers! To do so, first, think about multiplying every number by its probability of being picked; since all our numbers have the same chance of getting selected, this just means adding up all the numbers divided by 'n' (the total count). In math language, we call this "expectation," which gives us the long expression below: Expectation(X) = sum of (number * probability) This equation tells us that to get the average or expected value, we need to add up all those products. But wait! Instead of doing everything step by step, there's a quicker way using something mathematicians love: formulas! They discovered that when dealing with consecutive integers, their sum formula looks like half of the product of the two end numbers plus one. We call this the "closed form for triangular numbers." Let me write it down for you: Sum of integers from 1 to n = n*(n + 1)/2 As you see, I replaced our sum with this cool new formula. After dividing both sides by 'n', voila! There appears the magical result of your expected value, also known as the average number of the bag: Expectation(X) = (n + 1)/2 Isn't it amazing how easy it became after understanding the concept behind it? Next time someone asks you about expectations or averages, share this awesome story of the bags full of mysteriously numbered balls. And remember, mathematics isn't scary; sometimes, it even feels like magic! So I thought he might be more interested in them. XXX maybe you can get him to tell me something. The flavours are up (u), down (d), strange (s), charm (c), top (t), and bottom (b). Up, charm and top quarks have a charge of +, while down, strange and bottom quarks have a charge of -. Each quark has a matching antiquark. Antiquarks have a charge opposite to that of their quarks; meaning that up, charm and top antiquarks have a charge of - and down, strange and bottom antiquarks have a charge of +. For most of its history, the band consisted of three brothers all of whom were born on the Isle of Man, a British Crown Dependency. The brothers, Barry Gibb (born 1 September 1946), and twins Maurice Gibb (22 December 1949 - 12 January 2003) and Robin Gibb (22 December 1949 - 20 May 2012), started singing at a young age when living in Manchester. In the late 1960s, the band briefly expanded to include Australian born Vince Melouney and Colin Petersen. When the band reformed a couple of years after splitting in 1969, Australian born Geoff Bridgford briefly became a member. The Bee Gees existed for almost 40 years They are most famous for their album, "Saturday Night Fever", the soundtrack for the movie of the same name. They stopped performing after Maurice died. The two other brothers reunited as a duo in 2009. In 2012 Robin died. They had a younger brother, Andy Gibb (1958-1988), who was also a singer. oh, is he going to go by airplane or is he going to drive the Toyota? If you don't, we can try to make it up. Fake Sick the Right Way: A Comprehensive Guide 1. **Assess your situation:** Determine why you need to feign illness. Is it to avoid an unwanted social engagement, or perhaps to take a mental health day from work? Understanding your motivation will help you tailor your performance accordingly. Remember, this guide is intended only for minor, short-term situations; if you're dealing with something more serious, consult a medical professional. 2. **Choose your symptoms wisely:** Select mild, believable symptoms that won't require extensive explanation or proof. Common choices include headaches, stomachaches, fatigue, or sore throats. Avoid anything too severe or requiring hospitalization, as these can lead to uncomfortable questions and potential consequences. 3. **Prepare in advance:** Gather materials that may support your claim. These could include over-the-counter medication packaging, tissues, or heating pads. Having physical evidence can bolster your credibility. 4. **Time your sickness appropriately:** Plan ahead by choosing a time when others are also likely to be absent or preoccupied. This reduces the likelihood of running into people who might challenge your story. For example, calling in sick during peak flu season may raise suspicions. 5. **Communicate effectively:** When informing others about your supposed illness, keep it brief and vague. Provide just enough detail to seem convincing without revealing too much information. Use phrases like "I don't feel well" instead of describing specific symptoms. 6. **Display appropriate body language:** Mimic the behaviors associated with being ill. Speak softly, move slowly, and maintain poor posture. If possible, limit eye contact and appear pale or flushed depending on your chosen symptom. 7. **Manage your digital presence:** Temporarily adjust your online activity to reflect your alleged condition. Update social media statuses to indicate feeling unwell, turn off location sharing services, and avoid posting photos or engaging in activities that contradict your claims. 8. **Handle phone calls carefully:** Be prepared for unexpected calls from colleagues, friends, or family members. Keep conversations brief and focus on how miserable you feel. If pressed for details, express discomfort discussing it further due to your distress. 9. **Rest and recover:** Take advantage of your free time to relax and recharge. Catch up on sleep, watch movies, read books, or engage in other low-energy activities. Overexertion can prolong recovery and compromise your cover story. 10. **Return to normal gradually:** Ease back into regular routines once you've decided to end your fake sickness. Begin by resuming light tasks before diving into full workloads or social obligations. This gradual transition can help mask any lingering doubts about your authenticity. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Always prioritize honesty and integrity. Only use this guide sparingly and for legitimate reasons. Misusing it can damage relationships and trust. * Never falsify medical documents or impersonate healthcare professionals. These actions constitute fraud and carry severe legal penalties. * Consider alternative solutions such as setting boundaries, practicing assertiveness, or seeking guidance from mentors or HR representatives before resorting to faking sickness. * Recognize that everyone deserves rest and self-care. Instead of pretending to be ill, consider advocating for yourself and taking genuine time off when needed. No, don't put it in the rubbish. " And a little toy house. [puts silver stick on one of the men] yeah, he had I'm sure Mrs Jana have all of 'em XXX... If you watch the moon every night, you see its shape appear to change. Does the moon really change shape? Of course not, but its appearance from Earth certainly changes. How does this work? The answer lies within the part of the moon that receives sunlight, and the part of the moon that does not receive sunlight. Let s look at a diagram of the Earth moon system to figure out how this works. Sunlight approaches the Earth-moon system from the right on this diagram (Click on the picture to start the program). Your job is to determine which half of the moon is receives sunlight, and which half of the Earth receives sunlight. Move the mouse on each part of the diagram to arrange the light/dark shades over each moon position, click to set the shadow on the moon. (The program will tell you when you have them all correct). Sunlight will shine on the side of the Earth (or moon) that faces the sun. We call this day on the side of the Earth that faces the sun, and night on the side of the Earth that does not face the sun. Keep in mind that sunlight will illuminate the side of an object that faces it! Click on the arrow in the corner to go to Part 2. OK, so what does this have to do with moon phases? Well, the moon "shines" by reflected sunlight. As the moon orbits Earth, sunlight "shines" on different parts of the moon on the side of the moon that we can see. We always see the same side of the moon. (Why? Because the moon only spins once per lunar month on its own axis as it orbits the Earth, so we always see the same side.) The key to understanding moon phases is: although the sun always shines on the same side of the moon, the moon is not always in the same place while orbiting Earth with respect to the sun. That sounds confusing, so let's work through a little diagram so you can visually see this and figure it out. Click Activity 2 above to go to the next part of the activity. The diagram still shows the shadows that you correctly identified before, but in this diagram, you will need to determine what the moon looks like to humans on Earth. It's all a matter of perspective. The green dashed line divides the moon into two halves - the half we can see from Earth, and the half we don't see. Look at the moon when it is at position (a). It looks like half of the moon is light and half is dark. Find and click the picture that shows what the moon looks like in position (a). If you pick the correct picture and information box will pop up telling you about the phase. You can close the box and move on to the next phase. Your job is to continue through all the phases, through (h), to determine if you can figure out which moon phase matches with the position on this diagram. After you get match the picture for all of the phases, Try answering these questions to see if you really understand the facts about the Earth-moon This part puts everything in action and lets you observe moon moving around the earth in it's orbit. You can click on the moon and drag it with the mouse. Click the "Start Simulation" button and the moon will move in it's orbit and the earth will spin on it's axis. (The timing of the simulation is scaled accuratly, The earth makes a complete rotation 29.5 times in the time it takes the moon to go through a complete cycle). You can also click on the number buttons to jump to a certain day in the moon's phase cycle. Or click on the phase buttons 'First Quarter', 'New Moon', etc. It's just a XXX see it's a long one. XXX I think it might be a beet. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/10/4825GG.cha = = = Title: How to Find a Snakeskin Finding a snakeskin can be an exciting discovery, especially if you're interested in herpetology or just enjoy exploring nature. However, it requires patience, keen observation skills, and knowledge about snake behavior. This tutorial will guide you through the process of finding a snakeskin, providing detailed steps, useful tips, and important guidelines. ### Step 1: Understand Snake Shedding Snakes shed their skin periodically as they grow. The frequency of shedding depends on factors like age, size, health status, and species. On average, young snakes may shed every few weeks, while adults might do so less frequently - perhaps once or twice a year. When a snake sheds, it leaves behind its old skin, which looks like a translucent tube with some scales still attached at certain points. Key Tip: Look for shed skins during spring and fall when most snakes are actively growing. ### Step 2: Identify Snake Habitats To find a snakeskin, you need to know where snakes live. They prefer habitats that offer food sources (rodents, insects, amphibians), shelter, and warmth. These include forests, grasslands, deserts, swamps, marshes, rocky areas, and even gardens. Some common places to look for snakes are under rocks, logs, debris, or near water bodies. Guideline: Always respect private property; get permission before searching for snakeskins in someone else's yard or land. ### Step 3: Observe Safety Measures While looking for snakeskins, there is always a chance of encountering a live snake. Therefore, familiarize yourself with local snake species, including venomous ones. Never try to handle any snake without proper training and equipment. Maintain a safe distance and observe from afar. Tip: Carry a field guide or use a smartphone app to help identify different types of snakes. ### Step 4: Search During Active Hours Snakes are more active during dawn and dusk, making these times ideal for your search. Be prepared to spend several hours outdoors, moving slowly and carefully inspecting potential hideouts. Use a flashlight at night to illuminate dark spaces. Important Note: Avoid disturbing snakes during breeding season (usually late summer) because males often travel great distances in search of females. ### Step 5: Locate Discarded Skins Once you've identified likely snake hiding spots, start examining them closely. Snakeskin sheds are usually found curled up starting from the head end. They'll appear pale yellowish-white or grayish, depending on the snake's coloration. If you see what appears to be a complete tubular structure with distinct scale patterns, congratulations! You've found a snakeskin. Additional Tips: * Check both sides of objects since snakes can shed underneath as well as on top. * Don't overlook small branches, crevices, or holes in tree trunks. * Remember that snakes don't only inhabit rural areas; urban parks and gardens can also host snakes. In conclusion, finding a snakeskin involves understanding snake biology, identifying suitable habitats, observing safety measures, searching during active hours, and locating discarded skins. With patience and practice, you'll increase your chances of success. Happy hunting! that," said Mrs. Brigham. "She doesn't act like herself at all. Title: How to Check Your Windshield Wiper Fluid Introduction: Keeping your windshield wiper fluid at an appropriate level is essential for maintaining optimal visibility while driving, especially during inclement weather conditions. This guide will provide you with a comprehensive set of instructions on checking and refilling your vehicle's windshield washer fluid reservoir. By following these steps, you can ensure that your car remains safe and functional throughout its lifespan. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Perform this check regularly (at least once a month) or whenever you notice reduced performance from your windshield wipers. * Always use the recommended type of windshield wiper fluid specified by your vehicle manufacturer; using improper fluids may damage components or reduce effectiveness. * If you are unsure about any aspect of this procedure, consult your owner's manual or seek assistance from a professional mechanic. Step 1: Gather Necessary Materials Before beginning the process, gather all necessary materials to complete the task efficiently. You will need: * A funnel suitable for automotive applications (preferably one designed specifically for filling windshield washer fluid). * The correct type of windshield wiper fluid as indicated by your vehicle owner's manual. * Protective gloves (optional but recommended) to prevent skin irritation caused by contact with cleaning solutions. * Clean rags or paper towels to manage spills and maintain a clean work area. Step 2: Locate the Windshield Washer Reservoir The first step in checking your windshield wiper fluid is locating the proper reservoir. Typically found near the engine bay, the reservoir features a cap labeled "Washer Fluid" or similar markings. Consult your owner's manual if you have difficulty finding the location. Step 3: Examine Current Fluid Level Once you have identified the windshield washer fluid reservoir, carefully remove the cap. Observe the current fluid level within the container - most reservoirs include minimum and maximum fill lines molded into their interior surfaces. If the fluid level falls below the minimum line, proceed to Step 4. Step 4: Refill the Reservoir To add new windshield wiper fluid, position the funnel securely over the opening of the reservoir. Slowly pour the appropriate fluid through the funnel until it reaches the maximum fill line. Avoid overflowing the reservoir, which could lead to unnecessary mess and potential waste. Step 5: Replace Cap Securely After completing the refill process, replace the cap firmly onto the windshield washer fluid reservoir. Ensuring a snug fit prevents leaks and maintains the longevity of the component. Step 6: Test Functionality (Optional) For added assurance, test the functionality of your newly-filled windshield washer system. Turn on your vehicle's ignition and activate the windshield wipers along with the wash function. Verify that the spray nozzles distribute an adequate amount of fluid across the entirety of the glass surface. Conclusion: By adhering to this thorough guide, you have successfully checked and potentially refilled your windshield wiper fluid. Regular maintenance ensures safety and reliability while operating your vehicle, providing peace of mind for both yourself and other drivers sharing the road. Look at the thing carrying the wagon. All plays must start between the hashmarks-if the last play ended outside the hashmarks, the ball is moved to the nearest hashmark. At the ends of the field there are scoring areas, called the end zones. Neptune cannot be seen with naked eye alone, since Neptune's normal brightness are between magnitudes +7.7 and +8.0. It can be out-shined by Jupiter's Galilean moons, the dwarf planet Ceres, and the asteroids 4 Vesta, 2 Pallas, 7 Iris, 3 Juno and 6 Hebe. A telescope or strong binoculars will show Neptune as a small blue dot that looks similar to Uranus. The blue color comes from the methane in its atmosphere. Its small obvious size has made it difficult to study visually. Central coherence is the ability to build wholes out of parts. It is weak in people with autism. This means that autistic people focus more on details. They cannot build wholes out of them. Not being able to pay attention to wholes results in difficulties with perception and language. Therefore, people with autism usually need more time for dealing with information coming from their surroundings. As a consequence, they typically also need more time for responding. However, performance varies between people on the autism spectrum. Moreover, the term central coherence may include many interacting aspects. This makes it difficult to find the cause for it in the brain. Neural correlates for central coherence are not yet known. Elementary financial records are known from the beginning of history. Compound Complex Sentence When a sentence contains two or more independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses, the sentence is called a compound complex sentence. Before we go into details of compound complex sentences, it is better to have an idea of the simple, compound & the complex sentence. Let us examine the following sentences: Anne won the race. The road was clear and we started going. John did not pass the exam since he did not study hard. James tried hard but he could not succeed since he had no much experience. Look at that the first sentence has only one Subject and one Predicate. Such a sentence is called a simple sentence. Look at the second sentence. It contains two parts: The road was clear. We started going. These two parts are joined by a coordinating conjunction and. Each of the two sentences contains a Subject & a predicate of its own. Each part is therefore a sentence which is a part of a large sentence. In other words, each part is what we call a Clause. We can further notice that each of the two clauses makes good sense by itself and therefore can stand by itself as a complete sentence. Each clause is independent of the other and therefore called an Independent Clause. Note that there are several coordinating conjunctions other than and which can be used to join two independent clauses such as but, for, or, nor, yet & so. Also the punctuation mark semicolon (;) can function as a coordinating conjunction. Therefore a sentence such as the second which is made up of two or more independent clauses is called a compound sentence. Look at the third sentence, it too has two parts: John did not pass the exam. Since he did not study hard. Each part contains a subject & a predicate of its own and forma part of a large sentence. Each part is therefore a clause. When we further analyze, we can notice that the first part "John did not pass the exam" makes good sense by itself and can stand by itself as a complete sentence. It is therefore an independent clause. However the second part "since he did not study hard" cannot stand by itself and doesn't make good sense unless it is read with the first part. It is dependent on the first part. It is therefore called a dependent or subordinate clause. A dependent clause may be a noun clause, adjective clause or adverb clause from grammatical point of view. Please note the analysis of clauses is outside the scope of this site. Therefore a sentence such as the third which is made up of one independent clause and one or more dependent clauses is called a complex sentence. Now look at the fourth sentence, it has 03 parts (or clauses): James tried hard. He could not succeed. Since he had no much experience. The first & the second clause both can stand on its own and make good senses by themselves and therefore are complete sentences. In other words, if we take the first two parts, "James tried hard but he could not succeed" this is a compound sentence as explained previously but there is a third clause which is an independent clause which will make sense only if it is read with the second clause. Therefore a sentence such as the fourth which is made up of two or more independent clauses with one or more dependent clauses is called a compound complex sentence. Let us look at some compound complex sentence examples in the next page. [and continues to color and manipulate the crayons] " You wouldn't hit a girl with eyeglasses." Title: How to Have a Diary Having a diary is an excellent way to organize your thoughts, track personal growth, and record memories. This guide will walk you through the process of starting and maintaining a diary, providing clear steps along with helpful tips and guidelines. By following these instructions, you'll be able to enjoy the numerous benefits that come from keeping a regular journaling practice. ### Part 1: Choosing Your Diary #### Step 1: Decide between physical or digital diaries Physical diaries offer tactile engagement and can be customized with various styles and formats. Digital diaries provide convenience, accessibility, and backup options. Consider which format suits your preferences and lifestyle better before proceeding. #### Step 2: Select a suitable physical diary If opting for a physical diary, consider factors such as size, durability, layout, and design. Choose one that feels comfortable to write in, has enough space for daily entries, and reflects your personality. Some popular types include lined notebooks, bound journals, and refillable leather-bound diaries. #### Step 3: Pick a digital diary platform For those who prefer digital tools, explore different applications and websites designed specifically for journaling. Popular choices include Day One (Mac & iOS), Journey (Android & iOS), Penzu (Web-based), and Evernote (Multiplatform). Evaluate their features, pricing, and compatibility across devices before deciding. ### Part 2: Setting Up Your Diary #### Step 4: Create a consistent routine That's a rabbit too, and what's this? oh, somethin' goes in the little circle. Machine code is a computer program written in machine language. It uses the instruction set of a particular computer architecture. It is usually written in binary. Machine code is the lowest level of software. Other programming languages are translated into machine code so the computer can execute them. Solving equations with radicals, no matter what power, involves isolating the radical on one side of the equation and then raising both sides of the equation to the power of the radical. When solving radicals, the final step is to isolate the variable. If there are more than one radical, we isolate and remove one root, then isolate and remove the other root.finally, we solve the remaining equation for the variable. Solving equations with a radical. So there's always a couple of steps that we have to do when we're solving equations with radicals, and those are always going to be pretty much the same thing. First thing we want to do is isolate our radicals. So get the square roots or cube root or whatever it is by itself. Second we need to get rid of the root and so we're going to take each side to a power. If it has a square root, we want to square both sides, if we have a cube root we want to square both sides so on and so forth. Okay? And the we just want to solve it out. And lastly we always have to check our answers to make sure they work. So behind me I have a pretty straight forward example and we're going to solve this out, okay? So first thing we want to get our square root by itself. What we have to do is add 3 to both sides. Square root of x is equal to 5. In order to get rid of the square root we need to square. We square one side, we have to square the other to keep it balanced and so we end up with x is equal to 25, okay? Common mistake is that when we square things people want to put plus or minus. You square something it always turns out positive okay. So it's just that one positive number giving us x25 then we need to check make sure it works. Plug in 25. Square root 25 is 5 minus 3 is 2, okay? So by going through our steps, isolate our square root, take it to the appropriate power, solve and check. We're able to solve any equation with a radical. Daddy I don't need you to unzip my zipper, and I'm mad at you. A monophthong is a simple vowel sound that a person does not have to move his or her mouth to make, like the "oo" sound in "book." In a diphthong, the person combines two different monophthongs, as with the "oi" sound in the word "oil". The speaker starts with the mouth in the position to make an "o" sound, then quickly moves the mouth to make a hard "e" sound. Another example is the "ou" sound in the word "house". The mouth starts out making a sound like the soft "a" sound in "flat", then moves to make the a hard "oo" sound like the one in "caboose". James Watt (19 January 1736 19 August 1819) was a Scottish mathematician and engineer. He did not invent the first steam engine, but he did modify it to work better. There were other patented steam engines (such as the Savery and Newcomen steam engines) in use by the time Watt started his work on them. His major contribution is in developing the modern form of the steam engine. The Watt steam engine is credited for driving the Industrial Revolution of the 18th and 19th centuries. He said that um she was so loud she could break up the whole world. The scientific name of the red-eyed tree frog is Agalychnis Callidryas. It is also commonly called the red-eyed leaf frog. This amphibian's natural habitat is found in tropical rain forests located in southern Mexico, Central America and northern South America. Contrary to most frog species, the red-eyed tree frog lives in trees instead of ponds and plants located on the shore of these water sources. The red-eyed tree frog earned its name from the red coloration of its eyes, which also have a vertical pupil. Its body is bright green, its toes are orange and its flanks and thighs display a pattern of bright blue and yellow stripes. While resting during the day, this type of frog lies against the bottom of a tree leaf. It closes its eyes and hides its markings in order to camouflage itself with the color of the leaf. This allows the red-eyed frog to escape the predators' sight. When facing a predator, the red-eyed tree frog opens its eyes and displays all its markings in order to shock the predator. Several scientists believe that such a bright display of colors often causes the predator to be startled. This short time is often enough to allow the frog to jump away from the threat. Their natural predators are: snakes, birds, bats and spiders. Since the red-eyed tree frog is an arboreal frog species, it explains why it is such a great jumper. This type of frog is known to slightly change the color of its body and markings in order to allow it to camouflage itself better with its natural surroundings or depending on its mood. The size of the red-eyed tree frog varies between 5.08 cm to 6.35 cm (2 to 2 1/2 inches) for a male and 6.35 cm to 7.62 cm (2 1/2 an 3 inches) in length. The skin on its stomach is more fragile than the skin on its back. Juvenile frogs are dark brown and reach this neon-green coloration as they become older. When ready to mate, the red-eyed tree frog uses vibration to warn its rivals. It will also croak some deep sounds as a warning sign and high sounds to attract females for mating. Once the male gets the female's attention, the female will carry him on its back for several hours, during the mating process. She will then deposit a clutch of eggs on a leaf, located over a water source, usually a pond or a puddle of water. A few days later, the tadpoles hatch and fall into the water where they will face dragonflies, fish and water beetles as predators. For the 3 weeks following their birth, the tadpoles will live in the water until they go through the metamorphosis (this process can take up to a few months). The juvenile frogs who survived until then, will move out of the water and settle in tubular water plants such as the bromeliads. The juvenile frogs will feed on small flies and insects for the first months of their lives. As they grow, the diet of this nocturnal insectivore will be mainly composed of: crickets, moths and flies. The red-eyed tree frog is not an endangered species, but it is facing the disappearance of its natural habitat due to human encroachment. Humans are using their land to be real-estate as overpopulated cities face a need for expansion. The life expectancy of this frog species, in the wilderness, is 5 years old. The red-eyed tree frog has been known to increase its life expectancy, in captivity, up to 10 years of age. Let's be sensitive to the life of the red-eyed tree frog by trying to meet the humans' needs for expansion while protecting the natural habitat of this frog species! My mother nearly killed me never mind her granddaughter! [tape turned off while MOT on phone] Arithmetic Sequences and Finding Missing Terms Have you ever heard of an arithmetic sequence before? It's a special type of list or set of numbers where each number increases (or decreases) by the same amount every time. This constant difference between consecutive terms is called the common difference. For example, consider this arithmetic sequence: -2, 2, 6, 10, ... The common difference here is 4 because each term increases by 4 compared to its previous term. So, let me ask you a question! What would come next in this sequence? That's right; it's 10 + 4, which equals 14! Now, I want you to imagine an exciting mystery involving some secret treasure hidden within an arithmetic sequence. You have been given clues to figure out the value of the missing term (let's call it ${a}_{n}$) in the following arithmetic sequence: * The first term (${a}_{1}$) is -2 * The fourth term (${a}_{4}$) is 16 * And the mysterious magic term (${a}_{n}$) is 11998 Your goal is to discover two important secrets - the common difference ($d$) and the position of the magic term ($n$). Let's put on our detective hats and follow these steps to crack the code! Step 1: Recall the formula for any term in an arithmetic sequence: ${a}_{n}\ =\ a+\left(n-1\right)\times d$ Where: * ${a}_{n}$ represents the target term we want to know * $a$ stands for the first term (-2 in this case) * $n$ means the place or position of the target term * $d$ refers to the common difference Step 2: Find the Common Difference ($d$) using the fourth term clue: Since we already know the first term ($a$) and the fourth term (${a}_{4}$), we can write another equation based on their positions: ${a}_{4}=a+\left(4-1\right)\times d$ Plugging in the values we have: $16=\ -2+\left(4-1\right)\times d$ Solve for $d$, and you will get the enchanting answer: $\boxed{\mathbf{d}\mathbf{=}\mathbf{6}}$ Step 3: Uncover the Secret Position ($n$) using the Magic Term clue: With the newfound knowledge of $d$, return to your original mission-discovering the magical term's location. Plug in all known information into the main formula: ${a}_{n}=\ -2+\left(n-1\right)\times 6$ However, instead of solving for ${a}_{n}$, you need to determine the value of $n$. Use the second clue: $11998=\ -2+\left(n-1\right)\times 6$ After unraveling the math behind this bewitching puzzle, you will reveal the astonishing solution: $\boxed{\mathbf{n}\mathbf{=}\mathbf{2001}}$ Congratulations! By diligently following the steps and decoding the clues, you've successfully discovered the concealed common difference (6) and the mystical position (2001) of the magic term in this cunning arithmetic sequence adventure! The present-day county is a remnant of an independent Northern English kingdom that once stretched from Edinburgh to the River Humber. Reflecting its tumultuous past, Northumberland has more castles than any other county, and the greatest number of recognized battle sites. Once an economically important region that supplied much of the coal that powered the industrial revolution, Northumberland is now a rural county with a small and gradually shrinking population. Title: How to Be Temari from Naruto Step 1: Understanding Temari ------------------------------ Temari is a fictional character from the popular anime and manga series, Naruto. She is known for her exceptional skills as a kunoichi (female ninja) and her mastery over wind-based jutsu. Additionally, she has distinctive blonde hair with red highlights, usually tied up in high pigtails, and wears traditional Japanese clothing called a kimono. To become Temari, you'll need to mimic both her appearance and personality traits. ### Step 2: Dressing Up As Temari To resemble Temari's outfit, wear a pink or red colored kimono with white accents. You can purchase one online or at specialty costume stores. If that isn't feasible, any long pink dress could work too. Pair this with black leggings or stockings and split-toe shoes (also known as zori). Accessorize with gold earrings and bracelets if possible. Remember, accuracy is crucial here! Key Tip: Make sure your kimono fits well so you don't trip while cosplaying. Guideline: Choose comfortable footwear since you might be wearing them for extended periods during conventions or photoshoots. ### Step 3: Styling Your Hair Like Temari Temari's hairstyle consists of two tall pigtails held together by red ribbons. Start by brushing your hair thoroughly. Then divide your hair into two sections vertically down the middle. Tie each section tightly using sturdy hair ties. Next, take thin red ribbons about twice the length of your pigtails and tie them around the base where the hair tie is. Loop the remaining ribbon around the bottom end of the pigtail before tying another knot. Key Tip: Use hairspray to keep flyaways at bay and maintain the hairstyle throughout the day. Guideline: Consider using wigs if bleaching and dying your hair seems daunting or damaging. ### Step 4: Mastering Temari's Personality Temari is often portrayed as strong-willed, independent, and somewhat tsundere (a term used in anime fandom to describe a character who is initially cold, aloof, or even hostile but gradually shows a warmer, friendlier side over time). Practice being assertive yet polite when interacting with others. Show determination and resilience especially under pressure situations. Also, try expressing different emotions like excitement, anger, surprise, etc., just like Temari does in the series. Key Tip: Watch episodes featuring Temari closely to understand her nuances better. Guideline: Try role-playing exercises alone or with friends to get comfortable acting like Temari. ### Step 5: Learning Wind Jutsus While actual wind manipulation may not be achievable outside of fiction, there are ways to simulate these abilities through props and choreography. For instance, you can create paper fans labeled 'wind jutsu' and perform moves accordingly. Study fight scenes involving Temari carefully to emulate her movements accurately. Key Tip: Always prioritize safety when practicing combat routines. Guideline: Using lightweight materials will make executing moves easier and more realistic. Remember, cosplay should always be fun first and foremost. While aiming for authenticity is great, what truly matters is enjoying yourself and sharing your love for the character and their story. Good luck becoming Temari! Over the past years, individuals have been using the terms "autism" and "autism spectrum disorder" like they mean the same thing. Autism is a spectrum disorder. This means that some people who have autism are only mildly affected. These people may go to regular schools, go to work, and have partners and families. Some people are affected worse than others. These people may be able to take care of most of their own needs at home like dressing and getting food, but not be able to have a regular job or travel alone. A person who has severe autism may need to be cared for all their life. Few people with autism are extraordinarily gifted or talented. These people are said to have savant syndrome. They are often very good at just one thing in particular, like mathematics, playing the piano or remembering football scores. What would do you really like to do in gymnastics the very most? [no pause between question and statement and low volume with monotone] Slithering into Sssss Rationale: This lesson will help students identify /s/, the phoneme represented by S. Students will be learning to recognize /s/ in spoken language by learning a representation of the letter (slithering snake spiral movement) and the symbol S. This lesson will provide practice finding /s/ in words and apply the phonemic awareness of /s/ in phonetic cue reading through distinguishing rhyming words from beginning letters. Materials: Primary paper, pencil, tongue tickler on chart (Sally stroked Sue's snake Saturday), crayons, drawing paper, word cards (SOCK, GET, HAT, SLOP, RAKE, SIGHT), Dr. Suess ABC Book, worksheet (attached) 1. Say: "Our written language is a secret code. We have to learn what letters stand for, which can be tricky. The letters stand for the way our mouths move when we say a word. We will work on learning how our mouths move for different letters, and today we will work on spotting the mouth move for /s/. We spell /s/ with the letter S. S looks like a slithering snake moving through the yard, and /s/ sounds like a snake hissing at you. 2. Now, let's pretend we are all snakes, /s/, /s/, /s/. Did you notice where your tongue is? How does your mouth move when you say /s/? When we say /s/, our teeth come together and our tongue hits our teeth at the front of our mouths. 3. Let me show you how to find /s/ in the word tusk. I am going to stretch tusk out in slow motion and listen for my hissing snake. Tt-uu-ss-kk. Slower still: Ttttt-uuu-sssssss-kkkk. There is /s/, I felt my teeth hit the front of my mouth when my teeth came together. I heard the hissing snake /s/ in tusk. I am going to test if cold has /s/ in it: c-o-l-d, no it does not. My tongue never hits my teeth when my mouth closes. 4. Now we are going to try saying a tongue tickler, notice this sentence on the chart. "Sally stroked Sue's snake Saturday." We are going to say it together three times. Now this time, we are going to slow it down and stretch our /s/ at the beginning of each word. "Sssally sssstroked Ssssue'ssss ssssnake Ssssaturday." Try it again but this time separate the /s/ from each word: "/s/ally /s/troked /s/ue's /s/nake /s/aturday." 5. Get out a piece of paper and a pencil for this activity. We use letter S to spell /s/. Capital S looks like a large snake. Let's write the lowercase letter s. Start just below the fence, form a tiny c up in the air and then swing back to touch the sidewalk. I want to see everyone's slithering snake, crawling in between the sidewalk and fence. After I draw a smiley face on your paper, then you need to make nine more just like it. 6. I will now call on students to answer the following questions including an explanation: Do you hear /s/ in rock or sun? Win or sent? Tough or stay? Stuck or gill? Now let's see if you can spot the mouth move /s/ in some words. Slither like a snake if you hear /s/: The slimy, slippery lizard scurried across the sidewalk into the sunroom. 7. Now let's look in the alphabet book. Dr. Seuss tells us about a funny creature who actually has a name that starts with /s/. Can you guess it's name? Are there other words you can come up with that start with /s/. Now you get to become an illustrator and create your own creature like Dr. Seuss did. Your creature has to have a name that starts with /s/. After you are finished creating a name and drawing, we'll display them in the classroom. 8. Now we will use these index cards to decide if the word is this or that. For example, GET, if it get or set: the S tells me to slither like a snake. Now it's your turn: SOCK: is it sock or lock? HAT: is it hat or sat? SLOP: it is plop or slop? RAKE: is it rake or sake? SIGHT: is it sight or flight? (this is an individual, one-on-one activity for the teacher to implement as students complete step 9). 9. Assessment: Distribute the attached worksheet. Students are to complete the spellings and then color in the pictures that start with S. Today, we're going to talk about a fun problem involving numbers and functions. Let's dive into it! Imagine you have a magical box called "H." This box takes any rule that changes one number into another (we call these rules "functions") and gives you a new number. So if you give it the rule "add two," it might turn it into the number 7. Or if you give it the rule "multiply by three," it could become 72. Now here's where things get interesting. The person who showed you this magical box challenges you with something seemingly impossible. They say that even though the box changes each rule into a unique number, you can find two different rules that will make the box produce the same number! In other words, they want you to come up with two ways of changing numbers that result in the same final number when put through the box. Here's the catch - your answer has to include not just the two special rules but also a specific starting number to show that their trick doesn't work. You need to prove them wrong using math. Sounds exciting? Great! Let's see how we can do this together. First, let me introduce some basic concepts. Think of all those whole numbers - 0, 1, 2, 3, etc., which we call natural numbers. Now imagine having a bunch of boxes, each containing a different rule on how to change natural numbers. There are infinitely many such rules and thus infinitely many boxes. Our challenge now becomes finding two distinct boxes (rules), which, when applied, create the exact same output for at least one input number. We need to expose the magic behind the original magical box (H) by showing its limitations. Ready? Let's go! To start, think about making our own version of the magical box H. What would happen if we tried turning numbers back into rules instead of transforming rules into numbers? It seems strange, but bear with me. Our new "inverse box" would take a number and return a rule that matches it. However, remember that there are far more rules than numbers; therefore, most of the time, multiple rules will lead us to the same number inside H. Let's consider an example. Imagine our magical box H turned the rule "double the number" into the number 64. Then, suppose our inverse box created both the "double the number" rule and the "subtract 16 from the number" rule because these generate the same number 64 within the magical box H. We found two different rules that produce the same output after being transformed by the magical box H. And we did so by creating our very own inverse box! To recap, we proved that no matter how clever or mysterious someone makes their magical box seem, there will always be cases where different rules yield the same outcome. Math helps uncover hidden secrets everywhere! Should I throw it or roll it? Today we are going to talk about something called "end behavior" of a special type of function called a "polynomial." You may have seen these before - they are just equations that involve adding, subtracting, multiplying, and dividing numbers and variables together, like this one: f(x) = 2x^3 + 5x^2 - 7x + 9. When we talk about the end behavior of a polynomial, we mean what happens to the graph of the equation when we plug in really big positive or negative numbers for x. Does it shoot up towards the sky, or dive down into the ground? Or maybe it levels off and doesn't do too much. The end behavior of a polynomial is mostly determined by two things: its degree and its leading coefficient. The degree of a polynomial is just the highest power of x that shows up in the equation. So for our example above, the degree is 3 because the term with the highest power of x is 2x^3. Now, here comes the important part: the end behavior of polynomials with ODD degrees (like ours!) goes in OPPOSITE DIRECTIONS on either side of the y-axis. That means that if we keep increasing the value of x, eventually the graph will start heading downwards on the left side, while simultaneously soaring upwards on the right side. Let me give you another example using a different polynomial: g(x) = -4x^5 + 3x^3 - 2x + 1. In this case, the degree is 5, making it an odd number again. And sure enough, if we draw out the graph, we see that it dives deep down on the left side and rises very high on the right side. So next time you encounter a polynomial, try thinking about its end behavior based on its degree. Is it even or odd? Remember, if it's odd, then its ends point in opposite directions! Title: How to Hold a Door Open With a Coin Introduction: Holding a door open with a coin is an easy and convenient way to keep doors from closing when you need them to stay open temporarily. This technique can be used in various situations such as moving large items through narrow spaces or assisting individuals who have their hands full. By following this simple guide, you will learn how to hold a door open using a coin effectively and efficiently. Things You'll Need: - A suitable coin (preferably larger than a penny) - A door that latches properly but has some space between the door frame and the latch Step 1: Choose Your Coin Select a coin that fits comfortably within the gap between the door and its frame. Ideally, it should be slightly thicker than a penny so that it remains stable while holding the door open. Common coins include dimes, quarters, or fifty cent pieces depending on your location. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Make sure the chosen coin isn't too small, making it difficult to maneuver and maintain balance. - Avoid using valuable or collectible coins as they might get scratched or damaged during usage. Step 2: Position The Coin Correctly Place the coin horizontally into the gap above the latch plate where the door comes into contact with the frame. Align the edge of the coin along the bottom part of the opening, ensuring maximum surface area contact with both sides of the gap. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be gentle when inserting the coin; applying excessive force could potentially damage either the coin or the door frame. - If there's no space between the door and frame near the latch, try looking higher up or lower down for alternative spots. Step 3: Secure The Door With The Coin Push the door towards the direction you want it to remain open until the latch catches onto the coin. Apply slight pressure on the door to ensure proper alignment and secure placement of the coin. Once done correctly, the door should now stay open without any additional support. Key Tips & Guidelines: - To prevent injury or property damage, make sure the door is stabilized before leaving it unattended. - Always double-check if the door is held firmly by the coin before walking away. Step 4: Retrieve The Coin When Finished When you are ready to close the door, gently pull the door shut while carefully removing the coin from the gap. It's crucial to maintain control over the motion of the door to avoid damaging the coin or causing harm to yourself or others nearby. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Never attempt to remove the coin with your bare hand while the door is still open, as doing so may result in pinching or other injuries. - Dispose of or store the coin appropriately after usage to prevent loss or misplacement. Conclusion: By mastering the art of holding a door open with a coin, you can save time and effort in numerous situations. Remember always to choose an appropriate coin, position it accurately, secure the door firmly, and retrieve it safely once finished. Follow these steps consistently, and soon enough, this handy skill will become second nature. This tutorial will guide you through the process with clear, detailed steps. **Things You'll Need:** 1. An Action Replay device for Nintendo DS 2. A compatible microSD card (with adapter if necessary) 3. A computer with internet access 4. The Action Replays code manager software (available from the official website) **Step 1: Download and Install Code Manager Software** First, visit the Action Replay website and navigate to the downloads section. Locate the latest version of the code manager software for Nintendo DS and download it to your computer. Run the installer and follow the prompts to complete the installation. *Key Tip:* Keep your code manager software updated to ensure compatibility with new games and system updates. **Step 2: Prepare Your MicroSD Card** Insert the microSD card into your computer using a suitable adapter. Format the card within the Action Replay code manager software when prompted during setup. Make sure that you back up any important data before formatting as this process erases all existing content on the card. *Guideline:* Always format the microSD card within the code manager software instead of relying on your operating system's built-in formatter. **Step 3: Connect Your Action Replay Device** Connect your Action Replay device to your computer via USB cable. Launch the Action Replay code manager software. Click on the 'Device' tab at the top of the window and select 'Connect'. Wait for the software to recognize your connected device. If it fails to connect initially, try disconnecting and reconnecting the device. *Tip:* Ensure that no other applications are currently utilizing the USB port while attempting to establish a connection between the code manager software and the Action Replay device. **Step 4: Access Available Cheat Codes** Navigate to the 'Cheats' tab inside the code manager software. Here, you can search for available codes based on the title of the game you want to modify. Select your specific game from the list provided. Browse through the various categories like 'Max Stats', 'Infinite Items', etc., to find relevant codes. Tick the checkbox next to each desirable code to enable them. *Key Guideline:* Only activate tested and trusted codes to avoid potential issues such as crashes or corrupted save files. **Step 5: Transfer Enabled Codes to Your Action Replay Device** Once you have selected all preferred codes, click on the 'Download' button at the bottom right corner of the screen. Choose the option 'To ARDS/DSTT' depending upon which type of slot does your Action Replay utilize. Confirm the transfer action when prompted. *Note:* Be patient as the transfer may take some time depending on the number of activated codes. **Step 6: Disconnect and Test Your Action Replay** Disconnect your Action Replay device safely from your computer once the transfer completes successfully. Insert the microSD card back into the Action Replay cartridge. Power on your Nintendo DS console and load the previously chosen game. Mommy do I know how to do it? - Equation SolverFactoring CalculatorDerivative Calculator Point Slope Form Point-slope refers to a method for graphing a linear equation on an x-y axis. When graphing a linear equation, the whole idea is to take pairs of x's and y's and plot them on the graph. While you could plot several points by just plugging in values of x, the point-slope form makes the whole process simpler. Point-slope form is also used to take a graph and find the equation of that particular line. The point slope form gets its name because it uses a single point on the graph and the slope of the line. Think about it this way: You have a starting point on a map, and you are given a direction to head. You have all the information you need to draw a single line on the map. The standard point-slope equation looks like this: It should be noted that "y1" does not mean y multipled by 1. In this case it simply denotes a particular y value which you will plug into the equation. The variable m is the slope of the line. You are given the point (4,3) and a slope of 2. Find the equation for this line in point slope form. Just plug the given values into your point-slope formula above. Your point (4,3) is in the form of (x1,y1). That means where you see y1, use 3. Where you see x1, use 4. Your slope was given to you, so where you see m, use 2. Pretty simple, huh? Your final result should look like: Your point is (-1,5). The slope is 1/2. Create the equation that describes this line in point-slope form. Try working it out on your own. The answer is: . If that's not what you got, re-read the lesson and try again. Point-slope form is all about having a single point and a direction (slope) and converting that between an algebraic equation and a graph. In the example above, we took a given set of point and slope and made an equation. Now let's take an equation and find out the point and slope so we can graph it. Find the equation (in point-slope form) for the line shown in this graph: To write the equation, we need two things: a point, and a slope. It is simple to find a point because we just need ANY point on the line. The point I've indicated, (-1,0), just happens to be the easiest one to find. Note also that it is useful to pick a point on the axis, because one of the values will be zero. Finding the slope requires a little calculation, but it is also pretty easy. Just count the number of lines on the graph paper going in each direction of a triangle, like I've shown. Remember that slope is rise over run, or y/x. Therefore the slope of this line is 2. You could have used any triangle to figure out the slope and you would still get the same answer. Putting it all together, our point is (-1,0) and our slope is 2. We know how to use the point-slope form, so the final answer is: As you can see, point-slope form is nothing too complicated. It is just one method to writing an equation for a line. The other common way of doing this is the y=mx+b method. Just practice converting between a line on a graph and an equation and you'll get the hang of it in no time. And of course, if you need more help, feel free to ask the volunteers on our math help message board. On Wednesday, he ate through three plums, but he was still hungry. Title: How to Cook American Bacon Introduction: Bacon is a popular breakfast food enjoyed by many people around the world. This article will focus on cooking American-style bacon, which is typically streaky with alternating layers of fat and meat. Follow these steps for crispy, flavorful bacon that's perfect for sandwiches, salads, or as a standalone dish. **Time:** 15 minutes (cooking time may vary) **Equipment Needed:** * Non-stick frying pan or griddle * Tongs or fork * Paper towels * Plate * Optional: splatter screen Ingredients: * Thick cut American bacon slices * A small amount of oil or butter (if using a non-stick pan) Step 1: Prepare Your Pan Preheat your non-stick frying pan over medium heat. If you are concerned about grease splattering, place an optional splatter screen over the pan during this process. Add a little bit of oil or melt some butter if needed; otherwise, proceed to Step 2 since most preseasoned non-stick pans do not require additional fat. Tips & Guidelines: Properly heating up your pan ensures even cooking and prevents sticking. However, too high heat can lead to burning while low heat won't render enough fat from the bacon. Medium heat provides the best balance between browning and rendering. Step 2: Place Bacon Strips in the Pan Lay out individual strips of bacon flat in the pan without overlapping them. Giving each slice space allows for uniform cooking and adequate fat rendition. Step 3: Monitor Initial Sizzling Stage As soon as you put the bacon into the pan, listen carefully for sizzling sounds. The sound indicates that the fat is beginning to render out. Watch closely during this stage because once the sizzle slows down, it means the bacon has started releasing its maximum amount of fat. At this point, flip the bacon pieces over. Key Point: Timely flipping guarantees both sides receive equal attention leading to perfectly cooked bacon. Step 4: Flip Regularly but Not Excessively Flip the bacon every few minutes after initial placement. Each side should be facing downward at least twice before reaching desired doneness. Over-flipping might cause uneven cooking due to constant exposure to varying temperatures. Step 5: Check Doneness Level Check regularly for preferred level of crispiness. Depending on personal preference, cooking times may differ. For chewy bacon, cook until the edges start curling up slightly but still have flexibility when pressed. For extra crispy bacon, continue cooking till they turn golden brown and become brittle. Remember: Undercooked bacon poses health risks whereas overcooked ones lose their juiciness. So keep checking intermittently. Step 6: Transfer Cooked Bacon to a Plate Lined with Paper Towels Once done, remove the bacon slices gently using tongs or a fork onto a plate lined with paper towels. This absorbs excess grease ensuring cleaner handling and less messy consumption. Step 7: Serve Immediately Serve immediately while warm. Pair with eggs, toast, pancakes, waffles, or any other favorite breakfast items. Enjoy! Conclusion: By following these simple yet effective steps, anyone can prepare delicious American bacon right at home. With practice, you'll master the art of achieving consistent results each time. Happy cooking! Why did he get a stomach ache Chi? That's the handle to turn the film. You want me to hold this one and you squeeze it? Introduction to Vectors and Scalars Distance, displacement, speed and velocity. Difference between vectors and scalars Introduction to Vectors and Scalars Use this menu to view and help create subtitles for this video in many different languages. You'll probably want to hide YouTube's captions if using these subtitles. - What I want to do in this video is talk about the difference - between vectors and scalars. - And they might sound like very complicated ideas - but we'll see over the course of the videos - that they're actually very simple ideas. - So first I'll give you a little bit of a definition - and then I'll give you a bunch of examples - and I think the examples will make things super clear. - Hopefully they'll make things super clear. - A vector is something that has a magnitude - - or you can kind of view that as a size - - and it has a direction. - So, and it has a direction. And it has a direction. - A scalar only has a magnitude or size. - And if that if that doesn't make sense to you, - it will hopefully make sense to you in a second when I show you an example. - For example: let's say that I have a ... - Let's say, that's the ground right there - - Let me do the ground in a more appropriate ground-like color. - So, this is green right over here... - And let's say that I pick and I have a brick here - I have a brick on the ground. - And I pick up that brick, - and I move it over to this place right over here. - So I move the brick right over there - and then I take a ruler out and I say, - "Wow, I have moved the brick five meters." - So my question to you: is my measurement of five meters, - is it a vector or a scalar? - Well if I just tell you five meters, - you just know the size of the movement. - You just know the magnitude of the movement. - So if someone were to just say "five meters", - this is a scalar quantity. - When we're referring to moving something - or how much something has, I guess, changed its position - and I don't give you the direction, we're talking about distance. - and I'm assuming you've heard the word distance: - how far of a distance has something traveled? - So this is distance. - So we can say that this block or this brick, - because of my picking it up and moving it, - has moved a distance of five meters. - But if I didn't show you this picture here - and someone just told you that it moved a distance of five meters, - you wouldn't know if it moved to the right five meters, - you wouldn't know if it moved to the left five meters, - or if it moved up or down or in or out - - You don't know what direction it moved five meters. - You just know it moved five meters. - If you want to specify that, - we could say this brick right over here - that it moved five meters to the left. - Now we have specified a magnitude - right over there, - so that is a magnitude - - and we have specified a direction: "to the left". - So you now explicitly know that it went five meters to the- - oh, sorry, it should be five meters to the right. Let me change that. - So five meters to the right is what it got moved to. - It started here, it went five meters to the right. - So once again: - the magnitude is five meters, - and the direction is to the right. - So what I've just described to you right here is a vector quantity. - So all of this business over here - this is a vector. - And when you talk about the movement, the change in position, - and you give its direction - the vector version of distance, - I guess you could call it - is displacement. - So, this right here is displacement. - So the correct thing to say: - you would say that this brick has been displaced 5 meters to the right, - or it has been moved a distance of 5 meters. - Distance is a scalar quantity. - I didn't tell you what direction we moved it in. - Displacement is a vector quantity. - We told you that it is to the right. - Now let's explore this. - If we talk about the actual speed or velocity of something, - so let's say that this 5 meters was traveled, - and let's say that the change in time. - (let me, just... because you're probably not familiar with what that means) - Let's say that the change in time right here - change in time - when I moved this block five meters. - Let's say that the change in time was 2 seconds. - So maybe right when the block started moving, - maybe on my stopwatch it said zero, - and then on my stopwatch when it stopped moving, it said - - or when it got to this position, I should say - - When it left from this position my stopwatch said zero. - When it got to this position my stopwatch said 2 seconds. - So the change in time or the duration we're dealing with is 2 seconds. - For all we know, time only goes in the positive direction. - So you can pick that as a vector or a scalar quantity I guess - because there's only one direction for time as far as we know - or at least in what we're going to deal with for the simple physics. - So what's a measure of how fast this thing moved? - How fast did this thing move? - So we could say it moved five meters in two seconds. - Let me write this down. - So it moved 5 meters per 2 seconds. - Or, we could write this as 5/2 of a meter per second - or five divided by two is 2.5 meters per second. - This right here is just the 5 divided by 2. - (let me make that clear) - That right there is just the 5 divided by the 2. - So my question to you: this 2.5 meters per second, - tells you how far it travelled in a certain amount of time - - is this a vector or a scalar quantity? - It is telling you how fast it went, - but is it giving you just a size of how fast it went? - Or is it also giving you direction? - Well I don't see any direction here. - So this is a scalar quantity. - And the scalar quantity for how fast something is going is speed. - So we could say that the speed of the brick is 2.5 meters per second. - Now if we do the same calculation, if we say it went 5 meters - - I'll just write "m" for meters - - 5 meters to the right in 2 seconds. - Then, what do we get? - We get 2.5 meters per second - - I'll just abbreviate them - - as meters per second to the right. - So is this a vector or a scalar quantity? - I'm telling you the magnitude of the speed that's right here. - This is the magnitude: 2.5 meters per second - - and I'm also telling you the direction: to the right. - So this is a vector quantity. - And when you specify both the speed and the direction - - so the 2.5 meters is a scalar and the direction - - you are talking about velocity. - So, easy way to think about it: - If you're thinking about change in position - and you specify the direction of the change in position, - you're talking about displacement. - If you're not talking about the direction, - you want the scalar version, you're talking about distance. - If you're talking about how fast something is going - and you give the direction that it's going in, - you're talking about velocity. - If you don't give the direction, you are talking about speed. - Hopefully that helps you a little bit - - and in the next video we're going to start working with these a little bit - and start solving some basic questions - about how fast something is going or how far it might travel - or how long it might take it to get someplace. Do you want to put it back in the mitten? |HartRAO Home||Eclipse Home||Eclipse Next||Eclipse Previous| The diagram above shows what happens during an eclipse of the Sun. Diagram not to scale. As the Moon orbits around the Earth, the Moon crosses the direction from the Earth to the Sun once every 29 days 12 hours and 44 minutes. We call this moment New Moon. The Moon actually becomes visible again a day or two after New Moon, as a thin crescent in the evening sky. The plane of the orbit of the Moon around the Earth is tilted at 5 degrees to the plane of the Earth's orbit around the Sun. Hence at the time of New Moon, the Moon usually passes above or below the Sun, as seen from Earth, and there is no eclipse. The picture above shows how the Earth (blue) orbits the Sun (yellow), while the orbit of the Moon (grey) around the Earth is tilted (diagram not to scale). There are two places in the Moon's orbit where it can pass exactly between the Sun and the Earth. These are called the nodes of the orbit. If the Moon is at or near either of these nodes at New Moon, an eclipse of the Sun occurs. There is an interval of 31 days that occurs every 5.8 months, when the alignment to give us eclipses can happen. As the sizes of the Moon and Sun as seen from the Earth are almost the same, the Moon can completely block the light of the Sun over a small part of the Earth. In the same way, you can block out a distant building by holding your hand in front of it, even though the building is much bigger than your hand. The Moon passing in front of the Sun produces a shadow that moves from west to east over the Earth. Where the sunlight is completely blocked, in the "umbra", a total eclipse is seen. Where the sunlight is only partly blocked by the Moon, in the "penumbra", a partial eclipse is seen. Note that the diagram above is not to scale! The Sun is really 110 times bigger than the Earth, and very much further away than as it is shown in the diagram above. To get an idea of the sizes and distances of the Moon and the Sun, we can make a simple scale model of the Earth, Moon and Sun. Let's start by using your head as a scale model of the Sun. Let's say the diameter of your head is 14 centimetres. The Earth would then be 15 metres away, or about 20 paces. The model Earth is 1.2 millimetres in diameter - the size of the head of a pin. The Moon would be 4 centimetres, or about the width of two or three fingers, from the Earth. The Moon would be a tiny 0.4 millimetres in diameter, or about the thickness of the shaft of a pin. The real sizes and distances are 10 000 000 000 times bigger than our model. Here are three versions of this scale model: The picture below shows the relative sizes of the Sun, Earth and Moon. The Moon and Earth are shown above to the same scale as the Sun. The distance between the Moon and the Earth is shown to the same scale as the sizes of all three bodies. However, the Moon and Earth should be much further from the Sun - in fact 108 Sun diameters from it. The image of the Sun shown above is a real photograph - the dark patches are less hot regions on the surface, called sunspots. Your scale model should show you that the Sun is 400 times bigger than the Moon and that it is also 400 times further from us than the Moon. This coincidence is what allows us to experience total eclipses of the Sun - as a result the Sun and the Moon both appear to us on Earth to be the same size in the sky. Timmy loved music and would always listen to it on his little radio. One day, Timmy's mom told him to clean his room. Timmy was very clumsy and knocked over his radio while cleaning. It broke into a million pieces and Timmy was very sad. Timmy's mom asked him what happened and Timmy told her that he was trying to clean his room but he was too clumsy and broke his radio. His mom tried to fix it but it was too broken. Timmy was very upset because he loved listening to music on his radio. From that day on, Timmy learned to be more careful and not be so clumsy. He also learned that sometimes accidents happen and it's important to be careful with the things you love. Although Timmy was sad about his broken radio, he knew that he would always have music in his heart. Imagine you have a piggy bank where you save money by putting in some coins every day. The amount of money you put in each day depends on the temperature outside. On sunny days, you put in fewer coins because you spend more time playing outside and less time earning money. On rainy days, you put in more coins because you stay indoors and earn more money. Now let's say we want to predict how much money you will have in your piggy bank after a certain number of days. We know that the total amount of money you have is equal to the sum of all the coins you put in, so we can write this as: Total Money = Coins added on Sunny Days + Coins added on Rainy Days We can represent the amount of money you add on sunny days using the variable S (for Sun) and the amount of money you add on rainy days using the variable R (for Rain). So our equation becomes: Total Money = S + R In mathematics, this type of problem is called a "stochastic process," which means it involves random variables like S and R that change over time. In this case, the temperature (sunny or rainy) determines the value of S and R. Now, imagine that someone tells you that the Total Money is a "martingale." A martingale is a special kind of stochastic process where the expected value of the future Total Money is always equal to the current Total Money. This means that no matter how many sunny or rainy days there are in the future, the best prediction you can make for the Total Money is simply the amount you have today! So here's the question: Can the values of S and R both be nonzero if Total Money is a martingale? Intuitively, the answer is no. If R is positive (meaning you add more coins on rainy days), then the Total Money should increase over time, contradicting the definition of a martingale. Similarly, if S is negative (meaning you lose coins on sunny days), then the Total Money should decrease over time, again contradicting the definition of a martingale. Therefore, the most likely answer is that S must be zero, meaning you never add or remove coins based on the temperature outside. Of course, this explanation is very simplified compared to the original math problem, but hopefully, it gives you a sense of why mathematicians study these types of problems and how they relate to real-world situations!
